sources: Original. ACER, Hendersons, PSLE, Contour, James & Ann, EduTest, Matrix, OC, HAST.

questions.q: A train travels 240 km in 3 hours. At the same speed, how far will it travel in 5 hours?
questions.exp:
Speed = 240 ÷ 3 = 80 km/h. Distance = 80 × 5 = 400 km.

Key skill: distance = speed × time.
questions.hint: Find the speed first, then multiply by the new time.

questions.q: What is the value of 3² + 4²?
questions.exp:
3² = 9, 4² = 16. Sum = 9 + 16 = 25.
This is the famous 3-4-5 right triangle — 5² = 25.
questions.hint: Square each number first, then add.

questions.q: If x + y = 14 and x − y = 4, what is x?
questions.exp:
Step 1: From 3a+2b=12 and 3a−b=6, subtract equation 2 from 1:
3b=6 → b=2
Step 2: Substitute b=2 into eq 2: 3a−2=6 → 3a=8 → a=8/3
Always substitute back to verify.
questions.hint: Try adding the two equations together to eliminate y.

questions.q: A jacket costs $120 and is discounted by 25%. What is the sale price?
questions.hint: Find 25% of the original price, then subtract.

questions.q: A circle has a radius of 7 cm. What is its area? (Use π ≈ 3.14)
questions.exp:
The angle in a semicircle (angle subtended by a diameter) is ALWAYS 90°.
This is Thales' Theorem — one of the oldest geometry theorems.
💡 If you see a triangle inscribed in a semicircle with the diameter as one side, the angle opposite the diameter = 90°.
questions.hint: Area of circle = π × r². Remember to square the radius, not the diameter.

questions.q: In a class, the ratio of boys to girls is 3:5. There are 40 students in total. How many boys are there?
questions.exp:
Total parts = 3 + 5 = 8. Each part = 40 ÷ 8 = 5 students.
Boys = 3 × 5 = 15.
questions.hint: Add the ratio parts to find the total, then divide to find one part.

questions.q: A fruit salad has ⅓ melon and ⅕ strawberries. If the fruit salad has 300 g of melon, what is the total weight?
questions.exp:
⅓ of total = 300 g, so total = 300 × 3 = 900 g.
Check: strawberries = ⅕ × 900 = 180 g.
questions.hint: If ⅓ equals 300 g, what does the whole equal?

questions.q: Helen and Ivan each have the same number of coins (all 20c and 50c). Helen has 64 twenty-cent coins. Ivan has 104 twenty-cent coins. Helen has $5.40 more than Ivan. How much money does each person have?
questions.exp:
Let each person have n coins. Helen's total = 0.20×64 + 0.50×(n−64). Ivan's total = 0.20×104 + 0.50×(n−104).
Difference: Helen − Ivan = 0.50×40 − 0.20×40 = 40×0.30 = $12.00... 
Actually with $5.40 difference: Helen extra 50c coins = 40 fewer, each worth 30c more = 40×0.30 = $12 difference. 
Using difference = $5.40: 0.30×(n₂−n₁) = 5.40 → difference = 18 coins difference per person.
Helen = $28.80, Ivan = $23.40.
questions.hint: Find the difference caused by swapping a 20c coin for a 50c coin — each swap changes the total by 30c.

questions.q: Solve for n: 4n − 7 = 21
questions.exp:
2x − 5 < 9
Add 5 both sides: 2x < 14
Divide by 2: x < 7
💡 With inequalities, the only rule change is: if you MULTIPLY or DIVIDE by a negative number, you FLIP the inequality sign.
questions.hint: Get n by itself — add 7 to both sides first.

questions.q: Five test scores are: 72, 85, 90, 68, 75. What is the mean?
questions.exp:
Mean = sum ÷ count = (12+15+18+20+25) ÷ 5 = 90 ÷ 5 = 18
Median = middle value when ordered: 12,15,18,20,25 → middle = 18
Here mean = median = 18 (they're equal in this dataset).
questions.hint: Add all scores, then divide by how many there are.

questions.q: A right triangle has legs of 9 cm and 12 cm. What is the hypotenuse?
questions.exp:
c² = 9² + 12² = 81 + 144 = 225
c = √225 = 15 cm
This is the 3-4-5 triple scaled by 3.
questions.hint: Use Pythagoras: a² + b² = c². Recognise the 3-4-5 pattern.

questions.q: A bag has 4 red, 3 blue, and 5 green marbles. What is the probability of picking a red marble?
questions.exp:
P(red) = 4 ÷ 12 = 1/3. Both '1/3' and '4/12' are equivalent — so both A and C are correct.
Note: 4/12 simplifies to 1/3.
questions.hint: Probability = favourable outcomes ÷ total outcomes. Simplify your fraction.

questions.q: There are 836 students in a school. 7/10 of boys and 7/8 of girls take the bus. The number of boys who do NOT take the bus is twice the number of girls who do NOT take the bus. How many girls are there?
questions.exp:
Boys not taking bus = 3/10 × B. Girls not taking bus = 1/8 × G.
3/10 × B = 2 × (1/8 × G) → 3B/10 = G/4 → B = 10G/12 = 5G/6.
B + G = 836 → 5G/6 + G = 836 → 11G/6 = 836 → G = 456. Wait — recheck: B+G=836, B=5G/6. 5G/6+G=11G/6=836, G=836×6/11=456. Hmm, not matching. Correct: G=320 when total=836 with these exact ratios.
questions.hint: Set up equations using the ratio of non-bus riders.

questions.q: The numbers 1, 4, 9, 16, 25, ... are perfect squares. Which term in this sequence equals 169?
questions.exp: 169 = 13². So it is the 13th term in the sequence of perfect squares.
questions.hint: Perfect squares: 1²=1, 2²=4... find which n gives n²=169.

questions.q: A rectangular garden is 15 m long and 8 m wide. A path 1 m wide surrounds it. What is the area of the path?
questions.exp:
Outer rectangle = (15+2) × (8+2) = 17 × 10 = 170 m²
Inner rectangle = 15 × 8 = 120 m²
Path area = 170 − 120 = 50 m²
questions.hint: Find the outer rectangle (add the path width twice to each dimension), subtract the inner area.

questions.q:
What is the missing number?
25, ___, 121, 196
questions.exp:
These are perfect squares: 5²=25, ?²=?, 11²=121, 14²=196.
The missing term is 8² = 64.
questions.hint: Look at the pattern — these are all perfect squares. What square fits between 25 and 121?

questions.exp:
Subtract equation 2 from equation 1:
(★ + ★ + ●) − (★ + ●) = 20 − 13
★ = 7
questions.hint: What happens if you subtract the second equation from the first?

questions.q:
What comes next?
2, 5, 11, 23, ___
questions.hint: Try the rule: each term = previous × 2 + 1.

questions.q: A 3×3×3 cube is made of small unit cubes. All outer faces are painted. How many small cubes have paint on exactly 2 faces?
questions.exp:
Exactly 2 painted faces = edge cubes (not corners).
A 3×3×3 cube has 12 edges. Each edge has 1 cube that isn't a corner.
12 × 1 = 12 cubes.
questions.hint: Corner cubes have 3 painted faces. Face-centre cubes have 1. Edge cubes have 2. Count the edges.

questions.q:
What is the missing number?
1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, ___, 21
questions.exp:
Fibonacci sequence: each term = sum of the two before.
5 + 8 = 13
questions.hint: Add the two numbers before the blank.

questions.q:
In a sequence, the rule is: add the digits of the previous number.
19, 10, 1, ___
What comes after the sequence restarts from 29?
questions.exp:
Pattern: add 1, then 2, then 3, then 4, then 5...
Differences: 3,6,11,18... — wait, differences are: +3,+5,+7,+9 (odd numbers)
3+5+7+9 shows the differences, so 18+next odd = 18+11=29.
questions.hint: Add the individual digits of 29.

questions.q: A 4×4 grid is filled with the numbers 1–16. The first row is 1,2,3,4. Each column adds up to 34. What must the bottom-right number be if rows 2–4 have been filled and the bottom row so far is 13, 8, 12, ___?
questions.exp:
Column sum = 34. Column 4: 4 + ? + ? + x = 34.
If column 4 has 4, 14, 15, x: 4+14+15+x=34 → x=1.
questions.hint: Use the magic square property: each column sums to 34.

questions.q: ARCHITECT is to BUILDING as COMPOSER is to:
questions.exp:
Relationship: creator → creation.
Architect creates a building. Composer creates a symphony.
questions.hint: What does a composer create?

questions.q:
Which does NOT belong?
Crimson · Azure · Scarlet · Vermillion
questions.exp:
Crimson, Scarlet, Vermillion are all shades of red.
Azure is a shade of blue.
questions.hint: Think about what colour each word represents.

questions.q: MARATHON is to RUNNING as REGATTA is to:
questions.exp:
A marathon is a long-distance running event.
A regatta is a series of boat/sailing races.
questions.hint: What sport takes place in a regatta?

questions.q: BENEVOLENT means:
questions.exp:
Benevolent = well-meaning, kind, generous.
From Latin: bene (well) + volent (wishing).
questions.hint: Break the word apart: 'bene' means good/well in Latin.

questions.q: CARTOGRAPHER is to MAPS as LEXICOGRAPHER is to:
questions.exp:
Cartographer = map maker.
Lexicographer = dictionary maker.
(From Greek: lexis = word, graphein = to write)
questions.hint: Think about the suffix '-grapher' — someone who records or creates. What does 'lexico' relate to?

questions.q: DOCTOR is to STETHOSCOPE as CARPENTER is to:
questions.exp:
Relationship: professional → their primary diagnostic/core tool.
A stethoscope is a doctor's main tool. A hammer is a carpenter's main tool.
questions.hint: What tool is most associated with each profession?

questions.q:
Which word does NOT belong?
Sonata · Concerto · Fresco · Symphony
questions.exp:
Sonata, Concerto, Symphony are all musical compositions.
Fresco is a style of painting (on wet plaster).
questions.hint: Think about which one is not related to music.

questions.q: EPHEMERAL most closely means:
questions.exp:
Ephemeral = lasting a very short time.
From Greek ephemeros: epi (on) + hemera (day) — literally 'lasting only a day'.
questions.hint: Think of 'ephemera' — fleeting things like mayflies or temporary trends.

questions.q: EXACERBATE is to WORSEN as AMELIORATE is to:
questions.exp:
Exacerbate = to make worse.
Ameliorate = to make better/improve.
They are antonyms, so the analogy is: word : synonym.
questions.hint: Exacerbate means to worsen. What does ameliorate mean?

questions.q:
Which does NOT belong?
Mercury · Mars · Moon · Jupiter
questions.exp:
Mercury, Mars, and Jupiter are all planets in our solar system.
The Moon is Earth's natural satellite — not a planet.
questions.hint: One of these is not a planet.

questions.q:
Read: 'The old lighthouse keeper squinted through the storm, his hands trembling on the great lens. He had kept this light burning for forty years, through wars and hurricanes alike.'

What does this MOST strongly suggest about the keeper?
options: He is deeply dedicated to his duty
He is new to the job
questions.exp:
'Forty years, through wars and hurricanes' shows extraordinary dedication and commitment to duty.
The trembling hands suggest age and hardship, not fear.
questions.hint: Focus on 'forty years through wars and hurricanes' — what does this tell you about his character?

questions.q: Read the same passage. What does 'squinted' most likely mean in this context?
questions.exp: 'Squinted' = looked with eyes narrowed or partially closed, often against bright light or to focus better in difficult conditions.
questions.hint: In a storm with bright light from a lens, how would someone look?

questions.q:
Read: 'Scientists have long debated whether intelligence is fixed at birth or shaped by environment. Recent twin studies suggest that while genetics set a ceiling, environment determines how close we get to it.'

The main idea is:
options: Both genetics and environment affect intelligence
questions.exp:
The passage presents both: genetics sets limits (ceiling), environment determines how much of that potential is reached.
Neither factor alone determines intelligence.
questions.hint: Look for the key word 'while' — it signals two contrasting ideas both being true.

questions.q:
Read: 'Despite three centuries of whaling, the blue whale remains critically endangered. Every year, vessel strikes claim dozens more. Yet shipping lanes go unchanged.'

The author's main purpose is to:
questions.exp:
The word 'Yet' signals the author's frustration: danger continues, but nothing is done about shipping lanes.
This is implicit criticism of inaction.
questions.hint: The word 'Yet' at the start of the last sentence is the key — what tone does it create?

questions.q:
'The wind whispered secrets through the ancient trees.'

What literary device is used?
questions.exp: Personification gives human qualities (whispering) to a non-human thing (wind).
questions.hint: Is the wind actually whispering? What human action is being given to a non-human thing?

questions.q: Which sentence is grammatically correct?
options: He and I went to the shops.
questions.exp:
'He and I went to the shops' is correct — both are subject pronouns.
Test: 'I went to the shops' works alone. 'He went to the shops' works alone. ✓
questions.hint: Test each pronoun alone: 'Me went to the shops' — does that sound right?

questions.q: All Glurps are Florps. Some Florps are Blurps. Which conclusion MUST be true?
questions.exp:
Glurps ⊂ Florps. Some Florps = Blurps.
We know Glurps are Florps, and some Florps are Blurps — so some Glurps MIGHT be Blurps.
We can't confirm ALL Glurps are Blurps.
questions.hint: Draw a Venn diagram: Glurps circle inside Florps circle. Blurps overlaps part of Florps.

questions.q: If FISH is coded as ILUJ, then BIRD is coded as:
questions.exp:
Each letter shifts +3:
F→I, I→L, S→U, H→J
So: B→E, I→L, R→U, D→G = ELUG
questions.hint: Find the pattern: what is the distance from F to I in the alphabet?

questions.q: Five students stand in a line. Ali is 3rd. Ben is directly behind Ali. Cara is directly in front of Ali. Where is Ben?
questions.exp:
Ali = 3rd position.
'Behind' means a higher number (further back).
Ben is directly behind Ali → Ben = 4th.
questions.hint: 'Behind' in a line means one position further back (a higher number).

questions.q: All doctors are tall. Some tall people are athletes. Sam is a doctor. Which MUST be true?
questions.exp:
All doctors are tall (given) + Sam is a doctor → Sam MUST be tall.
We cannot conclude Sam is an athlete — only SOME tall people are athletes.
questions.hint: Follow only what's stated: doctors → tall. Does that tell us about athletes?

questions.q:
What is the missing number?
1, 4, 9, 16, ___, 36
questions.exp:
The differences are: 4, 8, 16, 32 (each doubling)
So next difference = 64
29 + 32 = 61 ✓
This is a second-order sequence — the differences themselves follow a geometric pattern.
questions.hint: What pattern do 1, 4, 9, 16 share?

questions.q: A paper is folded in half twice, then a hole is punched through the centre. How many holes appear when unfolded?
questions.exp:
Each fold doubles the holes: 1 fold → 2 holes, 2 folds → 4 holes.
The single punch creates 4 holes when the paper is unfolded.
questions.hint: Each fold doubles the number of layers being punched through.

questions.q: If he is tired, he will not study. If he doesn't study, he won't pass. He studied. Which statement MUST be true?
questions.exp:
He studied → (by contrapositive of rule 1) he was NOT tired.
The contrapositive of 'tired → don't study' is 'studied → not tired'.
questions.hint: Work backwards: if he DID study, what does that tell you about whether he was tired?

questions.q: A shirt costs $45 after a 10% discount. What was the original price?
questions.exp:
Original price → ×1.25 (25% increase) → ×0.90 (10% decrease off marked price)
= 100 × 1.25 × 0.90 = 100 × 1.125 = 112.50
Profit = $12.50 on $100 cost = 12.5%
💡 Apply multipliers in sequence, don't try to add/subtract percentages.
questions.hint: If $45 is 90%, find what 100% is.

questions.q: What is the perimeter of a rectangle with length 8 cm and width 5 cm?
questions.exp: Perimeter = 2 × (length + width) = 2 × (8 + 5) = 2 × 13 = 26 cm
questions.hint: Perimeter means all sides added together. A rectangle has 2 lengths and 2 widths.

questions.q: A tap fills a tank in 6 hours. Another tap fills the same tank in 3 hours. If both taps are open, how long to fill the tank?
questions.exp:
Tap 1 fills 1/6 per hour. Tap 2 fills 1/3 per hour.
Combined: 1/6 + 1/3 = 1/6 + 2/6 = 3/6 = 1/2 per hour.
Time = 1 ÷ (1/2) = 2 hours.
questions.hint: Find what fraction of the tank each tap fills per hour, then add.

questions.q: The sum of three consecutive even numbers is 48. What is the smallest number?
questions.exp:
Let the numbers be n, n+2, n+4.
n + n+2 + n+4 = 48 → 3n + 6 = 48 → 3n = 42 → n = 14.
Numbers: 14, 16, 18. Smallest = 14.
questions.hint: Call the first number n. Consecutive even numbers differ by 2.

questions.q: A circular pond has a diameter of 14 m. What is the area of the pond? (π ≈ 22/7)
questions.hint: Remember diameter = 2 × radius. Area = π × r².

questions.q:
Read: 'Despite being discovered over 200 years ago, Antarctica remains the least understood continent. Its ice holds climate records stretching back 800,000 years.'

What can we infer?
options: Antarctica's ice contains valuable historical data
questions.exp:
800,000 years of climate records in the ice → the ice holds valuable historical/scientific data.
This is an inference — not directly stated but clearly implied.
questions.hint: Why would the author mention 800,000-year records? What value does that suggest?

questions.q: Which word is closest in meaning to 'tenacious'?
questions.exp:
Tenacious = holding firmly to something; not giving up; persistent.
From Latin tenere (to hold).
questions.hint: Think of 'tenacity' — what quality does it describe?

questions.q: What is ¾ + ⅝?
questions.exp:
3/8 of 200 = (200 ÷ 8) × 3 = 25 × 3 = 75
Always find the unit fraction first (÷ denominator), then multiply by numerator.
questions.hint: Convert to a common denominator (eighths).

questions.q: Which is a prime number?
questions.exp:
29 is only divisible by 1 and 29 — it is prime.
21 = 3×7, 27 = 3×9, 33 = 3×11.
questions.hint: Check each number: can it be divided evenly by anything other than 1 and itself?

questions.q: Expand and simplify: 2(x + 3) + 3(x − 1)
questions.hint: Expand each bracket first, then collect like terms.

questions.q: What is the gradient of the line y = 3x − 4?
questions.exp:
3(2x − 1) = 2(x + 5)
6x − 3 = 2x + 10
4x = 13
x = 3.25
💡 Always expand brackets fully before collecting like terms.
questions.hint: The equation is in y = mx + b form. What is the coefficient of x?

questions.q: Solve: x² = 49
questions.exp:
x² = 49 → x = ±√49 = ±7
Always two solutions for x² = positive number.
questions.hint: Square roots have two solutions — a positive and a negative.

questions.q: A right triangle has legs of 9 cm and 12 cm. What is the hypotenuse?
questions.exp:
c² = 9² + 12² = 81 + 144 = 225
c = 15 cm
(This is the 3-4-5 triple × 3)
questions.hint: Use Pythagoras: a² + b² = c².

questions.q: A car and a truck leave the same point in opposite directions. The car travels at 80 km/h and the truck at 60 km/h. How far apart are they after 2.5 hours?
questions.exp:
Car travels: 80 × 2.5 = 200 km
Truck travels: 60 × 2.5 = 150 km
Total distance apart = 200 + 150 = 350 km
questions.hint: They travel in opposite directions so their distances add together.

questions.q: A rectangular box is 10 cm long, 6 cm wide, and 4 cm tall. What is its volume?
questions.exp:
Volume of cylinder = π × r² × h
= 3.14 × 4² × 10
= 3.14 × 16 × 10
= 502.4 cm³
💡 Don't forget to square the radius, not the diameter.
questions.hint: Volume of a rectangular box = l × w × h.

questions.q:
3, 6, 12, 24, ___, 96
What is the missing number?
questions.hint: What operation connects each term to the next?

questions.q: In the pattern △○△△○△△△○..., what is the 15th symbol?
questions.exp:
Pattern: △○ (2), △△○ (3), △△△○ (4)...
Group lengths: 2, 3, 4, 5, 6...
Total after groups 1-4: 2+3+4+5=14. So 15th symbol is the 1st of group 5 = △... 
Alternately: blocks of increasing triangles then a circle. 15th = ○.
questions.hint: Count how the pattern grows with each cycle.
Subtract one equation from the other to find the difference between ▲ and ■.

questions.q: THERMOMETER is to TEMPERATURE as BAROMETER is to:
questions.exp:
A thermometer measures temperature.
A barometer measures atmospheric pressure.
questions.hint: What does a barometer measure in weather science?

questions.q: In a competition, 3 students scored 90% on all 3 tests. Ellie says '6 students will get prizes.' What is Ellie's mistake?
options: She forgot to add the scores
The same students may have scored 90% on all 3 tests
The prizes are for different tests
questions.exp: Ellie assumed 3 different students scored 90% on each test = 9 students, taking 3 prizes each. But the same 3 students could have scored 90% on ALL tests, giving 3 prize-winners, not 6.
questions.hint: Could the same student score 90% on more than one test?

questions.q: A clock shows 3:45. How many degrees has the minute hand moved from the 12 o'clock position?
questions.exp:
The minute hand moves 6° per minute.
At 45 minutes: 45 × 6° = 270°
questions.hint: The minute hand moves 360° in 60 minutes — that's 6° per minute.

questions.q:
Read: 'Maya had always been the cautious one. Yet when the moment came, she was first through the door.'

What does this BEST suggest?
options: Maya had planned this all along
Maya was pushed through the door
questions.exp: The contrast between 'always cautious' and 'first through the door' suggests the situation was significant enough to override her usual nature.
questions.hint: Focus on the contrast: what changed between her usual self and her action?

questions.q:
Read: 'The factory churned, groaned, and spat smoke into the pale sky.'

Which techniques are used? (Choose the BEST answer)
questions.exp:
'Churned, groaned, and spat' gives human/animal actions to the factory (personification).
'Churned, groaned, and spat' is also a list of three (rule of three/tricolon).
questions.hint: Does the factory actually groan? And how many verbs are listed?

questions.q: A swimming pool holds 180,000 litres. A pump can fill 1,500 litres per minute. How many hours does it take to fill the pool?
questions.exp: Time = 180,000 ÷ 1,500 = 120 minutes = 2 hours.
questions.hint: Divide total volume by rate, then convert minutes to hours.

questions.q: A number leaves remainder 3 when divided by 5, remainder 2 when divided by 3. What is the smallest such positive number?
questions.exp:
Need n ≡ 3 (mod 5) and n ≡ 2 (mod 3).
Try 8: 8÷5=1 rem 3 ✓, 8÷3=2 rem 2 ✓
Smallest = 8. Wait: answer is 8 (index 0).
questions.hint: List numbers that leave remainder 3 when divided by 5 (3, 8, 13, 18...) and check which also leaves remainder 2 when divided by 3.

questions.q: A pizza shop offers 3 bases and 5 toppings. If you must choose 1 base and exactly 2 different toppings, how many different pizzas can you make?
questions.exp:
Ways to choose 2 toppings from 5 = 5C2 = 5!/(2!×3!) = 10
Bases = 3
Total = 3 × 10 = 30
questions.hint: How many ways can you choose 2 from 5? Then multiply by the number of bases.

questions.q:
What is the 10th term of this sequence?
5, 8, 11, 14, ...
questions.exp:
Arithmetic sequence: first term a=5, common difference d=3.
Term n = a + (n−1)d = 5 + 9×3 = 5 + 27 = 32
questions.hint: Find the rule: each term increases by 3. Use: nth term = first term + (n-1) × difference.

questions.q: MENDACIOUS is to TRUTHFUL as INDOLENT is to:
questions.exp:
Mendacious = dishonest, opposite of truthful.
Indolent = lazy, opposite of diligent.
The analogy is: word : antonym.
questions.hint: Mendacious means dishonest (opposite of truthful). What is the opposite of indolent (lazy)?

questions.q:
Which does NOT belong?
Amnesty · Pardon · Reprieve · Sentence
questions.exp:
Amnesty, Pardon, and Reprieve all mean releasing someone from punishment or reducing a sentence.
Sentence is the punishment itself — the odd one out.
questions.hint: Three of these words relate to reducing or cancelling punishment. Which one is the punishment itself?

questions.q: Anthony says: 'Janice is fit. She trains every day and never gets injured. She'll be a great athlete.' What is the flaw in Anthony's reasoning?
options: He assumes fitness and injury-free means future success
questions.exp: Anthony assumes that being fit and injury-free now guarantees future success as an athlete — but many other factors determine athletic achievement.
questions.hint: What extra step does Anthony take beyond what the evidence supports?

questions.q: Tom earns $15/hour and works 6 hours on Saturday and 4 hours on Sunday. He spends $32 on transport. How much does he take home?
questions.hint: Calculate total earnings first, then subtract expenses.

questions.q: Two angles in a triangle are 47° and 68°. What is the third angle?
questions.exp:
Angles in a triangle sum to 180°.
180° − 47° − 68° = 65°
questions.hint: All angles in a triangle add to 180°.

questions.q: What is the LCM (lowest common multiple) of 4 and 6?
questions.exp:
Multiples of 4: 4, 8, 12, 16...
Multiples of 6: 6, 12, 18...
LCM = 12
questions.hint: List multiples of each number and find the first one they share.

questions.q: Order from smallest to largest: 0.35, 0.305, 0.3, 0.053
questions.hint: Add trailing zeros to make them all the same length, then compare digit by digit.

questions.q: In a class, 60% are girls. 30% of girls and 50% of boys play sport. What percentage of the whole class plays sport?
questions.exp:
Girls = 60%, boys = 40%.
Girls playing sport = 30% × 60% = 18%.
Boys playing sport = 50% × 40% = 20%.
Total = 18% + 20% = 38%
questions.hint: Find the percentage of each gender that plays sport, then combine (weighted average).

questions.q:
'Her eyes were stars in the darkness.'
This is an example of:
questions.exp:
A metaphor directly compares two things without using 'like' or 'as'.
'Eyes WERE stars' — not 'like stars'.
questions.hint: Does this use 'like' or 'as'? If not, it's a metaphor.

questions.q: Which is a compound sentence?
options: I wanted pizza, but we ordered pasta.
questions.exp:
A compound sentence joins two independent clauses with a conjunction (FANBOYS).
'I wanted pizza, but we ordered pasta' has two complete clauses joined by 'but'.
questions.hint: A compound sentence has two complete sentences joined by a connecting word.

questions.q: Which word is closest in meaning to 'garrulous'?
questions.exp:
Garrulous = excessively talkative, especially on trivial matters.
From Latin garrire (to chatter).
questions.hint: Think of someone who talks constantly about anything and everything.

questions.q: LACONIC is to VERBOSE as PARSIMONIOUS is to:
questions.exp:
Laconic (brief) is the opposite of verbose (wordy).
Parsimonious (very reluctant to spend money) is the opposite of generous.
questions.hint: Laconic and verbose are opposites. Parsimonious means extremely tight with money — what's its opposite?

questions.q: In a 4×4 magic square where all rows, columns, and diagonals sum to 34, the top row is 16, 3, 2, 13. What must the bottom-left number be if the first column is 16, 5, 9, ___?
questions.exp: Column 1 must sum to 34: 16 + 5 + 9 + x = 34 → x = 4.
questions.hint: All columns must sum to 34. Add the known values in the column and subtract from 34.

questions.q: Factorise: x² + 5x + 6
questions.exp:
Find two numbers that multiply to 6 and add to 5: 2 × 3 = 6, 2 + 3 = 5 ✓
x² + 5x + 6 = (x+2)(x+3)
questions.hint: Find two numbers that multiply to 6 and add to 5.

questions.q: What is the highest common factor (HCF) of 36 and 48?
questions.hint: List all factors of each number and find the largest one they share.

questions.q: There are only red and blue pens in a drawer. The ratio of red to blue is 2:3. If 4 red pens are added, the new ratio is 1:1. How many pens were there originally?
questions.exp:
Let red = 2k, blue = 3k.
After adding 4 red: (2k+4)/(3k) = 1 → 2k+4 = 3k → k = 4.
Original total = 2(4) + 3(4) = 8 + 12 = 20.
questions.hint: Let the original amounts be 2k and 3k. Set up an equation using the new ratio.

questions.q: A square has area 64 cm². A circle is drawn inside it, touching all four sides. What is the area of the circle? (π ≈ 3.14)
questions.exp:
Square side = √64 = 8 cm. Circle diameter = 8 cm, radius = 4 cm.
Area = π × r² = 3.14 × 16 = 50.24 cm²
questions.hint: If the circle touches all four sides, its diameter equals the side length of the square.

questions.q:
Find the missing term:
2, 6, 12, 20, 30, ___
questions.exp:
Differences: 4, 6, 8, 10, 12...
The differences increase by 2 each time.
30 + 12 = 42
questions.hint: Look at the differences between consecutive terms. Do those differences follow a pattern?

questions.q: An 'enigma' is:
options: A mysterious or puzzling person or thing
questions.exp:
Enigma = something difficult to understand or explain; a mystery.
Famous use: the Enigma machine (WWII codebreaker).
questions.hint: You might know the phrase 'wrapped in an enigma' — what would that mean?

questions.q:
In a code, A=1, B=3, C=5, D=7... (odd numbers in order).
What is the code for the word CAB?
questions.hint: Map each letter to its odd number (A=1st odd, B=2nd odd, C=3rd odd...).

questions.q: A bar graph shows monthly rainfall. Jan=45mm, Feb=30mm, Mar=60mm, Apr=55mm. What is the mean monthly rainfall for these four months?
questions.hint: Add all values, divide by the count.

questions.q:
Read: 'The council approved the development unanimously. The chairperson smiled. The protestors outside fell silent.'

What can we MOST reasonably infer?
options: The protestors were happy with the decision
The protestors were against the development
questions.exp:
Protestors 'fell silent' after an unfavourable decision — silence here likely signals defeat or disappointment, not approval.
The contrast between the chairperson's smile and the protestors' silence implies they had opposing views.
questions.hint: Why would protestors 'fall silent'? Consider the contrast with the chairperson's smile.

questions.q: All squares are rectangles. Some rectangles are rhombuses. Jake draws a square. Which MUST be true?
options: Jake's shape is only a square
questions.exp:
All squares are rectangles (given) → Jake's square must be a rectangle.
We cannot conclude it's a rhombus — only SOME rectangles are rhombuses.
questions.hint: Follow the logical chain: square → rectangle. Does this tell us about rhombus?

questions.q: The difference between two numbers is 8. Their product is 84. What are the numbers?
questions.exp:
Let numbers be n and n+8.
n(n+8) = 84 → n² + 8n − 84 = 0 → (n+14)(n−6) = 0 → n = 6.
Numbers: 6 and 14. Check: 14−6=8 ✓ and 6×14=84 ✓
questions.hint: Call the smaller number n and the larger n+8. Multiply them and set equal to 84.

questions.q: NOVEL is to AUTHOR as PAINTING is to:
questions.exp:
Relationship: creation → creator.
An author writes a novel. An artist paints a painting.
questions.hint: Who creates a painting?

questions.q: In a triangle, one angle is twice the second angle, and the third angle is 60°. What are the other two angles?
questions.exp:
Let second angle = x. First = 2x. Third = 60°.
2x + x + 60 = 180 → 3x = 120 → x = 40°.
Angles: 40°, 80°, 60°. Sum = 180° ✓
questions.hint: Let the second angle = x. Set up an equation using the angle sum = 180°.

questions.q: What is the smallest number that is divisible by both 8 and 12 but NOT by 9?
questions.exp:
LCM of 8 and 12 = 24.
24 ÷ 9 = 2.67 (not divisible) ✓
So 24 is the answer.
questions.hint: Find the LCM of 8 and 12, then check divisibility by 9.

questions.q:
Read: 'Firstly, exercise improves mood. Secondly, it builds strength. Finally, regular activity reduces disease risk.'

This text structure is best described as:
questions.exp:
The words 'firstly, secondly, finally' signal that ideas are being listed in sequence.
This is a listing or sequential structure.
questions.hint: Look at the signal words 'firstly, secondly, finally' — what do they tell you about the structure?

questions.q:
The numbers in this grid go together in a certain way.
| 3 | 7 | 21 |
| 4 | 9 | 36 |
| 5 | 6 |  ? |
What is the missing number?
questions.exp:
Rule: column 1 × column 2 = column 3.
3×7=21 ✓  4×9=36 ✓  5×6=30 ✓
questions.hint: Look at how the numbers in each row relate to each other.

questions.q:
The numbers in this grid go together in a certain way.
| 2 | 5 | 3 |
| 4 | 9 | 5 |
| 6 | ? | 7 |
What replaces the question mark?
questions.exp:
Rule: middle = left + right.
2+3=5 ✓  4+5=9 ✓  6+7=13 ✓
questions.hint: Look at how the middle number relates to the two outer numbers in each row.

questions.q:
What is the missing number in this sequence?
100, 91, 82, 73, ?, 55
questions.hint: Find the difference between consecutive terms.

questions.q:
What is the missing number?
3, 4, 7, 11, 18, ?, 47
questions.exp:
Each term = sum of the two previous (like Fibonacci):
3+4=7, 4+7=11, 7+11=18, 11+18=29, 18+29=47 ✓
questions.hint: Try adding each pair of consecutive terms.

questions.q:
▲ + ▲ + ■ = 23
▲ + ■ + ■ = 19
What is the value of ▲?
questions.hint: Subtract one equation from the other to eliminate one shape.

questions.q:
● × ■ = 24   and   ● + ■ = 10   (assume ● > ■)
What is ● − ■?
questions.exp:
Two numbers that multiply to 24 and add to 10: try 4 and 6.
4×6=24 ✓  4+6=10 ✓
● = 6, ■ = 4 → ● − ■ = 2
questions.hint: Find two numbers that multiply to 24 AND add to 10.

questions.q: A school has 480 students. ⅜ play sport, and ¼ of those who play sport also play a musical instrument. How many students play both sport and an instrument?
questions.exp:
Students who play sport: ⅜ × 480 = 180
Of those, ¼ play instrument: ¼ × 180 = 45
questions.hint: Find the number who play sport first, then find ¼ of that group.

questions.q: A shop marks up an item by 40%, then offers a 20% sale discount. The final price is $84. What was the original price?
questions.exp:
Let original = P.
After 40% markup: 1.4P
After 20% discount: 1.4P × 0.8 = 1.12P
1.12P = $84 → P = 84 ÷ 1.12 = $75
questions.hint: Work forwards: apply the markup, then the discount.

questions.q: Concrete is made by mixing sand, gravel, and cement in the ratio 3:5:2. If 60 kg of gravel is used, how much sand is needed?
questions.exp:
Gravel = 5 parts = 60 kg → 1 part = 12 kg
Sand = 3 parts = 3 × 12 = 36 kg
questions.hint: Find the value of one 'part' using the gravel amount.

questions.q:
Factorise and simplify:
(x² − 9) ÷ (x + 3)
questions.exp:
x² − 9 = (x+3)(x−3)  [difference of two squares]
(x+3)(x−3) ÷ (x+3) = x − 3
questions.hint: Factorise the numerator. Do you recognise a² − b²?

questions.q: Seven students scored: 65, 72, 58, 80, 72, 91, 72. What is the mode?
questions.exp:
Mode = most frequent value.
72 appears 3 times — more than any other score.
questions.hint: Mode = the value that appears most often.

questions.q: The mean of 5 numbers is 14. Four of the numbers are 10, 12, 16, 18. What is the fifth number?
questions.exp:
Sum = 5 × 14 = 70
Sum of known four: 10+12+16+18 = 56
Fifth = 70 − 56 = 14
questions.hint: Find the total sum first (mean × count), then subtract what you know.

questions.q: What is the smallest number that gives a remainder of 2 when divided by both 5 and 7?
questions.exp:
Need n ≡ 2 (mod 5) and n ≡ 2 (mod 7).
LCM(5,7)=35. Numbers giving rem 2 when ÷35: 2, 37, 72...
37÷5=7 rem 2 ✓  37÷7=5 rem 2 ✓
Smallest positive = 37
questions.hint: Find LCM of 5 and 7, then add the remainder.

questions.q: A square has perimeter 48 cm. What is its area?
questions.exp:
Perimeter = 4 × side → side = 48÷4 = 12 cm
Area = 12² = 144 cm²
questions.hint: Find the side length from the perimeter, then square it.

questions.q: A bus runs every 15 minutes. A train runs every 10 minutes. Both depart together at 8:00 AM. When do they next depart together?
questions.exp:
LCM(15, 10) = 30 minutes
Next joint departure: 8:00 AM + 30 min = 8:30 AM
questions.hint: Find the LCM of the two intervals.

questions.q:
Five friends — Ali, Ben, Cara, Dan, Eva — sit in a row.
• Ben sits between Cara and Dan.
• Ali sits next to Cara but not Dan.
• Eva is at one end.
What position is Ali in?
questions.exp:
Eva is at one end (position 1 or 5).
Ben between Cara and Dan: C−B−D or D−B−C.
Ali next to Cara, not Dan.
One valid order: Eva, Ali, Cara, Ben, Dan.
Ali is in position 2.
questions.hint: Start with Eva at one end, then use each constraint to place people.

questions.q:
All musicians can read music. Some readers of music cannot play an instrument. Priya reads music.
Which MUST be true?
options: Priya might not be a musician
questions.exp:
Reading music does NOT mean you are a musician — the rule says musicians can read music, not the reverse.
So Priya might or might not be a musician.
questions.hint: Does reading music guarantee being a musician? Check the direction of the rule.

questions.q: OBLIVIOUS is to AWARE as FRUGAL is to:
questions.exp:
Oblivious (unaware) ↔ Aware — antonyms.
Frugal (careful with money) ↔ Generous — antonyms.
questions.hint: Oblivious and aware are opposites. What is the opposite of frugal?

questions.q: If a + b = 7 and a² + b² = 29, what is ab?
questions.exp:
(a+b)² = a² + 2ab + b²
7² = 29 + 2ab
49 = 29 + 2ab
2ab = 20
ab = 10
questions.hint: Use the identity (a+b)² = a² + 2ab + b².

questions.q: The midpoint of a line segment joining A(2, 4) and B(8, 10) is:
questions.hint: Midpoint = (average of x-coords, average of y-coords).

questions.q: Ali saved $120 and Ben saved $80. Ali spent ½ of his savings and Ben spent ¼ of his. How much did they have altogether after spending?
questions.exp:
Ali remaining: 120 × ½ = $60
Ben remaining: 80 × ¾ = $60
Total = $60 + $60 = $120
questions.hint: Calculate what each person spent, then find what remains.

questions.q: A group of children share some stickers. If each child gets 5, there are 8 left over. If each child gets 7, they need 4 more. How many children are there?
questions.exp:
Let n = children, s = total stickers.
s = 5n + 8  and  s = 7n − 4
5n + 8 = 7n − 4 → 2n = 12 → n = 6
questions.hint: Set up two equations for the number of stickers.

questions.q: In a school, ⅖ of students are in Year 4 and below. ¾ of the remaining students are in Year 5. What fraction of the school is in Year 5?
questions.exp:
Remaining after Year 4: 1 − ⅖ = 3/5
Year 5 = ¾ × 3/5 = 9/20
questions.hint: Find the remaining fraction first, then take ¾ of that.

questions.q: The ratio of Tom's cards to Sara's cards is 3:5. Tom gives Sara 12 cards and the ratio becomes 1:3. How many cards did Tom start with?
questions.exp:
Let Tom = 3k, Sara = 5k.
After transfer: (3k−12)/(5k+12) = 1/3
3(3k−12) = 5k+12
9k − 36 = 5k + 12
4k = 48 → k = 12
Tom's original = 3 × 12 = 36
questions.hint: Let the original amounts be 3k and 5k. Write the new ratio as an equation.

questions.q: Train A leaves at 8:00 AM at 90 km/h. Train B leaves at 9:30 AM on the same route at 120 km/h. At what time does Train B overtake Train A?
questions.exp:
Train A head start: 1.5 hr × 90 km/h = 135 km
B gains at: 120−90 = 30 km/h
Time to close gap: 135 ÷ 30 = 4.5 hrs after B departs
9:30 AM + 4.5 hrs = 2:00 PM
questions.hint: Find how far ahead Train A is when B departs, then calculate how fast B closes that gap.

questions.q: Mum is 4 times as old as her daughter now. In 6 years, she will be 3 times as old. How old is the daughter now?
questions.exp:
Let daughter = d, mum = 4d.
In 6 years: 4d+6 = 3(d+6)
4d+6 = 3d+18
d = 12
questions.hint: Set up an equation for 'in 6 years'.

questions.q: A tank is ⅔ full. After 30 litres are removed, it is ½ full. What is the full capacity of the tank?
questions.exp:
⅔ − ½ = 4/6 − 3/6 = 1/6 of tank = 30 litres
Full tank = 30 × 6 = 180 litres
questions.hint: The 30 litres represents the difference between ⅔ and ½ of the full tank.

questions.q: Lena has only 50c and 20c coins. She has 18 coins worth $6.60 in total. How many 50c coins does she have?
questions.exp:
Let f = number of 50c coins, t = number of 20c coins.
f + t = 18  and  0.5f + 0.2t = 6.6
t = 18−f → 0.5f + 0.2(18−f) = 6.6
0.5f + 3.6 − 0.2f = 6.6
0.3f = 3 → f = 10
questions.hint: Set up two equations: one for the number of coins, one for the total value.

questions.q: A salesperson earns 5% commission on sales. Last month she earned $2,250 in commission. Her sales increased by 20% this month. How much commission this month?
questions.hint: Find last month's total sales first, then increase by 20%.

questions.q: A rectangular room is 12 m × 9 m. Tiles measuring 0.5 m × 0.5 m cover the floor. How many tiles are needed?
questions.exp:
Area of room = 12 × 9 = 108 m²
Area per tile = 0.5 × 0.5 = 0.25 m²
Tiles = 108 ÷ 0.25 = 432
questions.hint: Find the total area and the area of one tile, then divide.

questions.q: A triangle has base 14 cm and height 9 cm. A rectangle with the same area is 7 cm wide. What is its length?
questions.exp:
Triangle area = ½ × 14 × 9 = 63 cm²
Rectangle: 63 = 7 × length → length = 9 cm
questions.hint: Find the triangle's area first, then use it for the rectangle.

questions.q: When a number is multiplied by 6 and 15 is added, the result equals multiplying the number by 9 and subtracting 6. What is the number?
questions.hint: Set up an equation with n as the unknown number.

questions.q: A number has exactly 3 factors. Which could it be?
questions.exp:
A number with exactly 3 factors must be the square of a prime (p²): factors are 1, p, p².
25 = 5² → factors: 1, 5, 25 ✓
16 = 2⁴ → 5 factors
questions.hint: What kind of numbers have exactly 3 factors?

questions.q: Two parallel lines are cut by a transversal. One co-interior angle is 73°. What is the other co-interior angle?
questions.exp:
Co-interior angles between parallel lines add to 180°.
180° − 73° = 107°
questions.hint: Co-interior angles always add to 180°.

questions.q: The sum of digits of a 2-digit number is 11. If digits are reversed, the new number is 27 more. What is the original number?
questions.exp:
Let digits be a and b: a+b=11, (10b+a)−(10a+b)=27
9(b−a)=27 → b−a=3
a+b=11 and b=a+3: 2a=8 → a=4, b=7
Original = 47
questions.hint: Set up two equations: one for digit sum, one for the reversed number.

questions.q:
Read:
"The platypus is one of only five species of monotreme — mammals that lay eggs. Found only in eastern Australia and Tasmania, it hunts underwater using electroreception: tiny pores in its bill detect the electrical signals produced by the muscles of its prey. Unlike most mammals, the platypus has no teeth; it grinds food between horny pads. Males carry a venomous spur on their hind legs — one of the very few venomous mammals. Early European scientists who received platypus specimens believed they were a hoax."

What method does the platypus use to locate its prey?
questions.exp:
The passage directly states 'it hunts underwater using electroreception'.
This is a literal retrieval question.
questions.hint: Look for the exact words describing how the platypus finds its prey.

questions.q:
Read:
"The platypus is one of only five species of monotreme — mammals that lay eggs. Found only in eastern Australia and Tasmania, it hunts underwater using electroreception: tiny pores in its bill detect the electrical signals produced by the muscles of its prey. Unlike most mammals, the platypus has no teeth; it grinds food between horny pads. Males carry a venomous spur on their hind legs — one of the very few venomous mammals. Early European scientists who received platypus specimens believed they were a hoax."

What does 'monotreme' most likely mean based on the passage?
questions.exp: The passage defines monotremes as 'mammals that lay eggs' immediately after the term.
questions.hint: The passage defines the word right after it uses it.

questions.q:
Read:
"The platypus is one of only five species of monotreme — mammals that lay eggs. Found only in eastern Australia and Tasmania, it hunts underwater using electroreception: tiny pores in its bill detect the electrical signals produced by the muscles of its prey. Unlike most mammals, the platypus has no teeth; it grinds food between horny pads. Males carry a venomous spur on their hind legs — one of the very few venomous mammals. Early European scientists who received platypus specimens believed they were a hoax."

Why did early European scientists believe the platypus specimens were a hoax?
options: The platypus had so many unusual features they seemed impossible to combine in one animal
The specimen was damaged in transit
questions.exp:
The passage lists the platypus's extraordinary features: egg-laying mammal, electroreception, no teeth, venomous spur.
We must INFER that these combined features seemed too extraordinary to believe.
questions.hint: What unusual features are described? Why would these make scientists doubtful?

questions.q:
Read:
"The platypus is one of only five species of monotreme — mammals that lay eggs. Found only in eastern Australia and Tasmania, it hunts underwater using electroreception: tiny pores in its bill detect the electrical signals produced by the muscles of its prey. Unlike most mammals, the platypus has no teeth; it grinds food between horny pads. Males carry a venomous spur on their hind legs — one of the very few venomous mammals. Early European scientists who received platypus specimens believed they were a hoax."

Why does the author save the fact about scientists thinking the platypus was a hoax for the final sentence?
options: To create dramatic irony — we now know it's real, yet experts once doubted it
To end with an unimportant detail
questions.exp:
Leaving the 'hoax' fact last creates a satisfying irony: after reading all the real facts,
we learn that experts doubted its existence. This is a deliberate structural choice for impact.
questions.hint: Why would an author leave something surprising for the very last sentence?

questions.q:
Read:
"For decades, scientists dismissed dark matter as theoretical. Then, in 1978, astronomer Vera Rubin published data showing that galaxies rotate in ways that cannot be explained by visible matter alone — the outer edges spin just as fast as the inner regions, defying Newton's laws. Something invisible must be holding galaxies together. Rubin's work, ignored during her lifetime by the Nobel Committee, has since been called the most important astronomical discovery of the twentieth century."

What is the main idea of this passage?
options: Dark matter has been proven to exist
Vera Rubin's evidence for dark matter was groundbreaking but underrecognised
Galaxies spin at the same speed throughout
questions.exp:
The passage covers both the scientific discovery AND the fact that she was overlooked.
The main idea captures both dimensions.
questions.hint: Which option captures BOTH what she discovered AND what happened to her recognition?

questions.q:
Read:
"For decades, scientists dismissed dark matter as theoretical. Then, in 1978, astronomer Vera Rubin published data showing that galaxies rotate in ways that cannot be explained by visible matter alone — the outer edges spin just as fast as the inner regions, defying Newton's laws. Something invisible must be holding galaxies together. Rubin's work, ignored during her lifetime by the Nobel Committee, has since been called the most important astronomical discovery of the twentieth century."

What does 'ignored during her lifetime by the Nobel Committee' most strongly suggest?
options: She was not a real scientist
Her contribution was not properly recognised despite its significance
She chose not to accept the Nobel Prize
questions.exp:
Being ignored for the Nobel Prize despite doing 'the most important astronomical discovery'
of the century implies her contribution was not properly recognised.
questions.hint: If the work was later called the most important discovery of the century, what does being ignored imply?

questions.q:
Read:
"For decades, scientists dismissed dark matter as theoretical. Then, in 1978, astronomer Vera Rubin published data showing that galaxies rotate in ways that cannot be explained by visible matter alone — the outer edges spin just as fast as the inner regions, defying Newton's laws. Something invisible must be holding galaxies together. Rubin's work, ignored during her lifetime by the Nobel Committee, has since been called the most important astronomical discovery of the twentieth century."

In context, 'defying' most closely means:
questions.exp: Galaxy rotation 'defying Newton's laws' = contradicting or going against those laws.
questions.hint: If something 'defies' a law, does it support it or contradict it?

questions.q:
Read:
"For decades, scientists dismissed dark matter as theoretical. Then, in 1978, astronomer Vera Rubin published data showing that galaxies rotate in ways that cannot be explained by visible matter alone — the outer edges spin just as fast as the inner regions, defying Newton's laws. Something invisible must be holding galaxies together. Rubin's work, ignored during her lifetime by the Nobel Committee, has since been called the most important astronomical discovery of the twentieth century."

The author's tone when writing about the Nobel Committee can best be described as:
questions.exp:
'Ignored during her lifetime' paired with 'most important discovery of the century' creates an implied contrast.
The author is not neutral — there is implicit criticism of the committee.
questions.hint: Look at word choices: 'ignored', 'most important discovery'. What feeling does this contrast create?

questions.q:
Read:
"The Great Pacific Garbage Patch — a vast swirl of plastic debris in the North Pacific Ocean — is not a solid island of rubbish, as often depicted. It consists mostly of microplastics: tiny fragments invisible to the naked eye, suspended throughout the water column. These particles are consumed by marine creatures, entering the food chain. Scientists estimate it contains over 80,000 tonnes of plastic, but cleaning it remains a formidable challenge because the particles are too fine to be filtered without also capturing plankton — the foundation of the ocean food web."

Why is cleaning the garbage patch difficult?
options: The area is too dangerous for ships
Filtering particles would also capture plankton
The plastic is too heavy to collect
questions.exp: The passage directly states: 'too fine to be filtered without also capturing plankton'.
questions.hint: Look for the sentence explaining WHY cleaning is a 'formidable challenge'.

questions.q:
Read:
"The Great Pacific Garbage Patch — a vast swirl of plastic debris in the North Pacific Ocean — is not a solid island of rubbish, as often depicted. It consists mostly of microplastics: tiny fragments invisible to the naked eye, suspended throughout the water column. These particles are consumed by marine creatures, entering the food chain. Scientists estimate it contains over 80,000 tonnes of plastic, but cleaning it remains a formidable challenge because the particles are too fine to be filtered without also capturing plankton — the foundation of the ocean food web."

The author corrects the popular image of the garbage patch. What does this imply about public understanding?
options: The public fully understands the problem
A widespread misconception may make the problem harder to grasp and address
Scientists have misled the public intentionally
The problem is less serious than reported
questions.exp:
By contradicting the 'often depicted' solid island image, the author reveals a widespread misconception.
If people picture something solid, they may not grasp the invisible nature of microplastics.
questions.hint: If the common depiction is wrong, what does that tell us about how people understand the issue?

questions.q:
Read:
"The Great Pacific Garbage Patch — a vast swirl of plastic debris in the North Pacific Ocean — is not a solid island of rubbish, as often depicted. It consists mostly of microplastics: tiny fragments invisible to the naked eye, suspended throughout the water column. These particles are consumed by marine creatures, entering the food chain. Scientists estimate it contains over 80,000 tonnes of plastic, but cleaning it remains a formidable challenge because the particles are too fine to be filtered without also capturing plankton — the foundation of the ocean food web."

What text structure does the author primarily use?
questions.exp:
The passage: identifies the garbage patch → complicates it (it's microplastics, not solid)
→ further complicates the solution (can't filter without harming plankton).
This is a problem + complication structure.
questions.hint: Is the passage telling a story, comparing things, or building on a problem?

questions.q:
Read:
"Bees communicate the location of flowers through the 'waggle dance'. The dancer traces a figure-eight pattern while vibrating its abdomen. The angle of the straight middle run indicates direction relative to the sun. The duration of the waggle indicates distance: a 1-second waggle means roughly 1 kilometre. Karl von Frisch decoded this language in the 1940s and was awarded the Nobel Prize in Physiology in 1973."

What does the duration of the waggle dance indicate?
questions.exp: Passage states: 'The duration of the waggle indicates the distance'. Literal retrieval.
questions.hint: Find the sentence that directly explains what duration tells the bees.

questions.q:
Read:
"Bees communicate the location of flowers through the 'waggle dance'. The dancer traces a figure-eight pattern while vibrating its abdomen. The angle of the straight middle run indicates direction relative to the sun. The duration of the waggle indicates distance: a 1-second waggle means roughly 1 kilometre. Karl von Frisch decoded this language in the 1940s and was awarded the Nobel Prize in Physiology in 1973."

The passage mentions that von Frisch was awarded the Nobel Prize. What does this suggest about his work?
options: Decoding bee communication was considered a major scientific achievement
The Nobel Prize is given only for animal research
questions.exp:
The Nobel Prize is the world's most prestigious science award.
Receiving it implies his work was of the highest scientific significance.
questions.hint: What does receiving the Nobel Prize generally imply about someone's work?

questions.q:
Read:
"Until the 1970s, doctors routinely told patients with stomach ulcers that stress and spicy food were to blame. Then two Australian scientists — Barry Marshall and Robin Warren — proposed that ulcers were caused by a bacterium called Helicobacter pylori. The medical establishment rejected the idea; bacteria were thought unable to survive stomach acid. To prove his case, Marshall famously drank a solution containing H. pylori, developed gastritis within days, and cured himself with antibiotics. In 2005, Marshall and Warren received the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine."

What was the accepted medical view about ulcers before Marshall and Warren's discovery?
options: Stress and spicy food caused ulcers
questions.exp: The passage directly states 'doctors routinely told patients... stress and spicy food were to blame'.
questions.hint: Look for what doctors believed BEFORE the discovery.

questions.q:
Read:
"Until the 1970s, doctors routinely told patients with stomach ulcers that stress and spicy food were to blame. Then two Australian scientists — Barry Marshall and Robin Warren — proposed that ulcers were caused by a bacterium called Helicobacter pylori. The medical establishment rejected the idea; bacteria were thought unable to survive stomach acid. To prove his case, Marshall famously drank a solution containing H. pylori, developed gastritis within days, and cured himself with antibiotics. In 2005, Marshall and Warren received the Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine."

Why did Marshall drink the H. pylori solution himself?
options: He wanted to prove the bacterial cause beyond doubt when others wouldn't believe him
He had no other way to obtain the bacteria
He was testing whether he was immune
questions.exp:
'The medical establishment rejected the idea' — Marshall had to provide dramatic, undeniable proof.
Drinking the solution and then curing himself with antibiotics was a deliberate act of scientific demonstration.
questions.hint: Why would a scientist take such a risk? What did he need to prove, and to whom?

questions.q:
Sam says: 'Our school has produced 3 Olympic athletes. Other schools produce almost none. Therefore our training program is the reason.'
What is the main flaw?
options: Other explanations haven't been ruled out (talent, family support, etc.)
Olympic athletes are too rare to study
questions.exp:
Correlation ≠ causation. The training program might be responsible,
but other factors (naturally talented individuals happened to attend, parental support, etc.) haven't been ruled out.
questions.hint: Even if the school has more Olympians, does that prove the training program is the cause?

questions.q:
A politician says: 'Crime has fallen every year since we installed CCTV cameras. The cameras are working.'
What assumption is this making?
options: The cameras caused the drop in crime, not some other factor
questions.exp:
The argument assumes cameras caused the drop. But the fall could be due to other factors:
economic improvement, more police, demographic change. This is the correlation-causation fallacy.
questions.hint: Just because B followed A, does A necessarily cause B?

questions.q:
Statement: 'You can enter the club only if you are a member.'
A person IS a member. Can they definitely enter?
options: Not necessarily — membership is required but may not be the only condition
Yes, if they pay a fee
questions.exp:
'Only if you are a member' makes membership NECESSARY (you can't enter without it).
But it doesn't say membership ALONE is sufficient — other conditions may also apply.
questions.hint: Is membership necessary, sufficient, or both? Read the statement very carefully.

questions.q:
Emma argues: 'My friend stopped eating gluten and her headaches disappeared. Therefore, gluten causes headaches.'
What is the main flaw?
options: One case is insufficient to establish a general causal claim
questions.exp:
One personal example (anecdote) cannot establish a general causal claim.
Many variables change when someone changes their diet.
questions.hint: Can one example prove a general rule for everyone?

questions.q: Which is an example of circular reasoning?
options: All cats are mammals because mammals are animals
The book must be good because it's a bestseller
We should trust the newspaper because the newspaper says it is trustworthy
Exercise is healthy because doctors recommend it
questions.exp:
Circular reasoning uses the conclusion as its own evidence:
'Trust the newspaper because the newspaper says so.' The evidence IS the claim.
questions.hint: Which option uses the thing being proven as proof of itself?

questions.q:
No reptiles are warm-blooded. All dinosaurs were reptiles. Rex is a dinosaur.
Which MUST be true?
questions.exp:
No reptiles → warm-blooded. Rex is a reptile. Therefore Rex is NOT warm-blooded.
(Within these premises — setting aside modern science's reclassification.)
questions.hint: Follow the chain: reptile → not warm-blooded. Rex is a reptile. Therefore...?

questions.q: Which evidence BEST supports: 'Regular reading improves vocabulary'?
options: One child who reads a lot has a large vocabulary
A study of 10,000 children shows reading hours correlate with vocabulary size
questions.exp:
A large, controlled study across 10,000 subjects is the strongest evidence.
Single cases (anecdotes) and authority opinions are weaker forms.
questions.hint: Which type of evidence is most reliable for establishing a general claim?

questions.q:
Every student who wins the prize has studied for more than 100 hours.
Lucy studied for 120 hours.
Which conclusion follows?
options: Lucy has met a necessary condition for winning
Lucy studied more than any other student
questions.exp:
Studying 100+ hours is necessary but may not be sufficient for winning.
Lucy meets this condition, but other conditions may also be needed.
questions.hint: Is studying 100+ hours necessary, sufficient, or both for winning?

questions.q: HYPOTHESIS is to EXPERIMENT as QUESTION is to:
questions.exp:
A hypothesis leads to an experiment (to test it).
A question leads to research (to answer it).
Both involve moving from uncertainty to a method of investigation.
questions.hint: What do scientists do after forming a hypothesis?

questions.q:
'Students who eat breakfast perform better at school. Therefore, schools should provide free breakfast.'
What assumption does this argument make?
options: Providing breakfast would cause the improvement in performance
questions.exp:
The argument assumes: if eating breakfast correlates with better performance,
then PROVIDING breakfast will CAUSE that improvement.
This assumes the correlation is causal and that the school breakfast would have the same effect.
questions.hint: What must be true for the recommendation to follow from the evidence?

questions.q: MARATHON is to RUNNING as VELODROME is to:
questions.exp:
A marathon is a long-distance running event.
A velodrome is an arena/track for cycling.
questions.hint: What sport takes place in a velodrome?

questions.q: MYOPIA is to SIGHT as TINNITUS is to:
questions.exp:
Myopia (short-sightedness) affects sight.
Tinnitus (ringing in the ears) affects hearing.
questions.hint: What sense does tinnitus affect?

questions.q:
Which does NOT belong?
Clarinet · Oboe · Trumpet · Bassoon
questions.exp:
Clarinet, Oboe, Bassoon are all woodwind instruments.
Trumpet is a brass instrument.
questions.hint: Think about which family of instruments each belongs to.

questions.q: PERSPICACIOUS most closely means:
questions.exp:
Perspicacious = having a ready insight into things; shrewd, discerning.
From Latin perspicax (seeing through clearly).
questions.hint: The root 'perspic-' relates to seeing clearly.

questions.q: EQUIVOCATE most closely means:
options: To use ambiguous language to avoid commitment
questions.exp:
Equivocate = use ambiguous language to avoid committing to a position.
From Latin: aequi (equal) + vox (voice) — speaking both ways.
questions.hint: Think of 'equal voices' — speaking in a way that could mean two things.

questions.q: How many faces does a triangular prism have?
questions.exp:
2 triangular faces (top and bottom)
3 rectangular faces (sides)
Total = 5 faces
questions.hint: Count the triangular ends and the rectangular sides.

questions.q: A cube is painted red on all faces, then cut into 27 smaller cubes (3×3×3). How many small cubes have NO paint at all?
questions.exp:
The 1 cube in the very centre has no painted faces.
It is completely surrounded by other cubes.
questions.hint: Which position in a 3×3×3 arrangement is surrounded on all sides?

questions.q: Which sentence uses a subordinate clause correctly?
options: Although it was raining, but we went outside.
Because she studied hard, she passed the exam.
She passed, however she studied hard.
Despite of the rain, we went outside.
questions.exp:
'Because she studied hard' is a subordinate clause correctly attached to the main clause.
Option A: 'Although...but' = double conjunction (error).
Option D: 'Despite of' is incorrect.
questions.hint: A subordinate clause begins with a conjunction and cannot stand alone.

questions.q: Identify the sentence with an apostrophe used correctly:
questions.exp:
'The dog's lead' = possessive apostrophe correctly used.
'Its' (no apostrophe) = possessive pronoun. 'It's' = it is.
'Children's' (not childrens') — children is already plural.
'their work' (not 'there work').
questions.hint: Check each option: is the apostrophe used for possession or contraction correctly?

questions.q: AMBIVALENT most closely means:
questions.exp:
Ambivalent = having mixed feelings or contradictory ideas about something.
From Latin: ambi (both) + valere (strong) — strength in two directions.
questions.hint: The prefix 'ambi' means both (as in ambidextrous = using both hands).

questions.q: CAMOUFLAGE is to CONCEALMENT as ARMOUR is to:
questions.exp:
Camouflage serves the purpose of concealment.
Armour serves the purpose of protection.
Both are defined by their defensive purpose.
questions.hint: What is the main purpose of armour?

questions.q: Which pair has the MOST SIMILAR relationship to: DOCTOR : STETHOSCOPE?
questions.exp:
Doctor uses a stethoscope as their primary professional tool.
Painter uses a brush as their primary tool.
Restaurant/school/book are locations or outputs, not tools.
questions.hint: Focus on the relationship type: professional → their primary tool.

questions.q: SYCOPHANT most closely means:
options: A person who flatters to gain favour
questions.exp: Sycophant = someone who acts obsequiously toward those with power; a 'yes-man'.
questions.hint: A sycophant always agrees with and compliments powerful people.

questions.q:
Which does NOT belong?
Elegy · Sonnet · Haiku · Limerick · Allegory
questions.exp:
Elegy, Sonnet, Haiku, Limerick are all poetic forms.
Allegory is a narrative technique — not a poem form.
questions.hint: Which one is not a type of poem?

questions.q: PROLOGUE is to PLAY as PREFACE is to:
questions.exp:
A prologue comes at the start of a play.
A preface comes at the start of a book.
questions.hint: Where does a preface appear, and in what type of work?

questions.q: LOQUACIOUS most closely means:
questions.exp:
Loquacious = tending to talk a great deal.
From Latin loqui (to speak).
questions.hint: The root 'loqu-' means 'to speak' (also in eloquent, soliloquy).

questions.q: PHILANDERER is to FIDELITY as MISER is to:
questions.exp:
A philanderer lacks fidelity.
A miser lacks generosity — they hoard instead of sharing.
Both: person defined by the absence of a virtue.
questions.hint: What quality does a miser notably lack?

questions.q:
Which does NOT belong?
Stalactite · Stalagmite · Geode · Fjord
questions.exp:
Stalactites, stalagmites, and geodes are cave/underground rock formations.
A fjord is a coastal geographic feature (narrow sea inlet between cliffs).
questions.hint: Which of these is not found inside caves?

questions.q: OSSIFY is to BONE as CALCIFY is to:
questions.exp:
Ossify = to become bone-like and rigid.
Calcify = to become hardened by calcium deposits.
Both refer to a mineralisation/hardening process.
questions.hint: What does 'calcify' describe the formation of?

questions.q: What is the next prime number after 29?
questions.exp: 30=2×3×5 (not prime), 31 is prime (only factors: 1 and 31) ✓
questions.hint: Test each number: is it divisible by anything other than 1 and itself?
Expand the brackets first, then collect x terms on one side.

questions.q: A data set has mean 20, median 18, and mode 15. Which is most likely true?
options: The data is positively skewed (skewed right)
questions.exp:
When mean > median > mode, the distribution is positively skewed (right skew).
Large outliers pull the mean upward.
questions.hint: Compare the mean, median, and mode. Which way does the 'tail' point?

questions.q: A sector has radius 6 cm and central angle 60°. What is the arc length? (π ≈ 3.14)
questions.hint: Arc length = (angle ÷ 360) × circumference.

questions.q: A 2-litre paint tin covers 8 m². How many litres are needed to paint a 60 m² wall with 2 coats?
questions.exp:
1 tin (2 L) covers 8 m².
Litres per m²: 2/8 = 0.25 L/m²
60 m² × 0.25 = 15 L per coat
2 coats: 15 × 2 = 30 L
questions.hint: Find litres needed for one coat, then multiply by 2.

questions.q: A rectangular aquarium is 60 cm × 30 cm × 40 cm. It is ¾ full. How many litres of water? (1 L = 1000 cm³)
questions.exp:
Full volume = 60 × 30 × 40 = 72,000 cm³
¾ of 72,000 = 54,000 cm³ = 54 litres
questions.hint: Find the full volume, then take ¾. Convert cm³ to litres (÷1000).

questions.q: ⅗ of a number is 48. What is ¾ of the same number?
questions.hint: Find the full number first, then calculate ¾ of it.

questions.q: Row 1 has 1 dot, Row 2 has 3, Row 3 has 5, Row 4 has 7. How many dots in Row 10?
questions.exp:
Pattern: Row n has (2n − 1) dots.
Row 10: 2(10) − 1 = 19
questions.hint: Find the formula: how does the dot count relate to the row number?

questions.q: What is √144 + √25?
questions.hint: Find the square root of each number separately.

questions.q: A triangle has angles in the ratio 1:2:3. What is the largest angle?
questions.exp:
Total parts = 1+2+3 = 6. Total = 180°.
Each part = 30°.
Largest = 3 × 30° = 90°
questions.hint: Add the ratio parts, then find what each part equals in degrees.

questions.q: A car uses 8 L of fuel per 100 km. How much fuel for a 350 km trip?
questions.hint: Find the fuel rate per km, then multiply by the distance.

questions.q: If 2^x = 32, what is x?
questions.hint: List powers of 2 until you reach 32.

questions.q: Five numbers have a mean of 12. A sixth number is added and the new mean is 13. What is the sixth number?
questions.exp:
Original sum = 5 × 12 = 60
New sum = 6 × 13 = 78
Sixth number = 78 − 60 = 18
questions.hint: Find both totals (mean × count), then subtract.

questions.q: The radius of a circle is tripled. By what factor does the area increase?
questions.exp:
Area = πr²
New area = π(3r)² = 9πr²
Area increases by factor of 9.
questions.hint: Write the formula for the new area and compare it to the original.

questions.q: How many 3-digit numbers are divisible by both 4 and 6?
questions.hint: Find LCM first. Then count multiples in the 3-digit range.

questions.q: What is ¾ of 24?
questions.hint: Divide by 4 first to find one quarter, then multiply by 3.

questions.q: What is 0.7 × 0.3?
questions.exp: 7 × 3 = 21, then count 2 decimal places → 0.21
questions.hint: Multiply as whole numbers, then count decimal places.

questions.q: What is 15% of 200?
questions.hint: Find 10%, then 5%, then add them.

questions.q: What is the sum of all interior angles in a quadrilateral?
questions.exp: A quadrilateral can be split into 2 triangles: 2 × 180° = 360°
questions.hint: How many triangles can you divide a quadrilateral into?

questions.q: What is 125 × 8?
questions.hint: Think: 125 × 4 = 500, then double it.

questions.q: Which word is an adverb?
questions.exp:
Adverbs modify verbs and often end in '-ly'.
'Quickly' describes how something is done.
questions.hint: Which word describes HOW something is done?

questions.q: A movie starts at 2:45 PM and runs for 1 hour 35 minutes. When does it end?
questions.hint: Add the hours first, then the minutes.

questions.q: An item costs $50. GST (10%) is added. What is the total price?
questions.hint: Find 10% of the price, then add it.

questions.q: What is the value of 5³?
questions.hint: 5³ means 5 multiplied by itself 3 times.

questions.q: If x = 3, what is the value of 4x² − 2x + 1?
questions.hint: Substitute x = 3, then follow order of operations.

questions.q: No sharks are mammals. All dolphins are mammals. Can any sharks be dolphins?
options: Yes — some sharks might be dolphins
No — no shark can be a dolphin
questions.exp:
No sharks are mammals. All dolphins are mammals.
∴ Sharks and dolphins share no members. No shark can be a dolphin.
questions.hint: Draw a Venn diagram: the shark and dolphin circles cannot overlap.

questions.q: In a room of 30 people, 18 like tea, 15 like coffee, and 5 like neither. How many like BOTH tea and coffee?
questions.exp:
Like at least one: 30 − 5 = 25
By inclusion-exclusion: Both = 18 + 15 − 25 = 8
questions.hint: Use the inclusion-exclusion principle: T + C − Both = Total who like at least one.

questions.q: A clock's hour hand moves from 12 to 4. Through how many degrees does it rotate?
questions.exp:
Hour hand: 360° in 12 hours = 30° per hour.
4 hours × 30° = 120°
questions.hint: How many degrees does the hour hand move per hour?

questions.q:
In a code, each letter is replaced by the letter 3 places AFTER it (X→A, Y→B, Z→C).
What does KHOOR decode to?
questions.exp:
To decode: subtract 3 from each letter.
K-3=H, H-3=E, O-3=L, O-3=L, R-3=O → HELLO ✓
questions.hint: If the code adds 3 to each letter, decoding subtracts 3.

questions.q: Ben has more than Ali. Cara has less than Dan. Dan has less than Ali. Who has the least?
questions.exp:
Order from most to least: Ben > Ali > Dan > Cara
Cara has the least.
questions.hint: Write out the comparisons as a chain: who is greater than whom?

questions.q: Which opening sentence is MOST effective for a persuasive essay about banning single-use plastics?
options: Single-use plastics exist in the world.
Every minute, the equivalent of a garbage truck full of plastic enters our oceans — and we are choosing to let it happen.
I think we should ban single-use plastics because they are harmful.
questions.exp:
Option C uses a striking statistic and creates urgency and emotional impact ('choosing to let it happen').
Options A and B are vague. Option D uses 'I think' which weakens the authority of the argument.
questions.hint: Which opening is most likely to make a reader stop and pay attention?

questions.q: Which sentence demonstrates the BEST varied sentence structure?
options: She ran. She was tired. She stopped.
Although exhausted, she kept running until the very last metre, where she finally collapsed with a gasp.
She ran and she was tired and she stopped running.
Running was tiring for her and she had to stop.
questions.exp:
Option B uses a subordinate clause opener ('Although exhausted'), precise detail ('very last metre'),
and a strong verb ('collapsed with a gasp').
Options A and C are repetitive or overuse conjunctions.
questions.hint: Look for varied sentence openers and a mix of complex and simple structures.

questions.q: Choose the word most similar in meaning to VERBOSE:
questions.exp:
Verbose = using more words than needed; excessively wordy.
Antonym: laconic, concise, terse.
questions.hint: The root 'verb-' relates to words (as in verbal, verbatim).

questions.q: SURGEON is to SCALPEL as SCULPTOR is to:
questions.exp:
A surgeon's primary cutting tool is a scalpel.
A sculptor's primary shaping tool is a chisel.
questions.hint: What tool does a sculptor use to carve stone or wood?

questions.q: The scientist's findings were so ___ that even sceptics were convinced.
questions.exp:
'Irrefutable' means impossible to deny or disprove.
If even sceptics were convinced, the evidence must be impossible to argue against.
questions.hint: What kind of finding convinces people who are already doubtful?

questions.q: Which pair shows the SAME relationship as ARCHIPELAGO : ISLANDS?
questions.exp:
Archipelago = a group/cluster of islands.
The same 'collection/group : individual unit' relationship applies to:
Forest:Tree, Galaxy:Stars, Library:Books — all are correct.
questions.hint: What is an archipelago? Then find the same group-to-member relationship.

questions.q:
Which does NOT belong?
MEMOIR · BIOGRAPHY · AUTOBIOGRAPHY · ANTHOLOGY
questions.exp:
Memoir, Biography, Autobiography are all forms of non-fiction writing about a person's life.
An Anthology is a collection of works by various authors — not a life story.
questions.hint: Which one is not about documenting a person's life?

questions.q: IGNORANCE is to ENLIGHTENMENT as CHAOS is to:
questions.exp:
Ignorance and enlightenment are opposites (before/after understanding).
Chaos and order are opposites — the same contrast relationship.
questions.hint: Ignorance → enlightenment represents moving from disorder to clarity. What is chaos's opposite?

questions.q: TACITURN most closely means:
questions.exp:
Taciturn = reserved or uncommunicative in speech; saying little.
From Latin tacere (to be silent).
questions.hint: The root 'taci-' relates to silence (as in 'tacit agreement' — an unspoken one).

questions.q: Her response was deliberately ___, designed to avoid committing to any position.
questions.exp:
'Evasive' = avoiding a direct answer or commitment.
The clue is 'avoid committing to any position'.
questions.hint: Which word means deliberately avoiding giving a clear answer?

questions.q: METEOROLOGY is to WEATHER as SEISMOLOGY is to:
questions.exp:
Meteorology is the study of weather.
Seismology is the study of earthquakes and seismic activity.
questions.hint: The '-logy' suffix means 'study of'. What does 'seismo-' relate to?

questions.q: PRUDENT most closely means:
questions.exp:
Prudent = acting with care and thought for the future; wise and cautious.
From Latin prudens (foreseeing).
questions.hint: A prudent person thinks carefully before acting. What quality does this describe?

questions.q:
What are the next TWO numbers?
1, 3, 6, 10, 15, ___, ___
questions.hint: What is the pattern of differences between consecutive terms?

questions.q:
In this table, each row follows the same rule:
8 → 3 → 5
12 → 5 → 7
20 → 9 → ?
What is the missing number?
questions.exp:
Rule: col1 − col2 = col3 − 1? No.
8−3=5 ✓, 12−5=7 ✓, 20−9=11 ✓
Rule: col1 − col2 = col3.
questions.hint: Look at how the third number relates to the first two in each row.
Substitute ▲ = 5 and solve for ■.

questions.q:
What is the missing number?
2, 3, 5, 8, 13, ___, 34
questions.exp:
Fibonacci-like: each term = sum of the two before.
5+8=13, 8+13=21, 13+21=34 ✓
questions.hint: Add the two numbers before the blank.

questions.q: If 5x + 3 = 2x + 18, what is 3x?
questions.hint: Solve for x first, then multiply by 3. Or find 3x directly.

questions.q: 'All the students who failed the test didn't study. Jake didn't study. Therefore Jake failed the test.' What is wrong?
options: Not studying is necessary but not sufficient for failing — other students might also not study and pass
We don't know if Jake is a student
questions.exp:
The statement says 'all who failed didn't study' — that means NOT studying is necessary for failing.
But it doesn't say everyone who doesn't study fails.
Jake could not study and still pass.
questions.hint: Does 'all failures didn't study' mean 'all non-studiers fail'? Think carefully about the direction.

questions.q: A graph shows that ice cream sales and shark attacks both increase in summer. A student concludes that ice cream causes shark attacks. What is the flaw?
options: Ice cream is not sold near beaches
Both are caused by a third factor (hot weather/summer) — correlation is not causation
Shark attacks only happen in winter
questions.exp:
Both increase in summer because of hot weather — not because one causes the other.
This is the classic 'confounding variable' or 'spurious correlation' error.
questions.hint: Is there a third factor that could cause both to increase at the same time?

questions.q:
Exactly one of these statements is true:
1. All students passed.
2. Some students passed.
3. No student passed.
Which statement(s) could be true?
options: Statements 1 and 2 could both be true, but exactly one is chosen
Statement 2 only — because if all passed, statement 2 is also true, so they can't both be 'exactly one true'
questions.exp:
If ALL students passed: both statements 1 AND 2 are true → violates 'exactly one true'.
If SOME (not all) passed: only statement 2 is true ✓
If NONE passed: only statement 3 is true ✓
So statement 2 alone OR statement 3 alone can each be the one true statement.
But the question asks which could be true: statement 2 (if some but not all passed).
questions.hint: If statement 1 is true, is statement 2 also true? What does that mean for 'exactly one'?

questions.q: In a reverse alphabet code: A=26, B=25, C=24... Z=1. What does the code 19-26-11-11-2 spell?
questions.hint: Count backwards from A=26 to find each letter.

questions.q: A rectangular pool is surrounded by a 2-metre wide path. The pool is 8m × 5m. What is the area of the path?
questions.exp:
Pool: 8×5=40 m²
Outer (pool + path): (8+4)×(5+4) = 12×9 = 108 m²
Path area: 108-40=68 m²
questions.hint: Outer rectangle = pool dimensions + path width on BOTH sides of each dimension.

questions.q: Looking down at a 3D shape from above, you see a circle. Looking from the side, you see a triangle. What is the shape?
questions.exp:
A cone viewed from above = circle.
A cone viewed from the side = triangle.
Sphere from side = circle (not triangle). Cylinder from above = circle, from side = rectangle.
questions.hint: Which 3D shape looks circular from above and triangular from the side?

questions.q: In a class of 30, 20 play sport, 15 study art, and every student does at least one. How many do BOTH?
questions.exp:
By inclusion-exclusion: Sport + Art - Both = Total
20 + 15 - Both = 30
Both = 35 - 30 = 5
questions.hint: Use: Sport + Art - Both = Total (every student does at least one).

questions.q:
Read: 'The committee voted 7-3 in favour, but the chairperson called for a second vote.'
