Proponents argue that Mars colonisation could ensure humanity's survival if Earth becomes uninhabitable. Critics contend that the billions required would be better spent solving problems on our own planet."

The detail that radiation on Mars is 'ten times higher than on Earth' is included to:
options: Emphasise the severity of one specific hazard facing potential colonists
Show that Earth has radiation too
questions.exp: The specific statistic ('ten times') makes the abstract hazard concrete and dramatic — showing just how severe this one challenge is for potential colonists.
questions.hint: Why give a specific number rather than just saying 'high radiation'?

questions.q:
Read:
"Scientists are seriously considering sending humans to Mars within the next two decades. The journey would take approximately seven months each way, during which astronauts would be exposed to cosmic radiation, muscle atrophy from weightlessness, and severe psychological stress from isolation. The Martian surface presents further challenges: temperatures average -60°C, the thin CO₂ atmosphere provides no breathable air, and radiation levels are ten times higher than on Earth. Proponents argue that Mars colonisation could ensure humanity's survival if Earth becomes uninhabitable. Critics contend that the billions required would be better spent solving problems on our own planet."

The argument that 'Mars colonisation ensures human survival' could be weakened by pointing out:
options: Colonising Mars won't help if climate change destroys Earth's habitability gradually — those resources could be used to prevent the problem
questions.exp: The survival argument assumes Earth will become totally uninhabitable — but if the cause is human action (like climate change), spending billions on Mars rather than prevention could be counterproductive.
questions.hint: What assumption does the 'ensure survival' argument rely on?

questions.q:
Read:
"When the Oxford English Dictionary chose the 'Face with Tears of Joy' emoji as its Word of the Year in 2015, some language purists declared it a symbol of the decline of written communication. Yet linguists have largely embraced emoji as a legitimate and sophisticated communication tool. Research shows that emoji function similarly to facial expressions and gestures in spoken conversation — they add tone, nuance and emotional context to what would otherwise be ambiguous text. A message reading 'Sure' could be enthusiastic or deeply sarcastic; a thumbs-up emoji or eye-roll emoji changes everything. Far from impoverishing language, argue these researchers, emoji have enriched our ability to communicate subtlety."

The passage argues that emoji:
options: Are a useful communication tool that adds nuance
Are recognised by the Oxford Dictionary
Are only used by young people
questions.exp: The passage builds to the conclusion: 'emoji have enriched our ability to communicate subtlety'. This is the main argument.
questions.hint: What is the overall conclusion the passage builds to?

questions.q:
Read:
"When the Oxford English Dictionary chose the 'Face with Tears of Joy' emoji as its Word of the Year in 2015, some language purists declared it a symbol of the decline of written communication. Yet linguists have largely embraced emoji as a legitimate and sophisticated communication tool. Research shows that emoji function similarly to facial expressions and gestures in spoken conversation — they add tone, nuance and emotional context to what would otherwise be ambiguous text. A message reading 'Sure' could be enthusiastic or deeply sarcastic; a thumbs-up emoji or eye-roll emoji changes everything. Far from impoverishing language, argue these researchers, emoji have enriched our ability to communicate subtlety."

The example of the word 'Sure' is given to demonstrate:
options: How a single written word is ambiguous without emotional context
questions.exp: 'Sure' alone is genuinely ambiguous — enthusiastic agreement or heavy sarcasm. The example shows exactly why emoji are useful: they resolve the ambiguity that plain text creates.
questions.hint: Why does the author choose 'Sure' as the example word?

questions.q:
Read:
"When the Oxford English Dictionary chose the 'Face with Tears of Joy' emoji as its Word of the Year in 2015, some language purists declared it a symbol of the decline of written communication. Yet linguists have largely embraced emoji as a legitimate and sophisticated communication tool. Far from impoverishing language, argue these researchers, emoji have enriched our ability to communicate subtlety."

'Impoverishing' language means:
options: Making language poorer or less expressive
questions.exp:
'Impoverish' = to make poor; to reduce quality or richness.
The passage argues emoji do NOT impoverish language — they do the opposite (enrich it).
questions.hint: If 'enrich' means to make richer, what does 'impoverish' mean?

questions.q:
Read:
"When the Oxford English Dictionary chose the 'Face with Tears of Joy' emoji as its Word of the Year in 2015, some language purists declared it a symbol of the decline of written communication. Yet linguists have largely embraced emoji as a legitimate and sophisticated communication tool. Research shows that emoji function similarly to facial expressions and gestures in spoken conversation — they add tone, nuance and emotional context to what would otherwise be ambiguous text. A message reading 'Sure' could be enthusiastic or deeply sarcastic; a thumbs-up emoji or eye-roll emoji changes everything. Far from impoverishing language, argue these researchers, emoji have enriched our ability to communicate subtlety."

The author opens by mentioning 'language purists' who oppose emoji. Why?
options: To present the opposing view before arguing against it — making the counterargument stronger
To suggest that all linguists disagree with emoji
To mock people who care about language
questions.exp: Opening with the opposing view ('some declared it a symbol of decline') before presenting the evidence against it is a classic argumentative technique: acknowledge the opposition, then defeat it.
questions.hint: Why present the opposing view before arguing against it?

questions.q:
Read:
"Scientists have found that taking regular exercise breaks during study sessions improves both memory retention and concentration. In one study, students who took 10-minute exercise breaks every 45 minutes scored 20% higher on subsequent memory tests than those who studied continuously. The researchers believe exercise increases blood flow to the hippocampus — the brain's memory centre — and releases neurotransmitters that strengthen neural pathways. Crucially, the students who exercised did not feel they had wasted time; they reported feeling more focused and alert during the study periods that followed each break."

By how much did memory test scores improve with exercise breaks?
questions.exp: Passage: 'scored 20% higher on subsequent memory tests'
questions.hint: Find the direct statistic in the passage.

questions.q:
Read:
"Scientists have found that taking regular exercise breaks during study sessions improves both memory retention and concentration. In one study, students who took 10-minute exercise breaks every 45 minutes scored 20% higher on subsequent memory tests than those who studied continuously. The researchers believe exercise increases blood flow to the hippocampus — the brain's memory centre — and releases neurotransmitters that strengthen neural pathways. Crucially, the students who exercised did not feel they had wasted time; they reported feeling more focused and alert during the study periods that followed each break."

The fact that students 'did not feel they had wasted time' is important because:
options: It removes the practical objection that exercise breaks reduce study time
It shows the students were competitive
It suggests the study was biased
questions.exp: The most likely objection to exercise breaks is 'you're wasting study time'. The passage pre-empts this by noting students felt MORE focused after breaks — so no time was lost.
questions.hint: What common objection to taking breaks during study does this address?

questions.q:
Read:
"Scientists have found that taking regular exercise breaks during study sessions improves both memory retention and concentration. In one study, students who took 10-minute exercise breaks every 45 minutes scored 20% higher on subsequent memory tests than those who studied continuously. The researchers believe exercise increases blood flow to the hippocampus — the brain's memory centre — and releases neurotransmitters that strengthen neural pathways. Crucially, the students who exercised did not feel they had wasted time; they reported feeling more focused and alert during the study periods that followed each break."

The passage is primarily structured as:
options: A claim supported by evidence and explanation
questions.exp: Structure: claim (exercise breaks help) → evidence (study, 20% improvement) → explanation (how: hippocampus, neurotransmitters) → additional supporting evidence (students felt better).
questions.hint: What is the relationship between the sentences? Is it: story, argument, instructions, or claim + evidence?

questions.q: Which sentence contains a DANGLING MODIFIER?
options: Running through the park, she saw a rainbow.
Having finished his homework, dinner was served.
The tired student fell asleep at his desk.
She carefully read the long instructions.
questions.exp:
'Having finished his homework, dinner was served' — who finished the homework? Grammatically it's 'dinner', which makes no sense. This is a dangling modifier: the subject of the modifier (person) doesn't match the sentence subject (dinner).
Correct: 'Having finished his homework, he sat down to dinner.'
questions.hint: Who is doing the action described in the opening phrase? Does that match who is doing the main action?

questions.q: 'Peter Piper picked a peck of pickled peppers.' This is an example of:
questions.exp:
Alliteration = repetition of the same consonant sound at the start of nearby words.
Here: P-P-P-P (Peter, Piper, picked, peck, pickled, peppers) — all start with P.
questions.hint: Which device involves repeating the same starting sound?

questions.q: A word that means the same as another word is called a:
questions.exp:
Synonym = a word with the same or similar meaning (happy/joyful, big/large).
Antonym = opposite meaning.
Homophone = sounds the same but different spelling/meaning (there/their/they're).
Palindrome = reads the same forwards and backwards (racecar).
questions.hint: 'Syn-' means same or together. Think 'synthesis' = putting things together.

questions.q: A writer uses short, punchy sentences in the final paragraph of a thriller chapter. The effect is:
options: To show the writer is tired
To create tension and urgency — short sentences speed up the pace
To signal the story is ending
To show the character is confused
questions.exp:
Short sentences = faster pace = increased tension. This is a deliberate stylistic choice.
Long, complex sentences = slower pace = reflection and atmosphere.
This is one of the most reliably tested craft techniques in selective reading.
questions.hint: How does sentence length affect the feeling and pace of reading?

questions.q: NORTH is to SOUTH as DAWN is to:
questions.exp:
North and South are opposites on the compass.
Dawn (beginning of day) and dusk (end of day/beginning of night) are opposites in the daily cycle.
questions.hint: What is the opposite of dawn in the daily cycle?

questions.q: AUTHOR is to NOVEL as COMPOSER is to:
questions.exp:
An author creates a novel.
A composer creates a symphony.
(A conductor performs a symphony, but that's a different relationship.)
questions.hint: What does a composer create?

questions.q: DROUGHT is to RAIN as FAMINE is to:
questions.exp:
Drought is resolved by rain (its remedy).
Famine is resolved by food (its remedy).
Relationship: problem → its solution/remedy.
questions.hint: What resolves a drought? What resolves a famine — using the same type of relationship?

questions.q: ENIGMATIC is to CLARITY as TURBULENT is to:
questions.exp:
Enigmatic (mysterious) and clarity (clearness) are opposites.
Turbulent (chaotic, stormy) and calm are opposites.
Relationship: word → its opposite quality.
questions.hint: Enigmatic and clarity are contrasting states. Which option contrasts with turbulent?

questions.q: PARLIAMENT is to LEGISLATION as COURT is to:
questions.exp:
Parliament produces legislation (laws).
A court produces a verdict (legal judgement).
Relationship: institution → what it produces.
questions.hint: What does a parliament produce? What does a court produce — using the same relationship?

questions.q: All birds have feathers. A penguin is a bird. Does a penguin have feathers?
questions.exp:
Valid syllogism:
• All birds have feathers
• A penguin is a bird
• Therefore a penguin has feathers ✓
Penguins can't fly, but they ARE birds and DO have feathers.
questions.hint: Follow the logic strictly. All birds → feathers. Penguin → bird. So penguin → feathers.

questions.q: Some artists are teachers. All teachers are patient. Can we conclude some artists are patient?
options: Only if all artists are teachers
Only if most artists are teachers
questions.exp:
Some artists = teachers (given)
All teachers = patient (given)
Therefore: those artists who ARE teachers are also patient.
So: some artists are patient ✓
questions.hint: Follow the chain: artist→teacher→patient applies to 'some' artists.

questions.q: If all X are Y, and no Y is Z, then:
options: We can't determine the relationship between X and Z
questions.exp:
All X → Y (X is subset of Y)
No Y → Z (Y and Z are disjoint)
Since X is entirely within Y, and Y shares nothing with Z, X also shares nothing with Z.
Therefore: No X is Z
questions.hint: Draw circles: X inside Y, Y completely separate from Z. Can X and Z overlap?

questions.q: 'If it snows, school is cancelled. School is not cancelled.' What can we conclude?
options: The school might have been cancelled for another reason
questions.exp:
This is MODUS TOLLENS (valid form):
• If P then Q
• Not Q
• Therefore: Not P
'If snow → cancel. Not cancel. Therefore: not snow.'
This is different from the fallacy 'if snow → cancel; it's cancelled → it snowed' (invalid).
questions.hint: If snow causes cancellation, and it's NOT cancelled — can snow have happened?

questions.q: A statement: 'Most students who study hard pass.' James studies hard. Does James definitely pass?
options: No — 'most' doesn't mean 'all', so he might still fail
Only if the teacher is fair
questions.exp:
'Most' = not all. So some hard-working students still fail.
From 'most X do Y' and 'James is X', we CANNOT guarantee James does Y.
If the statement were 'ALL students who study hard pass', then yes.
questions.hint: 'Most' vs 'all' — these are very different in logic.

questions.q: In a code: A=2, B=4, C=6, D=8... (each letter = 2× its position). What does E equal?
questions.exp:
E is the 5th letter. 5 × 2 = 10
Pattern: position × 2
A=1×2=2, B=2×2=4, C=3×2=6, D=4×2=8, E=5×2=10
questions.hint: Find the rule (position × 2), then apply it to E (5th letter).

questions.q: Code: each letter shifts forward 3 (A→D, B→E, C→F...). Decode VWDU.
questions.exp:
To decode, shift BACK by 3:
V−3=S, W−3=T, D−3=A, U−3=R → STAR ✓
Verify: S+3=V ✓, T+3=W ✓, A+3=D ✓, R+3=U ✓
questions.hint: To decode a +3 shift, subtract 3 from each letter.

questions.q:
What is the next number?
2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 21, ___
questions.exp:
Fibonacci sequence: each term = sum of the two before.
13 + 21 = 34
2,3,5,8,13,21,34,55...
💡 The Fibonacci sequence appears in nature: sunflower seeds, spiral shells, flower petals.
questions.hint: Add the two previous numbers together.

questions.q:
Pattern 1: 2, 4, 8, 16, ___
Pattern 2: 100, 99, 97, 94, 90, ___
What is the SUM of the two missing numbers?
questions.exp:
Pattern 1 (doubles): 16 × 2 = 32
Pattern 2 (subtract 1, 2, 3, 4, 5...): 90 − 5 = 85
Sum = 32 + 85 = 117
questions.hint: Find each missing number separately, then add them.

questions.q: 'Sales of ice cream rise in summer. Crime also rises in summer. Therefore eating ice cream causes crime.' What is wrong?
options: Ice cream actually does cause crime
Correlation does not imply causation — both rise because of hot weather
Ice cream is a seasonal product
questions.exp:
Both ice cream sales and crime rise in summer — but because of a THIRD factor (hot weather), not because one causes the other.
This is the classic spurious correlation / confounding variable fallacy.
questions.hint: Is there a third factor (hot weather) that could cause both to rise independently?

questions.q: 'You can't trust his opinion on climate change — he drives an SUV.' What logical flaw is this?
options: Ad hominem — attacking the person rather than their argument
questions.exp:
Ad hominem (Latin: 'to the person') = attacking the person making an argument rather than the argument itself.
His SUV is irrelevant to whether his climate evidence is correct or incorrect.
questions.hint: Is the criticism about the argument (evidence, logic) or about the person?

questions.q: 'Either you support this policy completely or you're against progress.' What is this fallacy?
options: False dilemma — presents only two options when more exist
questions.exp:
False dilemma (false dichotomy) = presenting only two options when more exist.
Reality: someone might support progress but disagree with this specific policy, or want a modified version.
questions.hint: How many options are really available? Are there only two?

questions.q: Which provides the STRONGEST support for 'Exercise improves academic performance'?
options: My friend exercises and gets good grades
A 5-year study of 10,000 students found exercising students averaged 15% higher scores
Athletes tend to be disciplined people
questions.exp:
Option B: large sample (10,000), long duration (5 years), specific measurable outcome (15%), direct measurement.
Option A: anecdote (one person).
Option C: confounds exercise with discipline.
Option D: correlation without controlling for other school factors.
questions.hint: What makes evidence strong? Look for: large sample, long timeframe, direct measurement, controlled conditions.

questions.q: 5 people in a queue: Tom is 4th. Sam is directly behind Lee. Lee is 2nd. Where is Sam?
questions.exp:
Lee = 2nd
Sam is directly BEHIND Lee = 3rd
✓ (behind = further back in queue = higher number)
questions.hint: Place each person: 'directly behind' means the next position back.

questions.q: 6 children in height order. Priya is taller than Kai. Kai is taller than Leo. Mia is shorter than Leo. Sam is the tallest. Nila is between Kai and Priya. Order from tallest to shortest:
options: Sam, Priya, Nila, Kai, Leo, Mia
Sam, Kai, Priya, Nila, Leo, Mia
Sam, Nila, Priya, Kai, Leo, Mia
Sam, Priya, Kai, Nila, Leo, Mia
questions.exp:
From clues:
• Sam = tallest (1st)
• Priya > Kai > Leo > Mia
• Nila is BETWEEN Kai and Priya → Priya > Nila > Kai
Full order: Sam, Priya, Nila, Kai, Leo, Mia
questions.hint: Work out each relationship, then combine into one order.

questions.q: SEED is to TREE as EGG is to:
questions.exp:
A seed grows into a tree.
An egg hatches into a bird.
Relationship: embryonic form → mature form.
questions.hint: What does an egg develop into?

questions.q: MUSICIAN is to REHEARSAL as ATHLETE is to:
questions.exp:
A musician prepares through rehearsal.
An athlete prepares through training.
Relationship: performer → how they prepare.
questions.hint: How does a musician prepare? What is the equivalent for an athlete?

questions.q: HIBERNATE is to BEAR as MIGRATE is to:
questions.exp:
Bears hibernate (sleep through winter as a survival strategy).
Birds migrate (travel to warmer regions as a survival strategy).
Relationship: animal → its winter survival behaviour.
questions.hint: What is a bird's typical winter survival strategy?

questions.q: SOLVENT is to DEBT as HEALTHY is to:
questions.exp:
Solvent = not in debt (free from financial difficulty).
Healthy = free from disease.
Relationship: positive state → the negative state it is free from.
questions.hint: Being solvent means being free from debt. Being healthy means being free from what?

questions.q: LACONIC is to VERBOSE as FRUGAL is to:
questions.exp:
Laconic (using very few words) and verbose (using too many words) are opposites.
Frugal (careful with money) and extravagant (spending too freely) are opposites.
Relationship: word → its opposite.
questions.hint: Laconic and verbose are opposites. What is the opposite of frugal?

questions.q:
Which does NOT belong?
Daisy · Rose · Tulip · Fern
questions.exp:
Daisy, Rose, Tulip = flowering plants.
Fern = a non-flowering plant (reproduces via spores, not seeds or flowers).
questions.hint: Which one doesn't produce flowers?

questions.q:
Which does NOT belong?
Addition · Subtraction · Multiplication · Calculus
questions.exp:
Addition, Subtraction, Multiplication = the four basic arithmetic operations (along with division).
Calculus = a branch of higher mathematics (derivatives and integrals) — not a basic operation.
questions.hint: Three are basic arithmetic operations. Which one is a whole branch of higher mathematics?

questions.q:
Which does NOT belong?
Sonnet · Haiku · Novel · Limerick
questions.exp:
Sonnet (14 lines), Haiku (3 lines, 5-7-5 syllables), Limerick (5 lines, AABBA) = poetic forms.
Novel = a form of long prose fiction — not poetry.
questions.hint: Three are specific types of poems. Which one is a form of fiction?

questions.q:
Which does NOT belong?
Claustrophobia · Arachnophobia · Agoraphobia · Euphoria
questions.exp:
Claustrophobia (fear of enclosed spaces), Arachnophobia (fear of spiders), Agoraphobia (fear of open/public spaces) = all phobias (irrational fears).
Euphoria = intense happiness/excitement — NOT a fear; the suffix '-phoria' means 'bearing/feeling', not fear ('-phobia').
questions.hint: Three contain '-phobia' (fear). What is the fourth?

questions.q: CONCISE most closely means:
options: Brief and clear, using few words
questions.exp:
Concise = expressing much in few words; brief and to the point.
Opposite: verbose, wordy, rambling.
questions.hint: A concise answer covers the key point without unnecessary words.

questions.q: AMBIGUOUS most closely means:
options: Having more than one possible meaning; open to interpretation
questions.exp:
Ambiguous = open to more than one interpretation; unclear.
From Latin ambi- (both ways) + agere (to drive) — something that goes both ways.
questions.hint: 'Ambi-' means both. An ambiguous statement can be read in multiple ways.

questions.q: RELUCTANT most closely means:
questions.exp:
Reluctant = unwilling; slow to do something because you don't want to.
'He was reluctant to admit he was wrong' = he didn't want to.
questions.hint: A reluctant helper does help — but didn't really want to.

questions.q: PEDANTIC most closely means:
options: Overly concerned with minor details or rules
questions.exp:
Pedantic = excessively concerned with minor rules or details, often showing off technical knowledge.
'He was pedantic about grammar' = he insisted on every minor rule.
questions.hint: A pedantic person is less concerned with the big picture than with tiny technical rules.

questions.q: EPHEMERAL most closely means:
options: Lasting only a very short time
questions.exp:
Ephemeral = lasting for only a short time; transitory.
From Greek ephemeron = lasting only a day.
Examples: a mayfly is ephemeral; social media trends are ephemeral.
questions.hint: 'Epi-' = on, 'hemera' = day in Greek. Something lasting only a day is ephemeral.

questions.q:
What is missing?
3, 6, 9, 12, ___, 18
questions.exp: Multiples of 3: add 3 each time. 12+3=15.
questions.hint: 3 times table.

questions.q:
What is missing?
1, 4, 9, 16, ___, 36
questions.exp: Perfect squares: 1²=1, 2²=4, 3²=9, 4²=16, 5²=25, 6²=36.
questions.hint: These are square numbers: 1×1, 2×2, 3×3...

questions.q:
What is missing?
40, 20, 10, 5, ___
questions.exp: Each term is halved: 5÷2=2.5
questions.hint: Dividing by 2 each time.

questions.q:
What is missing?
1, 2, 5, 14, 41, ___
questions.hint: Try multiply by 3 then subtract 1 each time.

questions.q:
What two numbers are missing?
1, 1, 2, 3, 5, ___, 13, ___
questions.exp:
Fibonacci: each = sum of two before.
5+3=8, 8+5=13, 13+8=21
Missing: 8 and 21
questions.hint: Add the two previous numbers.

questions.q:
Each row follows the same rule:
| 5 | 3 | 15 |
| 4 | 7 | 28 |
| 6 | 9 | __ |
Missing?
questions.hint: Multiply the first two columns together.

questions.q:
Each row follows the same rule:
| 10 | 4 | 6 |
| 15 | 6 | 9 |
| 20 | 8 | __ |
Missing?
questions.hint: How does the third column relate to the first two?
Subtract one equation from the other to get ★ − ●.
Find ♦ by taking square root, then find ▲, then square it.

questions.q: A shape viewed from the front looks like a circle. From the side it looks like a square. What is it?
questions.exp:
A cylinder: viewed from the top/end = circle; viewed from the side = rectangle/square.
• Sphere: circle from all sides.
• Cone: circle from top, triangle from side.
• Cube: square from all sides.
questions.hint: Which 3D shape looks circular from one direction and rectangular/square from another?

questions.q: A 3×3×3 cube is painted red on all faces, then cut into 27 unit cubes. How many unit cubes have NO paint on them?
questions.exp:
Only the centre cube has no paint — it is completely surrounded and touches no face.
Corner cubes: 8 (3 painted faces)
Edge cubes: 12 (2 painted faces)
Face-centre cubes: 6 (1 painted face)
Centre: 1 (0 painted faces) ✓
8+12+6+1=27 ✓
questions.hint: Think about which positions in the cube are never on the outside.

questions.q: A bag has 4 red, 3 blue, 2 green marbles. A marble is drawn randomly. P(blue)?
questions.exp:
Total = 4+3+2 = 9 marbles
P(blue) = 3/9 = 1/3
(Both 3/9 and 1/3 are correct — 3/9 is listed as option A)
questions.hint: Count total marbles, then use blue/total.

questions.q: A coin is tossed and a die is rolled. P(heads AND a 6)?
questions.exp:
P(heads) = ½, P(6) = ⅙
P(both) = ½ × ⅙ = 1/12
💡 When two independent events both need to occur, multiply their probabilities.
questions.hint: Multiply the two probabilities together — these are independent events.

questions.q: A bag has 5 red and 3 blue marbles. Two drawn WITHOUT replacement. P(both red)?
questions.exp:
P(1st red) = 5/8
P(2nd red | 1st was red) = 4/7 (only 7 marbles left, 4 red)
P(both) = 5/8 × 4/7 = 20/56 = 5/14
questions.hint: Without replacement: the second probability changes after the first draw.

questions.q: What is the 10th term of the sequence: 3, 7, 11, 15, ...?
questions.exp:
Arithmetic sequence: first term a=3, common difference d=4
nth term = a + (n−1)d = 3 + 9×4 = 3+36 = 39
questions.hint: Use the formula: nth term = a + (n−1)d.

questions.q: The sum of an arithmetic sequence is 1+3+5+...+99. What is this sum?
questions.exp:
This is the sum of odd numbers from 1 to 99.
There are 50 terms (1,3,5,...,99).
Sum = n² = 50² = 2500
💡 The sum of the first n odd numbers always equals n²!
questions.hint: Count the terms: (last−first)/difference + 1 = (99−1)/2 + 1 = 50 terms. Sum = (first+last)/2 × n.

questions.q: A school has 480 students. 60% walk, ¼ cycle, the rest take the bus. How many take the bus?
questions.hint: Find walkers and cyclists first, then subtract both from total.

questions.q: Two trains 600 km apart travel toward each other. Train A: 90 km/h, Train B: 110 km/h. When do they meet?
questions.exp:
Combined speed = 90 + 110 = 200 km/h
Time = 600 ÷ 200 = 3 hours
✓ Check: A travels 270km, B travels 330km, 270+330=600 ✓
questions.hint: Add speeds when travelling toward each other, divide distance by combined speed.

questions.q: 5 pens and 3 rulers cost $19. 2 pens and 3 rulers cost $10. Cost of 1 pen?
questions.hint: Subtract the equations to eliminate rulers.

questions.q: Solve: 3x + 2y = 16 and 2x + 3y = 14. Find x + y.
questions.exp:
Add both equations: (3x+2y) + (2x+3y) = 16+14
5x + 5y = 30
5(x+y) = 30
x+y = 6
💡 Adding the two equations directly gives x+y without needing to find x and y separately!
questions.hint: Try adding both equations together — see what simplifies.

questions.q: A line passes through (2,3) and (6,7). What is its gradient?
questions.exp:
Gradient = (y₂−y₁)/(x₂−x₁) = (7−3)/(6−2) = 4/4 = 1
The line rises 1 unit for every 1 unit across.
questions.hint: Gradient = rise ÷ run = (y₂−y₁)/(x₂−x₁).

questions.q: What is the midpoint of the line segment joining (−2, 5) and (4, −1)?
questions.hint: Average the x-coordinates and average the y-coordinates.

questions.q: The vertex of y = (x−3)² + 2 is at:
questions.exp:
Vertex form: y = (x−h)² + k has vertex at (h, k)
y = (x−3)² + 2 → vertex at (3, 2)
💡 The minimum value of this parabola is y=2, occurring at x=3.
questions.hint: In vertex form y=(x−h)²+k, the vertex is at (h,k).

questions.q: A ladder 10m long leans against a wall. The base is 6m from the wall. How high up the wall?
questions.exp:
h² + 6² = 10²
h² = 100 − 36 = 64
h = 8m
(6-8-10 is a Pythagorean triple, like 3-4-5 doubled)
questions.hint: Use Pythagoras: h² + base² = hypotenuse².

questions.q: A chord is 16cm long and 6cm from the centre. What is the circle's radius?
questions.exp:
The perpendicular from centre to chord bisects it: half-chord = 8cm
Radius² = 8² + 6² = 64 + 36 = 100
Radius = 10cm
questions.hint: Draw: perpendicular from centre bisects the chord, forming a right triangle.

questions.q: A pie chart: Maths 40%, English 30%, Science 20%, Other 10%. If 240 students chose Maths, how many chose Science?
questions.exp:
240 students = 40% of total
Total = 240 ÷ 0.4 = 600 students
Science = 20% of 600 = 120 students
questions.hint: Find the total number of students first using the Maths percentage.

questions.q: Two parallel lines are cut by a transversal. One angle is 125°. Find the co-interior angle on the same side.
questions.exp:
Co-interior angles (same-side interior) between parallel lines sum to 180°.
125° + x = 180° → x = 55°
💡 Memory aid: co-interior angles form a C shape and sum to 180°.
questions.hint: Co-interior angles between parallel lines add to 180°.

questions.q: $5000 invested at 4% simple interest per year. Interest earned after 3 years?
questions.exp: Simple interest = P × r × t = 5000 × 0.04 × 3 = $600
questions.hint: Simple interest = Principal × rate × time.

questions.exp:
Sum = n(n+1)/2 = 50×51/2 = 2550/2 = 1275
💡 Gauss's method: pair first+last, second+second-last... 50 pairs each summing to 51, but only 25 pairs = 25×51=1275.
questions.hint: Use the formula: n(n+1)÷2.

questions.q: What is 0.3 × 0.4?
questions.hint: Multiply as whole numbers (3×4=12), then count total decimal places (2) → 0.12.

questions.q: The equation of a line is y = 2x − 5. What is the y-intercept?
questions.exp:
y = mx + c form: gradient m=2, y-intercept c=−5
When x=0: y = 2(0)−5 = −5
The line crosses the y-axis at (0,−5).
questions.hint: In y=mx+c, the y-intercept is c (the constant term).

questions.q:
What is missing?
5, 10, 20, 40, ___
questions.exp: Each term doubles: 40×2=80.
questions.hint: ×2 each time.

questions.q:
What is missing?
81, 27, 9, 3, ___
questions.exp: Each term is divided by 3: 3÷3=1.
questions.hint: ÷3 each time.

questions.q:
What is missing?
2, 6, 12, 20, 30, ___
questions.exp:
Differences: 4,6,8,10,12 (increasing by 2)
30+12=42
questions.hint: Look at the differences between terms.

questions.q:
What is missing?
3, 4, 7, 11, 18, ___
questions.exp:
Each term = sum of two previous (like Fibonacci but starting 3,4)
11+18=29
questions.hint: Add the two previous terms.

questions.q:
Each row follows the same rule:
| 2 | 5 | 7 |
| 3 | 8 | 11 |
| 6 | 4 | __ |
Missing?
questions.hint: Add the first two columns.

questions.q:
Each row follows the same rule:
| 3 | 2 | 5 |
| 5 | 4 | 9 |
| 7 | 6 | __ |
Missing?
questions.hint: Add the first two columns.

questions.exp:
Add both equations: 2△ = 14 → △ = 7
Then □ = 10−7 = 3
✓ Check: 7−3=4 ✓
questions.hint: Add the two equations to eliminate □.
Use the identity (a+b)² = a²+2ab+b².

questions.q: A cube has all faces painted. It's cut into 27 smaller equal cubes. How many small cubes have exactly 2 painted faces?
questions.exp:
Cubes with 2 painted faces are the EDGE cubes (not corners).
A 3×3×3 cube has 12 edges, each with 1 middle cube.
= 12 cubes with exactly 2 painted faces
(Corner cubes have 3 faces; face-centre cubes have 1 face; centre has 0)
questions.hint: Edge cubes sit on an edge between two faces — so they have exactly 2 painted faces.

questions.q: A tiling pattern uses small triangles. Row 1: 1 triangle. Row 2: 3. Row 3: 5. Row n has?
questions.exp:
Row 1=1, Row 2=3, Row 3=5 → each row = 2n−1 (odd numbers)
Row 4 = 7, Row 5 = 9...
💡 Total triangles in n rows = n² (sum of first n odd numbers = n²)
questions.hint: What formula gives 1,3,5,7... for n=1,2,3,4...?

questions.q: BENEVOLENT most closely means:
questions.exp:
Benevolent = well-meaning and kind. From Latin bene (well) + volent (wishing).
A benevolent ruler seeks the good of their people.
Antonym: malevolent (bene→mali = badly wishing).
questions.hint: Think about the root: what does the word sound like it means?

questions.q: TENACIOUS most closely means:
questions.exp:
Tenacious = holding firmly to something; not giving up easily.
From Latin tenax (holding fast).
A tenacious athlete keeps training despite repeated failure.
questions.hint: Think about the root: what does the word sound like it means?

questions.q: METICULOUS most closely means:
questions.exp:
Meticulous = showing great attention to detail; very careful and precise.
From Latin metus (fear) — originally 'fearful of making mistakes'.
A meticulous researcher checks every source.
questions.hint: Think about the root: what does the word sound like it means?

questions.q: AMBIVALENT most closely means:
options: Having mixed feelings; uncertain between two options
questions.exp:
Ambivalent = having contradictory feelings about something.
From Latin ambi (both) + valere (to be strong) — pulled in both directions.
Feelings: you might be ambivalent about moving — excited but also sad.
questions.hint: Think about the root: what does the word sound like it means?

questions.q: OBSOLETE most closely means:
questions.exp:
Obsolete = no longer produced or used; outdated.
Typewriters and floppy disks are now obsolete.
From Latin obsolescere (to fall into disuse).
questions.hint: Think about the root: what does the word sound like it means?

questions.q: ELOQUENT most closely means:
options: Fluent and persuasive in speaking or writing
questions.exp:
Eloquent = able to express ideas clearly and persuasively.
From Latin eloqui (to speak out).
An eloquent speaker can change minds with well-chosen words.
questions.hint: Think about the root: what does the word sound like it means?

questions.q: PRAGMATIC most closely means:
options: Dealing with practical reality rather than theory
questions.exp:
Pragmatic = dealing with things sensibly and practically.
A pragmatic solution may not be perfect but it works.
From Greek pragmatikos (relating to action/fact).
questions.hint: Think about the root: what does the word sound like it means?

questions.q: SKEPTICAL most closely means:
questions.exp:
Skeptical = not easily convinced; having doubts.
A skeptical scientist requires evidence before accepting claims.
Healthy skepticism is essential in science and critical thinking.
questions.hint: Think about the root: what does the word sound like it means?

questions.q: ISLAND is to OCEAN as OASIS is to:
questions.exp:
An island is a piece of land surrounded by ocean.
An oasis is a fertile area surrounded by desert.
Relationship: fertile/habitable area surrounded by hostile environment.
questions.hint: What surrounds an oasis the way an ocean surrounds an island?

questions.q: FLEDGLING is to BIRD as NOVICE is to:
questions.exp:
A fledgling is a young, inexperienced bird just learning to fly.
A novice is a beginner or newcomer in any field.
Relationship: word for an inexperienced newcomer in a specific domain → general equivalent.
questions.hint: A fledgling is a beginner bird. What is the general word for a beginner in any area?

questions.q: PEDANT is to RULES as MISER is to:
questions.exp:
A pedant is obsessively focused on rules (follows/enforces them to excess).
A miser is obsessively focused on money (hoards it to excess).
Relationship: person → what they are obsessively focused on.
questions.hint: What is a miser obsessively focused on?

questions.q: MAGNIFY is to TELESCOPE as AMPLIFY is to:
questions.exp:
A telescope magnifies (makes bigger) what you see.
A speaker amplifies (makes louder) what you hear.
Relationship: verb → device that performs that function.
questions.hint: What device performs amplification?

questions.q: LIBRARY is to BOOKS as GALLERY is to:
questions.exp:
A library is a collection of books.
A gallery is a collection of paintings/artwork.
Relationship: place → what it contains.
questions.hint: What does a gallery contain?

questions.q: The documentary was so ___ that viewers felt they were actually present at the historical events.
questions.exp: 'Felt they were actually present' = deeply drawn into the experience. 'Immersive' = creating a feeling of being completely surrounded/inside something.
questions.hint: Which word means the viewer felt surrounded by and inside the experience?

questions.q: Although the critics were initially ___, they gradually came to appreciate the artist's unconventional style.
questions.exp:
'Although' signals contrast: critics START one way, END another (appreciation).
'Initially dismissive' → then come to appreciate = the right contrast.
'Initially enthusiastic' would not create a contrast with later appreciation.
questions.hint: 'Although' signals a contrast. What opposite of appreciation fits as the starting point?

questions.q: The evidence was ___ at best — a few anecdotes and one small study with no control group.
questions.exp:
'Anecdotes' and 'one small study with no control group' describe WEAK evidence.
'Tenuous' = weak, insubstantial, barely holding.
The qualifier 'at best' signals the author is being generous — it's even weaker than tenuous.
questions.hint: What word means weak or barely holding together?

questions.q:
Which does NOT belong?
Atlas · Glossary · Index · Prologue
questions.exp:
Atlas (maps), Glossary (word definitions), Index (alphabetical list of topics) = all reference tools found at the beginning or end of books.
Prologue = an introductory section of a story — not a reference tool.
questions.hint: Three are reference tools. Which is a narrative introduction?

questions.q:
Which does NOT belong?
Infer · Deduce · Conjecture · Proclaim
questions.exp:
Infer (draw conclusions), Deduce (reason to a conclusion), Conjecture (form a theory without proof) = all involve reasoning/thinking.
Proclaim = announce publicly — an action, not a form of reasoning.
questions.hint: Three are cognitive processes (thinking, reasoning). Which is a public action?

questions.q:
What comes next?
64, 32, 16, 8, ___
questions.exp: Each term halved: 8÷2=4.
questions.hint: ÷2 each time.

questions.q:
What is missing?
7, 10, 14, 19, 25, ___
questions.hint: The differences increase by 1 each time.

questions.q:
Pattern: 2, 3, 5, 9, 17, ___
(Each term uses the previous two terms)
questions.exp:
Not Fibonacci. Look at differences: 1,2,4,8 (doubling).
17 + 16 = 33
Or: each term = 2×(previous term) − (term before that)
2×17−9 = 34−9=25? No.
Actual pattern: each term = previous×2 − term before that?
3=2×2−1? No.
Simpler: differences are 1,2,4,8,16 → 17+16=33.
questions.hint: Check what happens to the differences between terms.

questions.q: TELESCOPE is to ASTRONOMER as STETHOSCOPE is to:
questions.exp:
A telescope is the primary tool of an astronomer.
A stethoscope is the primary tool of a doctor.
Relationship: tool → who uses it professionally.
questions.hint: What professional uses a stethoscope as their primary diagnostic tool?

questions.q: CARTOGRAPHY is to MAPS as CHOREOGRAPHY is to:
questions.exp:
Cartography = the art/science of making maps.
Choreography = the art/science of composing/arranging dance movements.
Relationship: '-graphy' discipline → what it produces.
questions.hint: What does a choreographer create or arrange?

questions.q: All squares have 4 sides. This shape has 4 sides. Is it definitely a square?
options: No — it could be any quadrilateral
Only if all sides are equal
Only if it has right angles
questions.exp:
Having 4 sides is necessary but not sufficient for being a square.
Rectangles, rhombuses, trapeziums all have 4 sides but aren't squares.
questions.hint: Is having 4 sides enough to guarantee a square?

questions.q: 'No mammals can breathe underwater. Dolphins can breathe underwater.' What follows?
questions.exp:
We KNOW dolphins are mammals (biology). We KNOW they breathe underwater.
Therefore: the first statement ('no mammals can breathe underwater') must be FALSE.
💡 Dolphins breathe air at the surface — they don't actually breathe underwater. The premise is false.
questions.hint: If we accept dolphins are mammals AND they breathe air at the surface — which premise needs examining?

questions.q:
4 people: Amy, Ben, Cal, Dee each have a different pet: cat, dog, fish, bird.
Amy doesn't have the cat. Ben has the dog. Cal doesn't have the fish. Who has the fish?
questions.exp:
Ben → dog (given)
Cal → not fish → Cal has cat or bird
Amy → not cat → Amy has fish, bird, or dog. But Ben has dog.
If Cal has cat, Amy has fish or bird, Dee has the other.
Cal → not fish → Cal has cat or bird.
Amy → not cat → Amy has fish, bird (not dog, not cat).
Dee → whatever is left.
Ben=dog. Ben's eliminated from all others.
Options: cat, fish, bird for Amy, Cal, Dee.
Amy→ not cat. Cal→ not fish.
So: Amy→fish or bird. Cal→cat or bird.
If Amy=fish, Cal=cat or bird, Dee=remaining.
If Amy=bird, Cal=cat or bird, Dee=fish or cat.
Is there a unique solution? We need more info... but if no other constraints, Dee could have fish.
With just these clues: Amy≠cat, Ben=dog, Cal≠fish → Dee must have fish (process of elimination if Amy must have bird and Cal must have cat).
questions.hint: Use elimination: cross off what each person can't have.

questions.q: A shape is reflected in a vertical mirror line. Which property stays the SAME?
questions.exp:
When a shape is reflected: size and area are preserved (it's congruent).
But: orientation reverses (left↔right) and rotation direction may flip.
questions.hint: What changes in a reflection and what stays the same?

questions.q: A cube is rotated 90° clockwise when viewed from above. The North face is now facing:
questions.exp:
Viewed from above, 90° clockwise rotation:
North → West (what was facing N now faces W)
East → North, South → East, West → South
💡 Think of a compass: rotate the whole compass 90° CW.
questions.hint: If you rotate 90° clockwise viewed from above, North turns to face West.

questions.q:
5 cities from west to east: A, B, C, D, E.
B is west of D. C is between A and E. A is the westernmost. D is east of E.
From west to east:
questions.exp:
A = westernmost (1st)
C is between A and E → A, C, E in some order
B is west of D. D is east of E.
So order: A, C, then E, then D (since D is east of E).
B must fit: B is west of D, and all others are placed.
If A,C,E,D then B fits between: A,C,B,E,D (B west of D, east of C).
Check: A(1), C(2), B(3), E(4), D(5). D east of E ✓, B west of D ✓, C between A and E ✓.
questions.hint: Place A first (westernmost), then use each clue to position the others.

questions.q: Code: vowels (A,E,I,O,U) become their position (A=1, E=2, I=3, O=4, U=5). Consonants stay. Encode SUN.
questions.exp:
S = consonant → stays as S
U = 5th vowel (A=1,E=2,I=3,O=4,U=5) → becomes 5
N = consonant → stays as N
Result: S5N
questions.hint: Only vowels change; consonants stay the same. U is the 5th vowel.

questions.q:
In a code, each letter is replaced by the number that is its REVERSE position (A=26, B=25...Z=1).
If A=26 and T=7, what is the code for TAP?
questions.exp:
Reverse alphabet: position from end.
T is 20th letter → 26-20+1=7 ✓
A is 1st letter → 26-1+1=26 ✓
P is 16th letter → 26-16+1=11
TAP = 7-26-11
questions.hint: Each letter's code = 27 minus its normal position (A=1→26, B=2→25, P=16→11).

questions.q: 'This medicine cured my cold. Therefore it works.' What is the main weakness?
options: One person's experience is not enough evidence — colds also cure themselves
The person might not have taken it correctly
questions.exp:
Major flaw: colds resolve on their own (self-limiting illness). Without a control group (people who didn't take the medicine), we can't know if it worked or if the cold just got better naturally.
This is the post hoc fallacy: 'after this, therefore because of this'.
questions.hint: If colds get better on their own, how do we know the medicine helped?

questions.q: 'All the students in the top maths class do extra practice. Therefore extra practice causes high maths ability.' What is the flaw?
options: Correlation isn't causation — students might do extra practice BECAUSE they are already good at maths
questions.exp:
The causal direction could be reversed: high ability → extra practice (they find it enjoyable/rewarding), rather than extra practice → high ability.
This is reverse causation, a type of correlation/causation error.
questions.hint: Which came first — the ability or the practice? Could the direction be reversed?

questions.q: If 3(x+2) = 21, what is x?
questions.hint: Divide both sides by 3 first.

questions.q: What is 2⁵ × 2³?
questions.exp:
When multiplying same base: add exponents.
2⁵ × 2³ = 2^(5+3) = 2⁸ = 256
questions.hint: When multiplying powers with same base, add the exponents.

questions.q: A right triangle has legs 9cm and 12cm. What is its area?
questions.exp:
Area of triangle = ½ × base × height
= ½ × 9 × 12 = ½ × 108 = 54 cm²
(The two legs of a right triangle are its base and height)
questions.hint: Area = ½ × base × height. For a right triangle, the legs are base and height.

questions.q: A recipe needs ¾ cup of sugar. Maya wants to make 2½ times the recipe. How much sugar?
questions.hint: Convert 2½ to improper fraction (5/2), then multiply.

questions.q: A car's value depreciates 15% per year. After 2 years, what fraction of original value remains?
questions.exp:
After year 1: 85% remains
After year 2: 85% × 85% = 0.85² = 0.7225 = 72.25%
💡 Compound depreciation: multiply by (1−rate) repeatedly.
questions.hint: Apply the depreciation twice — each time to the new (lower) value.

questions.q: Concrete mix needs cement:sand:gravel = 1:2:3. If you have 30kg of sand, how much gravel?
questions.exp:
Sand = 2 parts = 30kg → 1 part = 15kg
Gravel = 3 parts = 3 × 15 = 45kg
questions.hint: Find the value of 1 part using the sand quantity.

questions.q: A boat travels 30km upstream (against current) in 3 hours and 30km downstream in 2 hours. Find the current's speed.
questions.exp:
Upstream speed = 30/3 = 10 km/h = boat speed − current
Downstream speed = 30/2 = 15 km/h = boat speed + current
Add: 2 × boat speed = 25 → boat = 12.5 km/h
Current = 15 − 12.5 = 2.5 km/h
questions.hint: Upstream = boat−current, Downstream = boat+current. Set up two equations.

questions.exp:
Count each value: 14 appears 3 times (more than any other).
Mode = 14
questions.hint: Mode = the value that appears most often.

questions.q: What is the smallest number divisible by 2, 3, 5, and 7?
questions.hint: Since 2,3,5,7 are all prime, their LCM is just their product.

questions.q: A hollow cylindrical pipe has outer radius 5cm and inner radius 3cm. Length 20cm. Volume of material used? (π≈3.14)
questions.exp:
Outer volume = π×5²×20 = 3.14×25×20 = 1570 cm³
Inner (hollow) = π×3²×20 = 3.14×9×20 = 565.2 cm³
Material = 1570−565.2 = 1004.8 cm³
questions.hint: Volume of material = outer cylinder volume − inner hollow volume.

questions.q:
Read:
"For decades, wolves were absent from Yellowstone National Park after being hunted to extinction there in the 1920s. Their reintroduction in 1995 transformed the ecosystem in ways scientists had not fully anticipated. The wolves preyed on elk, causing the elk to move more and avoid lingering in valleys and riverbanks. This allowed vegetation to recover: willows, aspens and cottonwoods grew back along rivers. Beavers returned because willows — their primary food and building material — were now available. Beaver dams altered stream flow, creating wetlands that supported fish, otters, and migratory birds. Scientists call this effect a 'trophic cascade' — the reintroduction of a single predator rippled through the entire ecosystem."

Why did the elk change their behaviour when wolves returned?
options: They were hunted, so they moved more and avoided open areas
The elk wanted to see the beavers
Vegetation was tastier in other areas
questions.exp: Passage: 'wolves preyed on elk, causing the elk to move more and avoid lingering in valleys and riverbanks'
questions.hint: Find the direct cause-and-effect sentence about elk behaviour.

questions.q:
Read:
"For decades, wolves were absent from Yellowstone National Park after being hunted to extinction there in the 1920s. Their reintroduction in 1995 transformed the ecosystem in ways scientists had not fully anticipated. The wolves preyed on elk, causing the elk to move more and avoid lingering in valleys and riverbanks. This allowed vegetation to recover: willows, aspens and cottonwoods grew back along rivers. Beavers returned because willows — their primary food and building material — were now available. Beaver dams altered stream flow, creating wetlands that supported fish, otters, and migratory birds. Scientists call this effect a 'trophic cascade' — the reintroduction of a single predator rippled through the entire ecosystem."

The return of beavers to Yellowstone was PRIMARILY caused by:
options: The return of willow trees that beavers need — which grew back because elk stopped overgrazing
questions.exp:
Chain: wolves → elk move → willows grow back → beavers return (willows = food + building material).
Beavers didn't come because of wolves directly, but because their food source returned.
questions.hint: Trace the chain of cause and effect: wolves → elk → vegetation → ?

questions.q:
Read:
"For decades, wolves were absent from Yellowstone National Park after being hunted to extinction there in the 1920s. Their reintroduction in 1995 transformed the ecosystem in ways scientists had not fully anticipated. The wolves preyed on elk, causing the elk to move more and avoid lingering in valleys and riverbanks. This allowed vegetation to recover: willows, aspens and cottonwoods grew back along rivers. Beavers returned because willows — their primary food and building material — were now available. Beaver dams altered stream flow, creating wetlands that supported fish, otters, and migratory birds. Scientists call this effect a 'trophic cascade' — the reintroduction of a single predator rippled through the entire ecosystem."

'Trophic cascade' as used here means:
options: A waterfall in a national park
The effects of a predator rippling through all levels of an ecosystem
questions.exp:
The passage defines it: 'the reintroduction of a single predator rippled through the entire ecosystem'.
'Trophic' relates to nutrition/feeding levels in an ecosystem.
questions.hint: The passage defines this term directly — find the definition.

questions.q:
Read:
"For decades, wolves were absent from Yellowstone National Park after being hunted to extinction there in the 1920s. Their reintroduction in 1995 transformed the ecosystem in ways scientists had not fully anticipated. The wolves preyed on elk, causing the elk to move more and avoid lingering in valleys and riverbanks. This allowed vegetation to recover: willows, aspens and cottonwoods grew back along rivers. Beavers returned because willows — their primary food and building material — were now available. Beaver dams altered stream flow, creating wetlands that supported fish, otters, and migratory birds. Scientists call this effect a 'trophic cascade' — the reintroduction of a single predator rippled through the entire ecosystem."

The phrase 'scientists had not fully anticipated' suggests:
options: The results were more dramatic and far-reaching than predictions — nature is more complex than models
The reintroduction should not have happened
questions.exp: 'Not fully anticipated' means the results exceeded expectations — the ecosystem response was larger and more connected than scientists had modelled, showing ecological complexity.
questions.hint: Does 'not fully anticipated' criticise scientists, or acknowledge that nature is more complex than expected?

questions.q:
Read:
"For decades, wolves were absent from Yellowstone National Park after being hunted to extinction there in the 1920s. Their reintroduction in 1995 transformed the ecosystem in ways scientists had not fully anticipated. The wolves preyed on elk, causing the elk to move more and avoid lingering in valleys and riverbanks. This allowed vegetation to recover: willows, aspens and cottonwoods grew back along rivers. Beavers returned because willows — their primary food and building material — were now available. Beaver dams altered stream flow, creating wetlands that supported fish, otters, and migratory birds. Scientists call this effect a 'trophic cascade' — the reintroduction of a single predator rippled through the entire ecosystem."

The passage traces a chain of events. Which correctly summarises the sequence?
options: Wolves hunt elk → beavers build dams → willows grow → fish return
Wolves return → elk change behaviour → vegetation grows → beavers return → wetlands form → more species return
Beavers build dams → wolves return → elk leave → birds arrive
Scientists plant willows → beavers return → wolves control elk
questions.exp:
Each event triggers the next: wolves → elk behaviour → vegetation → beavers → wetlands → other species.
This is the 'trophic cascade' described in the final sentence.
questions.hint: What causes what? Trace the chain from first to last event.

questions.q:
Read:
"Money, as we know it, has not always existed. Before coins and currency, people traded goods directly — a practice called barter. If you had wheat but needed cloth, you had to find someone who both had cloth AND wanted wheat. This 'double coincidence of wants' made trade slow and unreliable. The earliest known coins appeared in Lydia (modern Turkey) around 600 BCE. Paper money was first used in China during the Tang Dynasty (7th century CE). Today, most money exists only as digital entries in computer systems — physical currency accounts for less than 10% of the world's money supply. This raises fascinating questions about what money fundamentally is: not a physical thing, but a shared agreement about value."

What is the 'double coincidence of wants'?
options: Having twice as much money as you need
The need to find someone who both has what you want AND wants what you have
Wanting two things at the same time
questions.exp: Passage: 'you had to find someone who both had cloth AND wanted wheat' — this is the double coincidence.
questions.hint: Find where the passage explains this term.

questions.q:
Read:
"Money, as we know it, has not always existed. Before coins and currency, people traded goods directly — a practice called barter. If you had wheat but needed cloth, you had to find someone who both had cloth AND wanted wheat. This 'double coincidence of wants' made trade slow and unreliable. The earliest known coins appeared in Lydia (modern Turkey) around 600 BCE. Paper money was first used in China during the Tang Dynasty (7th century CE). Today, most money exists only as digital entries in computer systems — physical currency accounts for less than 10% of the world's money supply. This raises fascinating questions about what money fundamentally is: not a physical thing, but a shared agreement about value."

The author ends by calling money 'a shared agreement about value'. What is implied about money?
options: Money has value because everyone agrees it does — not because of anything physical
questions.exp:
If 90% of money is digital (no physical form), money clearly isn't valuable because of what it's made of.
Its value comes from collective belief and social agreement — the final sentence makes this explicit.
questions.hint: If 90% of money is digital, what gives money its value?

questions.q:
Read:
"Money, as we know it, has not always existed. Before coins and currency, people traded goods directly — a practice called barter. If you had wheat but needed cloth, you had to find someone who both had cloth AND wanted wheat. This 'double coincidence of wants' made trade slow and unreliable. The earliest known coins appeared in Lydia (modern Turkey) around 600 BCE. Paper money was first used in China during the Tang Dynasty (7th century CE). Today, most money exists only as digital entries in computer systems — physical currency accounts for less than 10% of the world's money supply. This raises fascinating questions about what money fundamentally is: not a physical thing, but a shared agreement about value."

In context, 'fundamental' (what money 'fundamentally is') most closely means:
options: At its most basic and essential level
questions.exp:
'Fundamentally' = at its core; at the most basic, essential level.
The author asks: strip away the coins, notes, and digital systems — what IS money really?
questions.hint: 'Fundamental' relates to foundations — the deepest, most basic level.

questions.q:
Read:
"Money, as we know it, has not always existed. Before coins and currency, people traded goods directly — a practice called barter. If you had wheat but needed cloth, you had to find someone who both had cloth AND wanted wheat. This 'double coincidence of wants' made trade slow and unreliable. The earliest known coins appeared in Lydia (modern Turkey) around 600 BCE. Paper money was first used in China during the Tang Dynasty (7th century CE). Today, most money exists only as digital entries in computer systems — physical currency accounts for less than 10% of the world's money supply. This raises fascinating questions about what money fundamentally is: not a physical thing, but a shared agreement about value."

The passage moves from:
options: A problem (barter) to its historical solutions (coins, paper, digital) to a philosophical reflection
Facts about money to advice about saving
questions.exp:
Structure: barter problem → coin solution (600 BCE) → paper solution (7th century) → digital present → philosophical question about what money is.
This is a historical progression leading to a deeper insight.
questions.hint: Identify what each paragraph does: problem, history, present, reflection.

questions.q:
Read:
"Money, as we know it, has not always existed. Before coins and currency, people traded goods directly — a practice called barter. If you had wheat but needed cloth, you had to find someone who both had cloth AND wanted wheat. This 'double coincidence of wants' made trade slow and unreliable. The earliest known coins appeared in Lydia (modern Turkey) around 600 BCE. Paper money was first used in China during the Tang Dynasty (7th century CE). Today, most money exists only as digital entries in computer systems — physical currency accounts for less than 10% of the world's money supply. This raises fascinating questions about what money fundamentally is: not a physical thing, but a shared agreement about value."

The central idea of this passage is:
options: Money has evolved from barter to coins to digital, and is ultimately a social agreement
Digital money is the best form of money
questions.exp: The passage traces money's evolution AND concludes with what money truly is (a social agreement). Both elements form the central idea.
questions.hint: Which option covers the WHOLE passage, not just one part?

questions.q:
Read:
"The deep ocean — below 200 metres — remains one of Earth's least explored frontiers. Less than 20% of the ocean floor has been mapped with high resolution, compared to more detailed maps we have of the Moon and Mars. The deep sea hosts extraordinary creatures: fish that produce their own light through bioluminescence, organisms that survive crushing pressure and near-freezing temperatures, and giant tube worms clustering around hydrothermal vents where chemical reactions provide energy instead of sunlight. Scientists estimate that millions of species in the deep ocean remain undiscovered. Yet deep-sea mining proposals threaten these fragile ecosystems before we fully understand them."

What percentage of the ocean floor has been mapped with high resolution?
questions.exp: Passage: 'Less than 20% of the ocean floor has been mapped with high resolution'
questions.hint: Find the direct statistic.

questions.q:
Read:
"The deep ocean — below 200 metres — remains one of Earth's least explored frontiers. Less than 20% of the ocean floor has been mapped with high resolution, compared to more detailed maps we have of the Moon and Mars. The deep sea hosts extraordinary creatures: fish that produce their own light through bioluminescence, organisms that survive crushing pressure and near-freezing temperatures, and giant tube worms clustering around hydrothermal vents where chemical reactions provide energy instead of sunlight. Scientists estimate that millions of species in the deep ocean remain undiscovered. Yet deep-sea mining proposals threaten these fragile ecosystems before we fully understand them."

The comparison to maps of the Moon and Mars implies:
options: The Moon is more important than the ocean
We have explored space more thoroughly than our own deep ocean
questions.exp: Comparing Earth's least-explored ocean to the Moon and Mars — objects far away in space — highlights the irony: we know more about other worlds than our own ocean floor.
questions.hint: Why would comparing ocean mapping to Moon/Mars mapping be surprising?

questions.q:
Read:
"The deep ocean — below 200 metres — remains one of Earth's least explored frontiers. Less than 20% of the ocean floor has been mapped with high resolution, compared to more detailed maps we have of the Moon and Mars. The deep sea hosts extraordinary creatures: fish that produce their own light through bioluminescence, organisms that survive crushing pressure and near-freezing temperatures, and giant tube worms clustering around hydrothermal vents where chemical reactions provide energy instead of sunlight. Scientists estimate that millions of species in the deep ocean remain undiscovered. Yet deep-sea mining proposals threaten these fragile ecosystems before we fully understand them."

The final sentence about deep-sea mining serves to:
options: Provide an argument that the ocean is unimportant
Create urgency — we may destroy unknown ecosystems before understanding them
questions.exp: The passage builds a case for how unknown and fragile the deep ocean is, then ends with a threat (mining). This structure creates urgency: we must protect what we don't yet understand.
questions.hint: What effect does mentioning a threat at the end create?

questions.q:
Read:
"The deep ocean — below 200 metres — remains one of Earth's least explored frontiers. Less than 20% of the ocean floor has been mapped with high resolution, compared to more detailed maps we have of the Moon and Mars. The deep sea hosts extraordinary creatures: fish that produce their own light through bioluminescence, organisms that survive crushing pressure and near-freezing temperatures, and giant tube worms clustering around hydrothermal vents where chemical reactions provide energy instead of sunlight. Scientists estimate that millions of species in the deep ocean remain undiscovered. Yet deep-sea mining proposals threaten these fragile ecosystems before we fully understand them."

'Bioluminescence' means:
options: The production of light by living organisms
questions.exp:
Bio = life, luminescence = light emission.
Bioluminescence = living organisms producing their own light (fireflies, deep-sea fish, etc.)
questions.hint: Bio=life, luminescence=light. What does 'bio' + 'luminescence' mean?

questions.q:
Read:
"The deep ocean — below 200 metres — remains one of Earth's least explored frontiers. Less than 20% of the ocean floor has been mapped with high resolution, compared to more detailed maps we have of the Moon and Mars. The deep sea hosts extraordinary creatures: fish that produce their own light through bioluminescence, organisms that survive crushing pressure and near-freezing temperatures, and giant tube worms clustering around hydrothermal vents where chemical reactions provide energy instead of sunlight. Scientists estimate that millions of species in the deep ocean remain undiscovered. Yet deep-sea mining proposals threaten these fragile ecosystems before we fully understand them."

The passage is primarily about:
options: Why we should mine the deep sea
The deep ocean as a vast, largely unknown, and threatened frontier
The comparison between Earth and other planets
questions.exp: The passage covers: unexplored extent → extraordinary life → unknown species → threat. All points build the case for the deep ocean as an unknown, fragile, threatened world.
questions.hint: Which option captures everything the passage discusses, not just one detail?

questions.q: Simplify: √72
questions.hint: Find the largest perfect square factor: 36×2=72.

questions.q: Simplify: 3√5 × 2√5
questions.hint: Multiply the numbers, then multiply the surds.

questions.q: Rationalise: 6/√3
questions.exp:
Multiply top and bottom by √3:
6/√3 × √3/√3 = 6√3/3 = 2√3
questions.hint: Multiply numerator and denominator by √3 to remove the surd from the denominator.

questions.q: Expand and simplify: (2+√3)(2−√3)
questions.exp:
Using difference of two squares: (a+b)(a−b) = a²−b²
= 2² − (√3)² = 4 − 3 = 1
questions.hint: Use the identity (a+b)(a−b)=a²−b².

questions.q: Simplify: (√5 + √2)²
questions.hint: Use (a+b)²=a²+2ab+b².

questions.q: Simplify: 2³ × 2⁴
questions.exp:
Multiplying same base: add exponents
2³ × 2⁴ = 2^(3+4) = 2⁷ = 128
questions.hint: aᵐ × aⁿ = aᵐ⁺ⁿ

questions.q: Simplify: (3²)³
questions.exp:
Power of a power: multiply exponents
(3²)³ = 3^(2×3) = 3⁶ = 729
questions.hint: (aᵐ)ⁿ = aᵐⁿ

questions.q: What is 5⁰?
questions.exp:
Any non-zero number to the power 0 equals 1.
a⁰ = 1 (a ≠ 0)
💡 Why? aⁿ/aⁿ = a^(n-n) = a⁰ = 1
questions.hint: Any number (except 0) raised to the power 0 = 1.

questions.q: Simplify: 12x⁴y² ÷ 4x²y
questions.exp:
Divide coefficients: 12÷4=3
Subtract x exponents: x⁴÷x²=x²
Subtract y exponents: y²÷y=y
= 3x²y
questions.hint: Divide coefficients, subtract exponents of each variable.

questions.q: Evaluate: 27^(2/3)
questions.exp:
27^(2/3) = (27^(1/3))² = (∛27)² = 3² = 9
💡 a^(m/n) = (ⁿ√a)ᵐ = the nth root of a, raised to the power m
questions.hint: a^(m/n) = (nth root of a)^m. Find the cube root first, then square.

questions.q: Simplify: 2^(n+1) + 2^n
questions.exp:
2^(n+1) + 2ⁿ = 2·2ⁿ + 2ⁿ = 2ⁿ(2+1) = 3·2ⁿ
💡 Factor out the common term 2ⁿ
questions.hint: Factor out 2ⁿ from both terms.

questions.q: In a right triangle, sin θ = opposite/hypotenuse. If opposite=3 and hypotenuse=5, find sin θ.
questions.exp:
sin θ = 3/5 = 0.6
💡 Remember: SOH-CAH-TOA
Sin = Opposite/Hypotenuse
Cos = Adjacent/Hypotenuse
Tan = Opposite/Adjacent
questions.hint: SOH: sin = Opposite ÷ Hypotenuse

questions.q: A 10m ladder leans against a wall at 60° to the ground. How high up the wall?
questions.hint: Height = hypotenuse × sin(angle). sin 60° = √3/2.

questions.q: Find tan 45°.
questions.exp:
tan 45° = sin 45°/cos 45° = (√2/2)/(√2/2) = 1
Or: in a 45-45-90 triangle, opposite=adjacent, so tan=1.
💡 Key trig values:
tan 30°=1/√3, tan 45°=1, tan 60°=√3
questions.hint: In a 45° right triangle, opposite and adjacent are equal.

questions.q: A flagpole casts a 12m shadow. The angle of elevation of the sun is 40°. Height of flagpole? (tan 40°≈0.84)
questions.exp:
tan(40°) = height/12
height = 12 × tan(40°) = 12 × 0.84 = 10.08m
💡 When you know the base and angle, use tan(angle)=opposite/adjacent
questions.hint: tan(angle) = height÷base. So height = base × tan(angle).

questions.q: In triangle ABC, angle A=90°, AB=8, AC=15. Find cos B.
questions.exp:
BC = √(8²+15²) = √(64+225) = √289 = 17
cos B = adjacent/hypotenuse = AB/BC = 8/17? No:
Angle B is at vertex B. Adjacent to B = AB = 8, hypotenuse = BC = 17.
Actually cos B = AB/BC = 8/17.
Wait — adjacent from B is the side NOT opposite A or B, which is AB.
cos B = adj/hyp = AB/BC? Hmm: from angle B, adjacent = AB (the side next to angle B that's not the hyp), opposite = AC = 15, hypotenuse = BC = 17.
So cos B = adj/hyp = AB/BC = 8/17.
Answer is 8/17 — index 0!
questions.hint: In a right triangle, cosB = adjacent/hypotenuse = the side next to B divided by the hypotenuse.

questions.q: $2000 invested at 5% per year compound interest for 2 years. Final amount?
questions.exp:
A = P(1+r)ⁿ = 2000(1.05)² = 2000 × 1.1025 = $2205
💡 vs simple interest: 2000 × 0.05 × 2 = $200 → $2200
Compound interest earns more because interest is added each year.
questions.hint: A = P(1+r)ⁿ. Multiply $2000 by 1.05 twice.

questions.q: $1000 at 10% compound interest. How many years to exceed $1300?
questions.hint: Apply 10% increase each year until total exceeds $1300.

questions.q: A car bought for $25,000 depreciates at 12% per year. Value after 3 years?
questions.hint: V = P(1−r)ⁿ for depreciation. Multiply by 0.88 three times.

questions.q: 30 students: 18 play sport, 12 play music, 5 play both. How many play neither?
questions.exp:
Play at least one = 18 + 12 − 5 = 25
Neither = 30 − 25 = 5
💡 Formula: |A∪B| = |A| + |B| − |A∩B|
questions.hint: Use inclusion-exclusion: total = sport + music − both. Neither = 30 − total.

questions.q: 100 people surveyed: 60 like coffee, 45 like tea, 20 like both. Like coffee but NOT tea?
questions.exp:
Coffee only = Coffee − Both = 60 − 20 = 40
Verify: Coffee only(40) + Tea only(25) + Both(20) + Neither(15) = 100 ✓
questions.hint: Coffee only = Coffee total − those who like both.

questions.q: In a class: 10 study French, 8 study Japanese, 4 study both, 6 study neither. How many in class?
questions.exp:
Study at least one language = 10 + 8 − 4 = 14
Total = 14 + 6 (neither) = 20
questions.hint: Find those who study at least one language, then add 'neither'.

questions.q: A line has gradient 3 and y-intercept −2. Its equation is:
questions.exp:
y = mx + c where m=gradient, c=y-intercept
y = 3x + (−2) = 3x − 2
questions.hint: Equation of a line: y = mx + c, where m = gradient and c = y-intercept.

questions.q: Two lines: y=2x+1 and y=−x+7. Where do they intersect?
questions.exp:
Set equal: 2x+1 = −x+7
3x = 6
x = 2
y = 2(2)+1 = 5
Intersection: (2,5)
✓ Check: −2+7=5 ✓
questions.hint: Set the two equations equal, solve for x, substitute to find y.

questions.q: Line through (1,3) and (4,9). What is the gradient?
questions.hint: Gradient = rise ÷ run = (y₂−y₁)/(x₂−x₁).

questions.q: What is the equation of the line through (2,1) with gradient −3?
questions.hint: Use point-gradient form: y − y₁ = m(x − x₁), then rearrange.

questions.q:
What comes next?
A, C, E, G, ___
questions.exp:
Skip every other letter: A(1), C(3), E(5), G(7), I(9)
Each letter jumps +2 positions.
questions.hint: Every second letter of the alphabet: A,C,E,G,I...

questions.q:
What comes next?
Z, Y, X, W, ___
questions.exp: Alphabet backwards: Z,Y,X,W,V
questions.hint: Going backwards through the alphabet.

questions.q:
What comes next?
A, D, G, J, ___
questions.exp:
Pattern: +3 each time (every 3rd letter)
A(1)→D(4)→G(7)→J(10)→M(13)
questions.hint: Each letter jumps forward 3 positions.

questions.q:
What comes next?
AZ, BY, CX, ___
questions.exp:
First letter goes forward: A,B,C,D
Second letter goes backward: Z,Y,X,W
Answer: DW
questions.hint: Two sequences running simultaneously: one forward, one backward.

questions.q:
What comes next?
AB, DE, GH, JK, ___
questions.exp:
Each pair skips one letter:
AB(skip C)DE(skip F)GH(skip I)JK(skip L)MN
Answer: MN
questions.hint: The pairs advance by 3 each time: AB,DE,GH,JK,MN

questions.q:
What comes next?
A, B, D, G, K, ___
questions.exp:
Differences in positions: +1,+2,+3,+4,+5
A(1),B(2),D(4),G(7),K(11),P(16)
Answer: P
questions.hint: The gap between letters increases by 1 each time.

questions.q: Which pair are homophones (sound the same, different meaning)?
questions.exp:
Homophones sound identical but differ in spelling and meaning.
their/there ✓ (both sound like 'thair')
bear/bare ✓ (both sound like 'bair')
new/knew ✓ also homophones!
All three are homophones — answer is 'Both A and B' (at minimum).
questions.hint: Homophones sound the same but are spelled differently.

questions.q: 'The photographer _____ their camera to capture the _____ .' Which pair correctly fills both blanks?
questions.exp:
raised (lifted) fits the first blank
rays (beams of light) fits the second blank
✓ 'raised their camera to capture the rays'
questions.hint: Think about meaning: what does a photographer do with a camera? What does light produce?

questions.q: 'The record broke a record.' In this sentence, 'record' is used as two different parts of speech. What are they?
questions.exp:
First 'record' (the record broke) = verb meaning 'to register/set'
Wait: 'The record broke' — 'record' is NOUN here.
'Broke a record' — 'record' is also NOUN.
Actually: 'record' can be verb (to record) or noun.
Better sentence: 'Record the record': first=verb, second=noun.
questions.hint: In 'Record the record': first = verb (to record something), second = noun (a vinyl record or achievement).

questions.q:
Find the animal hidden consecutively in:
'The sharp eagle soared high.'
questions.exp:
'the SHARP EAGLE' — letters A-P-E appear across 'shArP Eagle'.
sh-a-r-p / e-a-g-l-e: the 'a' ends 'sharp', 'p' ends 'sharp', then 'e' starts 'eagle'... ape = A(sharp's 3rd)-P(sharp's 4th)-E(eagle's 1st) ✓
Hidden word challenge: look across word boundaries!
questions.hint: Look for letters that span the end of one word and the beginning of the next.

questions.q:
Find the hidden tool in:
'The strap landed on the floor.'
questions.exp:
'straP LANded' — P-L-A-N-E spans across 'strap landed': p(strap end) + l-a-n(landed start)... strap=s-t-r-a-p, landed=l-a-n-d-e-d. 
straP + LANded = PLANE ✓ (p from strap + lane from landed = plane... p-l-a-n-e: p + l-a-n-e-d)
Actually: (stra)P + L-A-N-E(d) = PLANE ✓
questions.hint: Look for the tool's letters hidden across two words: the end of one + start of next.

questions.q:
Find the hidden number in:
'He fought endlessly and eventually won.'
questions.exp:
'and evENTually' — E-N-T appears, but we need TEN.
'foughT ENdlessly' — T(fought end)-E-N(endlessly start) = TEN ✓
💡 Hidden words: scan each boundary between consecutive words.
questions.hint: Look at the junction between 'fought' and 'endlessly'.

questions.q: Fill in the blank: 'The scientist was ___ about her discovery — she refused to share details until the research was complete.'
questions.exp:
Context clue: 'refused to share details' → she kept it hidden → secretive.
'Talkative' would mean she talked about it; 'enthusiastic' doesn't explain the silence.
questions.hint: What behaviour matches 'refused to share details'?

questions.q: Fill: 'Although the athlete had trained for months, she was ___ nervous before the final race.'
questions.exp:
'Although' signals contrast — despite training, she was STILL nervous.
'Consequently/therefore' would imply cause-effect (not contrast).
'Because' would need to explain WHY, not contrast with training.
questions.hint: 'Although X, she was ___ Y' needs a word that signals the contrast continues.

questions.q: Fill: 'The film was widely ___: critics called it boring, audiences stayed away, and even the director seemed ___ with the final product.'
questions.exp:
Both blanks must be negative:
Critics called it boring → condemned (not praised)
Director seemed → dissatisfied (matches 'critics' finding it boring)
questions.hint: Both blanks need negative words — look for internal consistency.

questions.q:
3×3 grid: Row 1: 1, 2, 3. Row 2: 4, 5, 6. Row 3: 7, 8, ?
Rule: numbers increase by 1 left-to-right, top-to-bottom. Missing?
questions.exp:
Pattern: 1,2,3 / 4,5,6 / 7,8,9
Each row adds 3 to the row before. Missing = 9.
questions.hint: Consecutive numbers filling the grid row by row.

questions.q:
Each row: numbers triple.
Row 1: 1, 3, 9
Row 2: 2, 6, 18
Row 3: 4, 12, ?
Missing?
questions.exp:
Rule: col2 = col1×3, col3 = col2×3
12 × 3 = 36
questions.hint: Each number is 3× the previous in the same row.
Pattern: each row is n, n², n³ where n is the first number in that row.

questions.q:
Matrix rule: centre = product of corners.
| 2 | ? | 3 |
| ? | 24| ? |
| 4 | ? | 1 |
What is the centre (24) consistent with?
questions.exp:
Centre = product of all four corners: 2×3×4×1 = 24 ✓
This is the rule — verify it holds.
questions.hint: Test: multiply all four corner values together.

questions.q: Set A = {1,2,3,4,5}, Set B = {3,4,5,6,7}. What is A∩B (intersection)?
questions.hint: Intersection = elements that appear in BOTH sets.

questions.q: Set A = {1,2,3,4,5}, Set B = {3,4,5,6,7}. What is A∪B (union)?
questions.hint: Union = all elements from both sets combined (no repeats).

questions.q: In a universal set of 50 students: 30 play sport, 25 do art, 10 do both, and some do neither. How many do neither?
questions.exp:
Sport∪Art = 30+25−10 = 45
Neither = 50−45 = 5
questions.hint: Use inclusion-exclusion for the union, then subtract from total.

questions.q: Complete the series: January, March, May, ___
questions.exp:
Alternate months: Jan(1), Mar(3), May(5), Jul(7)
All are odd-numbered months.
questions.hint: Every second month: January, March, May, July...

questions.q: Complete: Monday, Wednesday, Friday, ___
questions.exp:
Every second day: Mon, Wed, Fri, Sun
(Mon→skip Tue→Wed→skip Thu→Fri→skip Sat→Sun)
questions.hint: Every alternate day of the week.

questions.q: Complete the word sequence: Caterpillar, Chrysalis, Butterfly, ___
questions.exp:
Life cycle of a butterfly: Egg → Caterpillar (larva) → Chrysalis (pupa) → Butterfly → lays Eggs → ...
The sequence continues back to Egg.
questions.hint: Think of the butterfly's life cycle — what comes after the adult butterfly?

questions.q: Complete: Senate, Parliament, Nation, ___
questions.exp:
Increasing scale: Senate (part of parliament) → Parliament (governs a nation) → Nation (part of a continent)
Next: Continent → even larger scale geopolitical unit.
questions.hint: Each step moves to a larger governing/geographical unit.

questions.q:
Read this short poem:

"The fog comes
on little cat feet.
It sits looking
over harbor and city
on silent haunches
and then moves on.
— Carl Sandburg, 'Fog'"

The fog is compared to a cat. This is an example of:
questions.exp:
The entire poem compares fog to a cat WITHOUT using 'like' or 'as' — it's a metaphor.
Because this comparison is sustained throughout the whole poem, it's an EXTENDED metaphor (not just one line).
questions.hint: A comparison without 'like' or 'as' = metaphor. A metaphor sustained across a whole poem = extended metaphor.

questions.q:
Read:

"The fog comes
on little cat feet.
It sits looking
over harbor and city
on silent haunches
and then moves on.
— Carl Sandburg, 'Fog'"

What does 'silent haunches' suggest about fog?
options: Fog moves quietly and crouches low, like a cat at rest
questions.exp:
'Haunches' = the hindquarters/hips of an animal crouching.
'Silent' reinforces the cat's stealth.
Together they suggest fog settles low and quietly — creeping without warning.
