questions.exp:
Rectangle area = 16×9 = 144 cm²
Square side = √144 = 12 cm
Square perimeter = 4×12 = 48 cm
questions.hint: Find the square's side from the area (√144), then multiply by 4.

questions.q: A cone has radius 6 cm and height 10 cm. What is its volume? (π≈3.14)
questions.exp:
V = ⅓πr²h = ⅓×3.14×36×10 = ⅓×1130.4 = 376.8 cm³
💡 A cone is ⅓ of the cylinder with same base and height.
questions.hint: Volume of cone = ⅓πr²h

questions.q: A sphere has volume 288π cm³. What is its radius? (V=4/3πr³)
questions.hint: Solve 4/3πr³ = 288π by dividing both sides by 4/3π, then cube rooting.

questions.q: How many ways can 3 students be chosen from a class of 8 to form a committee?
questions.exp:
This is a combination (order doesn't matter): ⁸C₃
= 8!/(3!×5!) = (8×7×6)/(3×2×1) = 336/6 = 56
questions.hint: ⁸C₃ = 8×7×6 / (3×2×1). Order doesn't matter for committee members.

questions.q: How many different 4-digit PIN codes can be made using digits 1-9 (no repetition)?
questions.exp:
Order MATTERS for PINs, no repetition: 9×8×7×6 = 3024 (permutation)
= ⁹P₄ = 9!/(9-4)! = 9!/5! = 3024
questions.hint: For PINs, order matters: use permutation = 9×8×7×6

questions.q: $5000 at 8% per annum compound interest for 2 years. Interest EARNED (not total amount)?
questions.exp:
A = 5000(1.08)² = 5000×1.1664 = $5832
Interest = 5832-5000 = $832
💡 Simple interest would give 5000×0.08×2=$800 — compound earns $32 more.
questions.hint: Find total amount first (A=P(1+r)ⁿ), then subtract principal.

questions.q: How long does it take $1000 to become $1331 at 10% compound interest?
questions.hint: Repeatedly multiply by 1.10 until you reach the target.

questions.q: A boat travels 60km downstream in 3 hours and 60km upstream in 4 hours. Find the boat's speed in still water.
questions.exp:
Downstream: 60/3=20 km/h = b+c (boat+current)
Upstream: 60/4=15 km/h = b-c (boat-current)
Add: 2b=35 → b=17.5 km/h
questions.hint: Downstream=boat+current, upstream=boat-current. Add the equations.

questions.q: Tickets: adult $12, child $8. 50 people paid $480. How many adults?
questions.hint: Set up two equations: one for people, one for money.

questions.q: A retailer buys at $200, marks up 40%, then discounts 25%. Profit or loss percentage?
questions.hint: Calculate: cost → marked price (+40%) → selling price (-25% of marked). Compare to original cost.

questions.q: If x is 20% more than y, then y is what percentage less than x?
questions.exp:
x = 1.2y → y = x/1.2 = x×(5/6)
y is 1/6 less than x = 16⅔% less than x.
💡 This is a common trap: 20% more and the reverse are NOT the same percentage.
questions.hint: If x=120% of y, then y=100/120 of x. The difference from x is 20/120=1/6≈16.67%.

questions.q: What is the remainder when 2^100 is divided by 3?
questions.exp:
Pattern of 2ⁿ mod 3:
2¹=2, mod3=2
2²=4, mod3=1
2³=8, mod3=2
2⁴=16, mod3=1
Pattern: odd powers→2, even powers→1
100 is even → 2^100 mod 3 = 1
questions.hint: Find the pattern of remainders — it cycles with period 2.

questions.q: A 3-digit number is a perfect square and a perfect cube. What is it?
questions.exp:
Need a number that is both n² AND n³ = n⁶.
Perfect 6th powers: 1⁶=1, 2⁶=64, 3⁶=729, 4⁶=4096
729 is the only 3-digit perfect 6th power.
✓ 729=27²=9³ ✓
questions.hint: A number that is both a perfect square and perfect cube must be a perfect 6th power.

questions.q: Solve: x² − x − 6 = 0
questions.exp:
Factorise: find two numbers multiplying to −6 and adding to −1.
3×(−2)=−6 ✓ and 3+(−2)=1... need −1.
(−3)×(2)=−6 ✓ and −3+2=−1 ✓
(x−3)(x+2)=0 → x=3 or x=−2
questions.hint: Find two numbers: product=−6, sum=−1.

questions.q: Parabola y=ax²+bx+c passes through (0,1), (1,0) and (−1,8). Find a.
questions.hint: Substitute each point to get three equations. The first gives c immediately.

questions.q: Data: 5, 8, 10, x, 14, 19. Mean = 11. Find x.
questions.hint: Total sum = mean × number of values. Find sum, then subtract all known values.

questions.q: Data: 3, 5, 7, 9, 11. If each value is multiplied by 3, the new standard deviation is:
questions.exp:
When you multiply all values by k, the standard deviation also multiplies by k (or |k|).
Multiplying by 3 → new SD = 3 × original SD.
💡 Adding a constant to all values does NOT change SD. Multiplying by k multiplies SD by k.
questions.hint: Multiplying all data by k multiplies the standard deviation by k.

questions.q: P(A)=0.4, P(B)=0.3, A and B are independent. P(A or B)?
questions.hint: Use inclusion-exclusion. For independent events, P(A∩B)=P(A)×P(B).

questions.q: Box: 5 red and 4 blue balls. Two drawn without replacement. P(both same colour)?
questions.hint: P(same) = P(both red) + P(both blue). For each, multiply consecutive probabilities.

questions.q: In a school of 120: 70 play sport, 60 study music, 30 do art. 25 do sport AND music, 15 do music AND art, 20 do sport AND art, 10 do all three. How many do NONE?
questions.hint: Use three-set inclusion-exclusion formula.

questions.q: What is the nth term of: 4, 7, 10, 13, ...?
questions.exp:
First term=4, common difference=3.
nth term = a+(n-1)d = 4+(n-1)×3 = 4+3n-3 = 3n+1
Check: n=1→4 ✓, n=2→7 ✓, n=3→10 ✓
questions.hint: nth term of arithmetic sequence = a+(n-1)d

questions.q: The sum of first n terms of a sequence is S(n)=n²+3n. What is the 5th term?
questions.hint: nth term = S(n) − S(n-1)

questions.q: A snail climbs 3m up a pole each day but slips back 1m each night. The pole is 12m. How many days to reach the top?
questions.exp:
Net gain per day (daytime+night) = 3−1 = 2m.
But on the FINAL day it reaches the top and doesn't slip.
After 5 nights: 5×2=10m. Day 6: climbs 3m to reach 13m > 12m ✓
Answer: 6 days
(After 5 days: at 10m. Day 6: climbs 3m to 13m > 12m — done!)
questions.hint: The snail doesn't slip on the day it reaches the top. Work out when that day is.

questions.q: 100 lockers, all closed. Student 1 opens every locker. Student 2 closes every 2nd. Student 3 toggles every 3rd. After all 100 students, which lockers are open?
questions.exp:
A locker ends OPEN if it's toggled an ODD number of times.
Locker n is toggled once for each divisor of n.
Odd number of divisors → ODD number of toggles → stays open.
Only perfect squares have an odd number of divisors (e.g., 9: divisors 1,3,9).
Open lockers: 1,4,9,16,25,36,49,64,81,100 — perfect squares!
questions.hint: A locker stays open if toggled an odd number of times. When does a number have an odd number of divisors?

questions.q: If you write all integers from 1 to 100, how many times does the digit '7' appear?
questions.exp:
7 in units: 7,17,27,37,47,57,67,77,87,97 → 10 times
7 in tens: 70,71,72,73,74,75,76,77,78,79 → 10 times
But 77 has two 7s (already counted: once in units, once in tens)
Total = 10+10 = 20
questions.hint: Count separately: how many times 7 appears in the units place, then the tens place.

questions.q: Using exactly 5 Australian coins, make $1.55. Which combination works? (Coins: 5c, 10c, 20c, 50c, $1)
questions.exp:
Option B: $1.00+$0.20+$0.20+$0.10+$0.05 = $1.55 ✓ and exactly 5 coins ✓
Option A: only 3 coins. Option C: 4 coins. Option D: 7 coins.
questions.hint: Try combinations adding to $1.55 using exactly 5 coins.

questions.q: Without a calculator: 37 × 43
questions.exp:
37×43 = (40−3)(40+3) = 40²−3² = 1600−9 = 1591
💡 The difference-of-squares trick: (a−b)(a+b) = a²−b²
questions.hint: Notice 37 and 43 are symmetric around 40: use (40−3)(40+3)=40²−9.

questions.q:
Read:
"The question of whether artificial intelligence can truly 'think' has divided philosophers and computer scientists for decades. Alan Turing proposed in 1950 that if a machine could converse indistinguishably from a human, it should be considered intelligent — a test now called the Turing Test. Critics argue this conflates performance with genuine understanding: a calculator performs mathematics without understanding numbers. John Searle's famous 'Chinese Room' thought experiment illustrated this: a person following rules to manipulate Chinese symbols could produce correct Chinese responses without understanding Chinese at all. Defenders of AI intelligence counter that the brain itself is, at some level, just neurons following electrochemical rules — so the distinction may be philosophically meaningless."

The Chinese Room thought experiment suggests that:
options: Following rules to produce correct output is not the same as understanding
The brain is similar to a computer
questions.exp:
Searle's point: the person in the room produces correct Chinese responses WITHOUT understanding Chinese.
This challenges the idea that producing the right output means you understand or 'think'.
questions.hint: What does the Chinese Room person demonstrate about rule-following vs understanding?

questions.q:
Read:
"The question of whether artificial intelligence can truly 'think' has divided philosophers and computer scientists for decades. Alan Turing proposed in 1950 that if a machine could converse indistinguishably from a human, it should be considered intelligent — a test now called the Turing Test. Critics argue this conflates performance with genuine understanding: a calculator performs mathematics without understanding numbers. John Searle's famous 'Chinese Room' thought experiment illustrated this: a person following rules to manipulate Chinese symbols could produce correct Chinese responses without understanding Chinese at all. Defenders of AI intelligence counter that the brain itself is, at some level, just neurons following electrochemical rules — so the distinction may be philosophically meaningless."

Defenders say the brain is also just neurons following rules. This response works by:
options: Challenging the assumption that rule-following precludes genuine understanding
questions.exp:
If we accept that humans think, and the brain is also 'just following electrochemical rules', then dismissing AI as 'just following rules' is inconsistent — it might apply equally to humans.
This is a tu quoque (turn it back) argument: the same criticism applies to human intelligence.
questions.hint: If the criticism is that AI 'just follows rules', what does pointing to the brain achieve?

questions.q:
Read:
"The question of whether artificial intelligence can truly 'think' has divided philosophers and computer scientists for decades. Alan Turing proposed in 1950 that if a machine could converse indistinguishably from a human, it should be considered intelligent — a test now called the Turing Test. Critics argue this conflates performance with genuine understanding: a calculator performs mathematics without understanding numbers. John Searle's famous 'Chinese Room' thought experiment illustrated this: a person following rules to manipulate Chinese symbols could produce correct Chinese responses without understanding Chinese at all. Defenders of AI intelligence counter that the brain itself is, at some level, just neurons following electrochemical rules — so the distinction may be philosophically meaningless."

'Conflates' most closely means:
options: Confuses two separate things by treating them as one
questions.exp:
Conflate = merge or mix two distinct things together, causing confusion.
'Conflates performance with genuine understanding' = incorrectly treats them as the same thing.
questions.hint: Conflate = incorrectly merge two separate concepts.

questions.q:
Read:
"The question of whether artificial intelligence can truly 'think' has divided philosophers and computer scientists for decades. Alan Turing proposed in 1950 that if a machine could converse indistinguishably from a human, it should be considered intelligent — a test now called the Turing Test. Critics argue this conflates performance with genuine understanding: a calculator performs mathematics without understanding numbers. John Searle's famous 'Chinese Room' thought experiment illustrated this: a person following rules to manipulate Chinese symbols could produce correct Chinese responses without understanding Chinese at all. Defenders of AI intelligence counter that the brain itself is, at some level, just neurons following electrochemical rules — so the distinction may be philosophically meaningless."

How does the passage organise its ideas?
options: Argument, counterargument, and a final philosophical challenge to the question itself
questions.exp:
Structure: Turing's test (argument for AI intelligence) → Searle's Chinese Room (counterargument) → defenders' response (reframing the question itself).
This is a classic dialectical structure: thesis → antithesis → synthesis.
questions.hint: Map each paragraph to its function: presenting a position, challenging it, or responding to the challenge.

questions.q:
Read:
"In 1972, the UN declared: 'The capacity of the Earth to support life has been disrupted at a rate unprecedented in human history.' Over fifty years later, scientists debate whether that disruption has deepened into what they call the 'sixth mass extinction' — a period of species loss comparable to the five previous extinction events in Earth's history, including the one that killed the dinosaurs. Unlike previous extinctions caused by asteroid strikes or volcanic activity, this one is attributed almost entirely to a single species. The irony is acute: the species responsible is also the only one capable of preventing it."

The final sentence ('The irony is acute...') most powerfully implies:
options: Humans have both the power to destroy and the unique responsibility and ability to save other species
questions.exp:
'Responsible' (for the problem) + 'only one capable of preventing it' creates the irony: the agent of destruction is also the only possible agent of salvation.
This implies both a moral burden and a unique hope.
questions.hint: What two contradictory roles does 'the species' have?

questions.q:
Read:
"In 1972, the UN declared: 'The capacity of the Earth to support life has been disrupted at a rate unprecedented in human history.' Over fifty years later, scientists debate whether that disruption has deepened into what they call the 'sixth mass extinction' — a period of species loss comparable to the five previous extinction events in Earth's history, including the one that killed the dinosaurs. Unlike previous extinctions caused by asteroid strikes or volcanic activity, this one is attributed almost entirely to a single species. The irony is acute: the species responsible is also the only one capable of preventing it."

The phrase 'The irony is acute' signals what authorial tone?
questions.exp:
'Acute' (sharp/severe) paired with the devastating comparison (species causing AND possibly preventing extinction) conveys gravity and implicit criticism of the species responsible.
This is controlled, serious writing — not neutral, not optimistic.
questions.hint: Is 'acute irony' a neutral observation, or does it imply something about the author's view?

questions.q:
Which opening paragraph is MOST effective for a short story about loss?
A) 'There was sadness everywhere after he died.'
B) 'She still set two coffee cups every morning without thinking — then stood at the counter, one cup cooling in each hand, remembering.'
C) 'This is a story about grief.'
D) 'He died suddenly last year and everyone was very sad about it.'
questions.exp:
Option B uses concrete, specific detail (two cups, the automatic routine, the realisation).
It SHOWS grief through behaviour rather than stating it ('was sadness', 'very sad').
'Show don't tell' is the core principle of effective creative writing.
Options A, C, D all tell the reader what to feel rather than creating the feeling.
questions.hint: Which option SHOWS the emotion through specific detail rather than stating it directly?

questions.q: The technique of revealing character through small, specific actions rather than direct description is called:
questions.exp:
Indirect characterisation = revealing character through actions, dialogue, choices, and reactions — rather than directly stating 'he was nervous' or 'she was kind'.
💡 Direct: 'She was generous.' Indirect: 'She left the last slice for him without being asked.'
questions.hint: Showing character through behaviour, not stating it directly = indirect characterisation.
Try: each term = 2×(previous) + 1
The differences between terms increase by 1 each time.

questions.q:
What fraction of this grid is shaded?
□□■
■□□
□■□
(■=shaded, □=unshaded)
questions.exp: 3 shaded out of 9 total = 3/9 = 1/3
questions.hint: Count shaded squares, divide by total.
Subtract equations systematically to find each symbol.
Find ◆ from the square root, then find ★.

questions.q:
Each row follows same rule:
| 5 | 3 | 8 |
| 7 | 4 | 11|
| 9 | 5 | __|
Missing?
questions.exp: Rule: col1+col2=col3. 9+5=14 ✓
questions.hint: Add the first two columns.

questions.q:
Each row follows same rule:
| 4 | 3 | 12|
| 6 | 4 | 24|
| 8 | 6 | __|
Missing?
questions.hint: Multiply the first two columns.

questions.q:
What comes next?
Z, X, V, T, R, ___
questions.exp:
Every other letter backwards from Z:
Z(26), X(24), V(22), T(20), R(18), P(16)
questions.hint: Going backwards through the alphabet, skipping one letter each time.

questions.q:
What is the pattern and next pair?
AC, BF, CI, DL, ___
questions.exp:
First letters: A,B,C,D,E (+1 each)
Second letters: C(3),F(6),I(9),L(12),O(15) (+3 each)
Answer: EO
questions.hint: Two interleaved sequences: first letters +1, second letters +3.

questions.q: A 3×3×3 cube is painted on all 6 faces, then cut into 27 unit cubes. How many unit cubes have paint on exactly 3 faces?
questions.exp:
Cubes with paint on exactly 3 faces are the CORNER cubes.
A 3×3×3 cube has 8 corners, each corner cube touches 3 faces.
= 8 cubes with exactly 3 painted faces
questions.hint: Corner cubes sit at the intersection of 3 faces.

questions.q: A rectangular room is 8m×6m×3m (l×w×h). What is the total surface area of the four walls only?
questions.exp:
Two long walls: 2×(8×3)=48m²
Two short walls: 2×(6×3)=36m²
Total walls only = 48+36 = 84m²
questions.hint: Four walls: 2 pairs. Don't include floor or ceiling.

questions.q: Orange juice and water in ratio 2:3. Total 500mL. How much OJ?
questions.exp: 2 parts of 5 total. 500×2/5=200mL
questions.hint: 2 parts out of 5 total.

questions.q: A:B=3:4, B:C=2:5. Find A:B:C.
questions.exp: Scale so B matches: A:B=3:4=6:8, B:C=2:5=8:20. A:B:C=6:8:20
questions.hint: Make B the same value in both ratios.

questions.q: Cyclist: 60km at 20km/h, then 40km at 10km/h. Average speed?
questions.exp:
Time1=60÷20=3h, Time2=40÷10=4h. Total=100km÷7h≈14.3 km/h.
💡 Average speed ≠ average of speeds! Use total distance÷total time.
questions.hint: Average speed = total distance ÷ total time.

questions.q: A and B together do a job in 2h24min. A alone takes 4h. How long does B alone take?
questions.hint: Rate together minus rate of A = rate of B.

questions.q: Peter earns $18/hr normal and $27/hr overtime. He worked 45hrs and earned $882. How many overtime hours?
questions.hint: Assume first 40 are normal rate, then solve for overtime.

questions.q: Data: 2,3,3,4,5,5,5,7,8. Find the IQR.
questions.exp: Median=5(5th). Lower half 2,3,3,4→Q1=3. Upper half 5,5,7,8→Q3=6. IQR=6-3=3
questions.hint: IQR=Q3-Q1. Find medians of lower and upper halves.

questions.q: Regular pentagon: sum of interior angles?
Two angles of a quadrilateral are 85° and 110°. The other two are equal. What are they?
questions.hint: Quadrilateral angles sum to 360°.

questions.q: Chord 24cm, distance from centre 5cm. Find radius.
questions.hint: Right triangle: half-chord, distance, radius. Use Pythagoras.

questions.q: Are y=2x+3 and y=2x-5 parallel, perpendicular, or intersecting?
questions.exp: Same gradient m=2, different intercepts → parallel.
questions.hint: Same gradient = parallel.

questions.q: Line through (-2,5) and (4,2). Equation?
questions.hint: gradient=rise/run, then y-y₁=m(x-x₁)

questions.q: Simplify: (2x²y)³
questions.exp: (2)³(x²)³(y)³=8x⁶y³
questions.hint: (abc)ⁿ=aⁿbⁿcⁿ

questions.q: Solve: 4^x = 8
questions.hint: Write both as powers of 2.

questions.q: P(A)=0.4, P(B)=0.3, independent. P(A∩B)?
questions.exp: Independent: P(A∩B)=P(A)×P(B)=0.4×0.3=0.12
questions.hint: For independent events, P(A and B)=P(A)×P(B).

questions.q: Trapezium: parallel sides 8cm and 14cm, height 6cm. Area?
questions.hint: Area=½×(sum of parallel sides)×height

questions.q: $5000 at 8% pa compound for 2 years. Interest earned?
questions.hint: A=P(1+r)ⁿ, then subtract P.

questions.q: Triangle sides 7,8,9. Area by Heron's formula?
questions.hint: s=(a+b+c)/2, Area=√(s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c))

questions.q: Solve x²-x-6=0
questions.exp: Find factors: (x-3)(x+2)=0 → x=3 or x=-2. Check: product=-6✓ sum=-1✓
questions.hint: Find two numbers: product=-6, sum=-1.

questions.q: Mean=20, median=18, mode=15. Distribution is:
questions.exp: Mean>median>mode → right/positive skew. High outliers pull mean up.
questions.hint: When mean>median, distribution skews right (positive).

questions.q: Remainder when 2^100 ÷ 3?
questions.exp: 2^1 mod3=2, 2^2 mod3=1, pattern period 2. 100 is even → remainder 1.
questions.hint: Find the cycle of remainders. Even powers give remainder 1.

questions.q: Cyclist A and B start 180km apart heading toward each other. A: 30km/h, B: 60km/h. When do they meet?
questions.exp: Combined speed=90km/h. Time=180÷90=2h.
questions.hint: Combined speed when approaching = sum of speeds.

questions.q: All A→B. All B→C. Some C→D. Must 'all A→C' be true?
questions.exp: All A are B, all B are C → all A are C. ✓
questions.hint: Chain: A→B→C, so A→C

questions.q: 'Students with pets do better in school. Therefore pets improve academic performance.' Flaw?
questions.exp: Higher performers may choose pets (reverse causation), or a third factor causes both.
questions.hint: Which direction does causality run? Could it be reversed?
Group into pairs (n²-(n+1)²)=-（2n+1), then sum.

questions.q: CIRCUMFERENCE:CIRCLE as PERIMETER:___
questions.exp: Circumference=distance around a circle. Perimeter=distance around a polygon.
questions.hint: Circumference is the boundary measurement of a circle. Perimeter is the boundary measurement of a ___.

questions.q: Square perimeter 40cm, folded diagonally. Hypotenuse of resulting triangle?
questions.exp: Side=10cm. Diagonal=10√2≈14.14cm.
questions.hint: Diagonal of square=side×√2

questions.q: 6 runners A-F. B is 3rd. A before B. D directly after B. E is last. C between A and B. Where is F?
questions.exp: E=6th,B=3rd,D=4th. A&C before B: A=1st,C=2nd. F=5th.
questions.hint: Place definite positions first.

questions.q: 'Olympic athletes endorse this supplement!' This is:
questions.exp: Athlete endorsement ≠ scientific proof of effectiveness.
questions.hint: Is an athlete's endorsement the same as a scientific study?

questions.exp: Each term: 2×prev+1. 31×2+1=63.
questions.hint: Rule: 2×(previous term)+1
Pattern doubles each time.

questions.q: 'Only members can vote. Priya voted.' Therefore:
questions.exp: Contrapositive of 'non-member cannot vote' = 'voted → is member'. Definite conclusion.
questions.hint: Contrapositive: if she voted, she must be a member.

questions.q: AUDACIOUS most closely means:
questions.exp: Audacious=showing bold willingness to take risks. From Latin audax (bold).
questions.hint: Audacity=boldness. Audacious=?

questions.q: VERBOSE most closely means:
questions.exp: Verbose=using more words than necessary. From Latin verbum (word).
questions.hint: Verbose comes from 'verbum' (word) + excess.

questions.q: MALEVOLENT most closely means:
questions.exp: Malevolent=having evil intentions. Latin: male(badly)+volent(wishing). Opposite: benevolent.
questions.hint: Male=badly, volent=wishing. Compare benevolent (bene=well).

questions.q: NOCTURNAL:NIGHT as DIURNAL:___
questions.exp: Nocturnal=active at night. Diurnal=active during the day.
questions.hint: Nocturnal→night. Diurnal→?

questions.q: TAUTOLOGY:REDUNDANCY as OXYMORON:___
questions.exp: Tautology=redundant repetition. Oxymoron=contradiction (e.g. 'deafening silence').
questions.hint: Tautology creates redundancy. Oxymoron creates what?

questions.q: FELINE:CAT as BOVINE:___
questions.exp:
Feline=relating to cats. Bovine=relating to cows.
More: canine(dog), ovine(sheep), porcine(pig), equine(horse)
questions.hint: Feline relates to cats. Bovine relates to?

questions.q: BIBLIOGRAPHY:SOURCES as GLOSSARY:___
questions.exp: Bibliography=list of sources. Glossary=list of terms and their definitions.
questions.hint: What does a glossary contain?

questions.q: EPITAPH · ELEGY · EULOGY · DIRGE · DIATRIBE — which does NOT belong?
questions.exp:
Epitaph(grave inscription), Elegy(mournful poem), Eulogy(memorial speech), Dirge(funeral song) all relate to death/mourning.
Diatribe=bitter verbal attack — unrelated to death.
questions.hint: Four associate with death/mourning. Which is an angry verbal attack?

questions.q: SATIRE · PARODY · CARICATURE · IRONY — which does NOT belong?
questions.exp:
Satire, Parody, Caricature all use exaggeration to mock or critique.
Irony=saying opposite of what you mean — no exaggeration required.
questions.hint: Three mock through exaggeration. Which works through saying the opposite?

questions.q:
Which does NOT belong?
Moratorium · Hiatus · Interlude · Interim · Abolition
questions.exp:
Moratorium(temporary halt), Hiatus(pause/break), Interlude(pause between parts), Interim(meantime/temporary) all refer to temporary pauses.
Abolition=permanently ending something — not temporary.
questions.hint: Four mean 'temporary pause'. Which means 'permanent end'?

questions.q: The historian's account was so ___ that it included a 20-page appendix on the colour of Napoleon's boots.
questions.exp: Pedantic=obsessively focused on minor/trivial details. The boot colour is trivial but gets 20 pages.
questions.hint: 'Pedantic' means overemphasising trivial details specifically.

questions.q:
Read:
"For most of human history, what we knew about the universe was limited to what our eyes could detect — a sliver of the electromagnetic spectrum we call visible light. Then, in the twentieth century, we learned to 'see' in radio waves, X-rays, infrared, and gamma rays. Each wavelength revealed a different universe. Radio astronomy showed us pulsars. X-ray telescopes revealed black holes. Infrared penetrated the dust clouds that obscure new star formation. The James Webb Space Telescope, launched in 2021, detects infrared radiation from the early universe, letting us peer back nearly 13.8 billion years to see the first galaxies forming just after the Big Bang. Each new instrument, in a very real sense, creates a new universe to explore."

The central idea is:
options: Each new detection method reveals new aspects of the universe
The universe is 13.8 billion years old
questions.exp: Each example (radio→pulsars, X-ray→black holes, infrared→star formation, JWST→early galaxies) shows: new detection = new discovery. That's the pattern.
questions.hint: What does EVERY example in the passage show?

questions.q:
Read:
"For most of human history, what we knew about the universe was limited to what our eyes could detect — a sliver of the electromagnetic spectrum we call visible light. Then, in the twentieth century, we learned to 'see' in radio waves, X-rays, infrared, and gamma rays. Each wavelength revealed a different universe. Radio astronomy showed us pulsars. X-ray telescopes revealed black holes. Infrared penetrated the dust clouds that obscure new star formation. The James Webb Space Telescope, launched in 2021, detects infrared radiation from the early universe, letting us peer back nearly 13.8 billion years to see the first galaxies forming just after the Big Bang. Each new instrument, in a very real sense, creates a new universe to explore."

'Obscure' as used here means:
questions.exp: Dust clouds 'obscure' star formation = they block/hide it from view.
questions.hint: If dust 'obscures' star formation, what are the clouds doing to it?

questions.q:
Read:
"For most of human history, what we knew about the universe was limited to what our eyes could detect — a sliver of the electromagnetic spectrum we call visible light. Then, in the twentieth century, we learned to 'see' in radio waves, X-rays, infrared, and gamma rays. Each wavelength revealed a different universe. Radio astronomy showed us pulsars. X-ray telescopes revealed black holes. Infrared penetrated the dust clouds that obscure new star formation. The James Webb Space Telescope, launched in 2021, detects infrared radiation from the early universe, letting us peer back nearly 13.8 billion years to see the first galaxies forming just after the Big Bang. Each new instrument, in a very real sense, creates a new universe to explore."

'Each new instrument creates a new universe' is:
options: A metaphor — each reveals a previously invisible aspect of the same universe
questions.exp: Can't literally create universes. Metaphor: instrument reveals a universe WE couldn't previously see — so in terms of our knowledge, it is new.
questions.hint: Can a telescope create a universe? What does 'creates' mean here?

questions.q:
Read:
"When European settlers arrived in Australia, they carried with them not just tools and ambitions but species. Rabbits, foxes, cats, and cane toads were all deliberately or accidentally introduced. Without natural predators, these invasive species multiplied catastrophically. By the 1900s, rabbits had devastated native vegetation across millions of hectares, triggering the extinction of several native species. Cats, meanwhile, are responsible for the extinction of at least 20 native mammal species in Australia — making Australia home to one of the worst extinction records in the world. The lesson is now well understood: ecosystems are exquisitely balanced, and even a single introduction can unravel them."

'Exquisitely balanced' suggests:
options: Small changes can cause large disruptions due to precise interdependence
questions.exp: 'Exquisitely'=with extreme precision. Precisely balanced system → any disruption cascades through everything.
questions.hint: 'Exquisitely' means with extreme precision. What does that imply about the balance?

questions.q:
Read:
"When European settlers arrived in Australia, they carried with them not just tools and ambitions but species. Rabbits, foxes, cats, and cane toads were all deliberately or accidentally introduced. Without natural predators, these invasive species multiplied catastrophically. By the 1900s, rabbits had devastated native vegetation across millions of hectares, triggering the extinction of several native species. Cats, meanwhile, are responsible for the extinction of at least 20 native mammal species in Australia — making Australia home to one of the worst extinction records in the world. The lesson is now well understood: ecosystems are exquisitely balanced, and even a single introduction can unravel them."

The statistic 'at least 20 extinctions' is included to:
options: Make the argument concrete and hard to dispute
questions.exp: Specific, verifiable numbers make the claim measurable and credible — harder to dismiss than vague language.
questions.hint: Why '20' rather than just saying 'many'?

questions.q:
Read:
"When European settlers arrived in Australia, they carried with them not just tools and ambitions but species. Rabbits, foxes, cats, and cane toads were all deliberately or accidentally introduced. Without natural predators, these invasive species multiplied catastrophically. By the 1900s, rabbits had devastated native vegetation across millions of hectares, triggering the extinction of several native species. Cats, meanwhile, are responsible for the extinction of at least 20 native mammal species in Australia — making Australia home to one of the worst extinction records in the world. The lesson is now well understood: ecosystems are exquisitely balanced, and even a single introduction can unravel them."

The final sentence ('ecosystems are exquisitely balanced') functions as:
options: A summary lesson drawn from all the examples
questions.exp: After listing examples of catastrophic introductions, the final sentence extracts the principle those examples demonstrate — a classic inductive structure.
questions.hint: What is the relationship between the examples and the final sentence?

questions.q: What is 3/4 + 2/3?
questions.hint: Common denominator = 12

questions.q: Simplify: 2/3 ÷ 4/9
questions.hint: Dividing by a fraction = multiplying by its reciprocal.

questions.q: 0.125 as a fraction in simplest form?
questions.hint: 125/1000: divide both by 125.

questions.q: Which is largest? 3/7, 5/11, 7/15, 9/20
questions.hint: Convert each to a decimal to compare.
Each term halves. Use common denominator 32.

questions.q: A fair die is rolled. P(even)?
questions.exp: Even: 2,4,6 = 3 outcomes out of 6 = 1/2
questions.hint: Count favourable outcomes ÷ total outcomes.

questions.q: Two dice rolled. P(sum=7)?
questions.hint: List all pairs that sum to 7.

questions.q: Bag: 4 red, 3 blue, 2 green. Pick 2 without replacement. P(both red)?
questions.hint: First pick: 4/9. Second pick: 3/8 (one red removed).

questions.q: Convert 2.5 km to metres.
A cube has volume 125 cm³. What is its side length?
questions.exp: ∛125=5. Side=5cm
questions.hint: V=s³, so s=∛V

questions.q: Water flows at 5 litres/min. How long to fill a 300L tank?
questions.exp: 300÷5=60 minutes
questions.hint: Time = volume ÷ rate

questions.q: Simplify: √50 + √18
questions.hint: Simplify each surd, then combine like terms.

questions.q: Rationalise: 4/(√2)
questions.hint: Multiply top and bottom by √2.

questions.q: If x²+5x+6=0, find x.
questions.hint: Find two numbers: product=6, sum=5.

questions.q: x²-16=0. Solutions?
questions.hint: Square root both sides.

questions.q: Area of triangle: base 12cm, height 8cm?
questions.hint: A=½bh

questions.q: Area of a circle with diameter 14cm? (π=22/7)
questions.hint: A=πr², and radius = diameter÷2.

questions.q: Car travels 240km in 3 hours. Speed?
questions.hint: Speed=Distance÷Time

questions.q: Two trains 200km apart approach each other at 60km/h and 40km/h. When do they meet?
questions.exp: Combined speed=100km/h. Time=200÷100=2h
questions.hint: Combined speed = sum of speeds when approaching.

questions.q: Midpoint of (2,3) and (8,7)?
questions.hint: Midpoint=((x₁+x₂)/2,(y₁+y₂)/2)

questions.q: Distance between (1,2) and (4,6)?
questions.hint: d=√((x₂-x₁)²+(y₂-y₁)²)

questions.q: Simplify: 5⁰ × 3²
questions.hint: Any number to the power 0 = 1.

questions.q: 45 students: 25 play cricket, 20 play football, 8 play both. How many play neither?
questions.exp: Play at least one=25+20-8=37. Neither=45-37=8
questions.hint: Inclusion-exclusion: A∪B=A+B-A∩B

questions.q: HOT:COLD as TALL:___
questions.exp: Hot and cold are opposites. Tall↔Short.

questions.q: PUPPY:DOG as KITTEN:___
questions.exp: A puppy grows into a dog. A kitten grows into a cat.

questions.q: CHAPTER:BOOK as SCENE:___
questions.exp: A chapter is a section of a book. A scene is a section of a play.

questions.q: CALM:SERENE as HAPPY:___
questions.exp: Calm/serene are synonyms (slight intensity difference). Happy/elated are synonyms.

questions.q: DOCTOR:HOSPITAL as TEACHER:___
questions.exp: A doctor works in a hospital. A teacher works in a school.

questions.q: PROLOGUE:EPILOGUE as PREFACE:___
questions.exp: Prologue=beginning, epilogue=end. Preface=beginning, appendix=end.

questions.q: BARREN:FERTILE as ARID:___
questions.exp: Barren↔fertile (land). Arid↔humid (climate). Both are opposites.

questions.q: SCULPTOR:CHISEL as PAINTER:___
questions.exp: Primary tool: sculptor uses chisel, painter uses brush.

questions.q: SUMMIT:MOUNTAIN as ZENITH:___
questions.exp: Summit=highest point of mountain. Zenith=highest point of career/achievement.

questions.q: ARCHIPELAGO:ISLANDS as CONSTELLATION:___
questions.exp: Archipelago=group of islands. Constellation=group of stars.

questions.q: ENIGMA:PUZZLE as PARADOX:___
questions.exp: Enigma≈puzzle (something mysterious). Paradox≈contradiction (seemingly impossible).

questions.q: PENURY:WEALTH as APATHY:___
questions.exp: Penury(poverty)↔wealth. Apathy(no feeling)↔enthusiasm.

questions.q: UBIQUITOUS most closely means:
questions.exp: Ubiquitous=found everywhere. Smartphones are ubiquitous in modern life.

questions.q: RETICENT most closely means:
questions.exp: Reticent=unwilling to speak freely; reserved.

questions.q: BENEVOLENT most closely means:
questions.exp: Benevolent=well-meaning, kindly. From Latin bene(well)+volent(wishing).

questions.q: PRAGMATIC most closely means:
questions.exp: Pragmatic=dealing with things in a practical, realistic way.

questions.q: EXACERBATE most closely means:
questions.exp: Exacerbate=to make a bad situation worse. Opposite of ameliorate.

questions.q: EPHEMERAL most closely means:
questions.exp: Ephemeral=lasting a very short time. Flowers are ephemeral.

questions.q: CACOPHONY most closely means:
questions.exp: Cacophony=a harsh, discordant mix of sounds. From Greek kakos(bad)+phone(sound).

questions.q: PERFUNCTORY most closely means:
options: Done without care or interest; routine
questions.exp: Perfunctory=carried out with minimum effort; superficial.

questions.q: PERNICIOUS most closely means:
questions.exp: Pernicious=having a harmful effect, especially gradually. Pernicious rumours.

questions.q: SYCOPHANT most closely means:
options: A person who flatters to gain advantage
questions.exp: Sycophant=a person who acts obsequiously to gain advantage.

questions.q:
Which does NOT belong?
Cello · Violin · Trumpet · Viola
questions.exp: Cello, Violin, Viola = string instruments. Trumpet = brass.
questions.hint: Three are string instruments.

questions.q:
Which does NOT belong?
Metaphor · Simile · Onomatopoeia · Hyperbole
questions.exp: Metaphor, Simile, Hyperbole = figures of comparison/exaggeration. Onomatopoeia = sound imitation.
questions.hint: Three involve comparison or exaggeration.

questions.q:
Which does NOT belong?
Stoic · Spartan · Epicurean · Byzantine · Georgian
questions.exp: Stoic, Spartan, Epicurean, Byzantine = from ancient Greek/Roman proper nouns. Georgian = from a British king's name (different origin).
questions.hint: Four derive from ancient Greek/Roman names.

questions.q: Despite the heavy rain, the match ___ as planned.
questions.exp: 'Despite' signals contrast — rain should cancel, BUT it proceeded.
questions.hint: 'Despite' signals something unexpected happened.

questions.q: The artist's work was so ___ that critics couldn't agree whether it was brilliant or terrible.
questions.exp: Can't agree + extreme reactions = polarising.
questions.hint: What word means causing sharply divided opinions?

questions.q: The witness's testimony was ___ by several inconsistencies that emerged during cross-examination.
questions.exp: Inconsistencies weaken testimony = undermined.
questions.hint: Inconsistencies weaken or strengthen?

questions.q: Group: Mercury, Venus, Mars, Jupiter. Category?
questions.exp: All are planets.
questions.hint: What do Mercury, Venus, Mars, Jupiter have in common?

questions.q: SANGUINE, CHOLERIC, MELANCHOLIC, PHLEGMATIC are all types of:
questions.exp: The four classical temperaments (Hippocrates).
questions.hint: Ancient Greek system of personality types.

questions.q: All roses are flowers. Some flowers are red. Which MUST be true?
options: None of these must be true
questions.exp: 'Some flowers are red' doesn't mean those red flowers are roses.
questions.hint: 'Some flowers are red' doesn't tell us which flowers.

questions.q: If no A are B, and all B are C, then:
options: Cannot determine A and C relationship
questions.exp: No A are B, all B are C. But A could still overlap with C through another route.
questions.hint: No A are B tells you nothing about A and C directly.

questions.q: Five people sit in a row. A is not next to B. C is at one end. D is next to E. B is in the middle. Where is A?
questions.exp: B=pos3(middle). C=1 or 5. D next to E. A not next to B(3), so A≠2,4. A must be at end not taken by C.
questions.hint: B is middle (pos 3). A can't be pos 2 or 4. A must be at an end.

questions.q: If it rains, the ground is wet. The ground is wet. Can we conclude it rained?
options: No — the ground could be wet for another reason
questions.exp: Affirming the consequent fallacy. Sprinklers, flooding etc could also make ground wet.
questions.hint: The ground being wet doesn't prove rain — other causes exist.

questions.exp: Differences: 5,7,9,11,13. Next=35+13=48
questions.hint: Differences increase by 2 each time.

questions.exp: Perfect squares: 1²,2²,3²,4²,5²,6²=36
questions.hint: These are perfect squares!

questions.exp: Prime numbers! Next prime after 13 is 17.
questions.hint: These are consecutive prime numbers.
Each term is halved.

questions.exp: Differences: 1,2,3,4,5,6. Next=16+6=22
questions.hint: Differences increase by 1 each time.

questions.q: 'All my friends think this movie is great, so it must be great.' Fallacy?
questions.exp: Using popularity/consensus as proof = appeal to popularity (ad populum).
questions.hint: Does popularity prove quality?

questions.q: 'Either you support this policy completely or you're against progress.' Fallacy?
questions.exp: Only two extreme options presented when many exist = false dilemma.
questions.hint: Are there really only two options?

questions.q: 'You can't trust his climate research — he drives a petrol car!' Fallacy?
questions.exp: Attacking the person's behaviour instead of their argument = ad hominem/tu quoque.
questions.hint: Is the car relevant to the quality of the research?

questions.q: How many faces does a triangular prism have?
questions.exp: 2 triangular ends + 3 rectangular sides = 5 faces
questions.hint: Count the ends and sides separately.

questions.q: A cube has 6 faces. How many edges?
questions.exp: 12 edges: 4 on top, 4 on bottom, 4 vertical.
questions.hint: Count systematically: top edges + bottom edges + vertical edges.

questions.q: If you cut a cube with one straight cut, what cross-section shapes are possible?
questions.exp: Depending on angle: square(parallel to face), rectangle(angled), triangle(through 3 edges), hexagon(through all 6 faces).
questions.hint: Try different cutting angles mentally.

questions.q: Mercury, Mars, Jupiter, Sun — which does NOT belong?
questions.exp: Mercury, Mars, Jupiter = planets. Sun = star.
questions.hint: Three orbit the Sun. One IS the Sun.

questions.q: Simile, Metaphor, Alliteration, Personification — which uses repeated sounds?
questions.exp: Alliteration = repetition of initial consonant sounds (e.g., 'Peter Piper picked').
questions.hint: Which is about sound repetition, not comparison?

questions.q: Solve: 2(x+3)=x+10
questions.hint: Expand, collect like terms.

questions.q: A spinner has 4 equal sections: red, blue, green, yellow. P(blue)?
questions.exp: 1 favourable out of 4 equal sections = 1/4
questions.hint: Favourable outcomes ÷ total outcomes.

questions.q: Volume of rectangular prism: 6×4×3 cm?
questions.hint: V=l×w×h

questions.q: 3 workers do a job in 12 days. How many days for 4 workers?
questions.exp: Worker-days=3×12=36. Days for 4=36÷4=9
questions.hint: More workers = fewer days. Total work stays same.

questions.q: nth term of 5,8,11,14...?
questions.hint: nth = a + (n-1)d, then simplify.

questions.q:
Read:
"The development of antibiotics in the twentieth century transformed medicine, turning previously fatal infections into treatable conditions. Penicillin, discovered by Alexander Fleming in 1928, became the first widely used antibiotic. However, the overuse and misuse of antibiotics has led to the emergence of antibiotic-resistant bacteria — so-called 'superbugs' — which the World Health Organisation has called one of the greatest threats to global health."

Who discovered penicillin?
questions.exp: 'Penicillin, discovered by Alexander Fleming in 1928'
questions.hint: Find the name directly stated in the passage.

questions.q:
Read:
"The development of antibiotics in the twentieth century transformed medicine, turning previously fatal infections into treatable conditions. Penicillin, discovered by Alexander Fleming in 1928, became the first widely used antibiotic. However, the overuse and misuse of antibiotics has led to the emergence of antibiotic-resistant bacteria — so-called 'superbugs' — which the World Health Organisation has called one of the greatest threats to global health."

The passage implies that antibiotics:
options: Were an unmixed blessing with no downsides
Solved one problem but created another
questions.exp: Transformed medicine (positive) BUT overuse created superbugs (negative) → solved one problem, created another.
questions.hint: Positive outcome + negative consequence = ?

questions.q:
Read:
"The development of antibiotics in the twentieth century transformed medicine, turning previously fatal infections into treatable conditions. Penicillin, discovered by Alexander Fleming in 1928, became the first widely used antibiotic. However, the overuse and misuse of antibiotics has led to the emergence of antibiotic-resistant bacteria — so-called 'superbugs' — which the World Health Organisation has called one of the greatest threats to global health."

'Emergence' most closely means:
options: The process of coming into being
questions.exp: Emergence = the process of becoming visible or coming into existence. Antibiotic-resistant bacteria emerged (came into being) due to overuse.
questions.hint: What does 'emerge' mean?

questions.q:
Read:
"Every year, millions of wildebeest cross the Serengeti plains in Tanzania, following the rains in search of fresh grazing. This Great Migration is one of nature's most spectacular events. The journey is perilous: crocodiles lurk in rivers, lions patrol the grasslands, and exhaustion claims the weakest. Yet the herds press on, driven by an instinct older than memory."

The main idea is:
options: The wildebeest migration is both magnificent and dangerous
questions.exp: 'Most spectacular' (magnificent) + 'perilous' (dangerous) = both.
questions.hint: What two qualities does the passage emphasise?

questions.q:
Read:
"Every year, millions of wildebeest cross the Serengeti plains in Tanzania, following the rains in search of fresh grazing. This Great Migration is one of nature's most spectacular events. The journey is perilous: crocodiles lurk in rivers, lions patrol the grasslands, and exhaustion claims the weakest. Yet the herds press on, driven by an instinct older than memory."

'An instinct older than memory' suggests:
options: The migration has been happening for so long that no individual remembers its origin — it's deeply biological
questions.exp: 'Older than memory' = predates any individual's lifetime. It's hardwired instinct, not learned behaviour.
questions.hint: Can any living wildebeest 'remember' why they migrate?

questions.q:
Read:
"Every year, millions of wildebeest cross the Serengeti plains in Tanzania, following the rains in search of fresh grazing. This Great Migration is one of nature's most spectacular events. The journey is perilous: crocodiles lurk in rivers, lions patrol the grasslands, and exhaustion claims the weakest. Yet the herds press on, driven by an instinct older than memory."

'Perilous' means:
questions.exp: Perilous = full of danger or risk. The dangers listed (crocodiles, lions, exhaustion) confirm this.
questions.hint: What follows 'perilous' in the passage? Crocodiles, lions, exhaustion — all dangers.

questions.q:
Read:
"In 1969, humans first set foot on the Moon. Neil Armstrong's words — reported as 'That's one small step for man, one giant leap for mankind' — captured the moment's significance. The Apollo 11 mission represented the culmination of a decade of effort, billions of dollars, and the work of over 400,000 people. Yet within three years, public interest had waned, and the Apollo programme was cancelled in 1972."

The final sentence creates a tone of:
options: Irony — such an enormous achievement was quickly forgotten
questions.exp: Massive effort → cancellation within 3 years = ironic contrast between achievement and public indifference.
questions.hint: What's the contrast between the effort described and the ending?

questions.q:
Read:
"In 1969, humans first set foot on the Moon. Neil Armstrong's words — reported as 'That's one small step for man, one giant leap for mankind' — captured the moment's significance. The Apollo 11 mission represented the culmination of a decade of effort, billions of dollars, and the work of over 400,000 people. Yet within three years, public interest had waned, and the Apollo programme was cancelled in 1972."

The numbers (400,000 people, billions of dollars) are included to:
options: Emphasise the enormous scale of the achievement
questions.exp: Specific large numbers make the scale concrete and impressive.
questions.hint: Why include exact numbers rather than just saying 'a lot'?

questions.q:
Read:
"In Australia, the platypus holds a unique place in biology. It is one of only five species of monotremes — mammals that lay eggs. It has a bill like a duck, a tail like a beaver, and venomous spurs on its hind legs. When a preserved specimen was first sent to European scientists in 1799, they assumed it was a hoax — different animal parts sewn together."

What are monotremes?
questions.exp: 'Monotremes — mammals that lay eggs'
questions.hint: Find the definition given in the passage.

questions.q:
Read:
"In Australia, the platypus holds a unique place in biology. It is one of only five species of monotremes — mammals that lay eggs. It has a bill like a duck, a tail like a beaver, and venomous spurs on its hind legs. When a preserved specimen was first sent to European scientists in 1799, they assumed it was a hoax — different animal parts sewn together."

Why did scientists think the specimen was a hoax?
options: Its combination of features from different animal groups seemed impossible
questions.exp: Bill like a duck + tail like a beaver + venomous spurs = seemingly impossible combination.
questions.hint: What about the platypus seemed fake to scientists?

questions.q:
Read:
"Language shapes thought as much as thought shapes language. The Sapir-Whorf hypothesis suggests that the structure of a language affects its speakers' cognition and worldview. For example, some Indigenous Australian languages describe direction using absolute terms (north, south, east, west) rather than relative terms (left, right). Speakers of these languages maintain an almost perfect sense of cardinal direction at all times — a cognitive feat that English speakers rarely develop."

The central claim is:
options: English is better than other languages
The language you speak influences how you think
questions.exp: 'Language shapes thought' is the opening thesis; the Indigenous direction example illustrates it.
questions.hint: What does the passage argue language does to thought?

questions.q:
Read:
"Language shapes thought as much as thought shapes language. The Sapir-Whorf hypothesis suggests that the structure of a language affects its speakers' cognition and worldview. For example, some Indigenous Australian languages describe direction using absolute terms (north, south, east, west) rather than relative terms (left, right). Speakers of these languages maintain an almost perfect sense of cardinal direction at all times — a cognitive feat that English speakers rarely develop."

'Cognition' means:
options: The mental process of acquiring knowledge and understanding
questions.exp: Cognition = mental processes: thinking, understanding, perceiving, reasoning.
questions.hint: 'Cognition and worldview' — both relate to mental processes.

questions.q:
Read:
"Language shapes thought as much as thought shapes language. The Sapir-Whorf hypothesis suggests that the structure of a language affects its speakers' cognition and worldview. For example, some Indigenous Australian languages describe direction using absolute terms (north, south, east, west) rather than relative terms (left, right). Speakers of these languages maintain an almost perfect sense of cardinal direction at all times — a cognitive feat that English speakers rarely develop."

The passage suggests English speakers:
options: May lack constant directional awareness because English uses relative terms like 'left' and 'right'
questions.exp: English uses left/right (relative), not N/S/E/W (absolute). This means English speakers don't develop the constant compass-awareness that absolute-direction speakers do.
questions.hint: How does 'left/right' differ from 'north/south' in terms of awareness?

questions.q:
Read:
"Coral reefs occupy less than 1% of the ocean floor but support approximately 25% of all marine species. Often called 'rainforests of the sea,' they provide food, coastal protection, and income for hundreds of millions of people. Yet reefs are among the most threatened ecosystems on Earth. Rising ocean temperatures cause coral bleaching, where stressed corals expel the algae that give them colour and nutrition. Without intervention, scientists predict that 90% of reefs could be lost by 2050."

The passage structure is:
questions.exp: Para structure: how important reefs are (1%) → what threatens them (bleaching) → prediction (90% by 2050).
questions.hint: Map each section: what does it do?

questions.q:
Read:
"Coral reefs occupy less than 1% of the ocean floor but support approximately 25% of all marine species. Often called 'rainforests of the sea,' they provide food, coastal protection, and income for hundreds of millions of people. Yet reefs are among the most threatened ecosystems on Earth. Rising ocean temperatures cause coral bleaching, where stressed corals expel the algae that give them colour and nutrition. Without intervention, scientists predict that 90% of reefs could be lost by 2050."

The comparison to 'rainforests of the sea' emphasises:
options: Reefs are extraordinarily biodiverse relative to their size, just like tropical rainforests
questions.exp: Rainforests = small area, enormous biodiversity. Reefs = same pattern (1% of ocean, 25% of species). The comparison highlights disproportionate importance.
questions.hint: What quality do rainforests and reefs share?

questions.q:
Read:
"Coral reefs occupy less than 1% of the ocean floor but support approximately 25% of all marine species. Often called 'rainforests of the sea,' they provide food, coastal protection, and income for hundreds of millions of people. Yet reefs are among the most threatened ecosystems on Earth. Rising ocean temperatures cause coral bleaching, where stressed corals expel the algae that give them colour and nutrition. Without intervention, scientists predict that 90% of reefs could be lost by 2050."

'Expel' means:
questions.exp: Expel = to force out or discharge. Stressed corals force out their symbiotic algae.
questions.hint: What does a stressed coral do to its algae? The opposite of keeping them in.

questions.q: Next: B, D, F, H, ___
questions.exp: Every 2nd letter: B(2),D(4),F(6),H(8),J(10)
questions.hint: Skip one letter each time.

questions.q: Next: AA, CC, EE, GG, ___
questions.exp: Double letters advancing by 2: A,C,E,G,I
questions.hint: Pairs of letters, each pair jumps 2.
Perfect cubes!

questions.q: Square numbers: 1,4,9,16,25. What's the 10th square number?
questions.hint: nth square number = n²
Solve simultaneously.

questions.q: A baker makes 36 cupcakes and puts them equally in 4 boxes. How many per box?
What is half of 3/4?
What is 1000 − 357?
How many sides does a hexagon have?
questions.exp: Hex=6. Hexagon has 6 sides.

questions.q: How many minutes in 2.5 hours?
What is 10% of 250?
Round 4,567 to the nearest hundred.
questions.exp: 67≥50, round up to 4,600

questions.q: What gas do plants absorb for photosynthesis?
questions.exp: Plants absorb CO₂ and release O₂.

questions.q: How many bones in an adult human body?
questions.exp: 206 bones in an adult.

questions.q: A bus has 42 seats. 17 are taken. How many empty?
What number is 100 more than 4,899?
Area of rectangle 7cm × 4cm?
Which ocean is to the east of Australia?
questions.exp: The Pacific Ocean is east of Australia.

questions.q: What planet is closest to the Sun?
questions.exp: Mercury is the closest planet to the Sun.

questions.q: Five times a number minus 3 is 32. The number?
If 2ˣ=32, what is x?
Angle between clock hands at 6:00?
questions.exp: Hands are exactly opposite=180°
questions.hint: Hands point up and down.

questions.q: Arc length of 90° sector, r=14cm? (π=22/7)
questions.hint: Arc=θ/360 × 2πr

questions.q: P(drawing a face card from standard deck)?
questions.exp: 12 face cards (J,Q,K × 4 suits) out of 52=12/52=3/13
questions.hint: Face cards=J,Q,K in each of 4 suits.

questions.q: A map scale is 1:50000. 3cm on map = real distance?
questions.hint: Multiply by scale, convert cm to km.

questions.q: Dataset: 12,15,18,21,24. Standard deviation concept: if you add 10 to every value, the mean:
questions.exp: Adding constant shifts all values and mean by that constant.
questions.hint: Adding a constant shifts the entire distribution.

questions.q: First 3 common multiples of 4 and 6?
questions.exp: LCM=12. Multiples: 12,24,36
questions.hint: LCM of 4 and 6 is 12. Then 2×12, 3×12.

questions.q: What is 2⁴×2³?
questions.exp: Same base: add exponents. 2⁴⁺³=2⁷=128
questions.hint: aᵐ×aⁿ=aᵐ⁺ⁿ

questions.q: $1000 at 5% simple interest for 3 years. Interest earned?
questions.hint: Simple interest: I=Prt

questions.q: Population grows from 2000 to 2500. % increase?
questions.hint: % increase = increase ÷ original × 100

questions.q: Average of 5 numbers is 18. Four numbers are 12,15,20,22. The fifth?
questions.hint: Total=mean×count. Subtract known values.

questions.q: Two parallel lines cut by a transversal. Co-interior angles sum to?
questions.exp: Co-interior (same-side interior) angles are supplementary=180°
questions.hint: Co-interior angles are supplementary.

questions.q: If f(x)=2x+3, find f(5).
questions.hint: Substitute x=5 into the function.

questions.q: y-intercept of y=4x-7?
questions.hint: y-intercept is the value when x=0.

questions.q: In a right triangle, if cos θ=4/5, find sin θ.
questions.exp: sin²θ+cos²θ=1. sin²θ=1-16/25=9/25. sinθ=3/5
questions.hint: Use sin²θ+cos²θ=1

questions.q: Two coins tossed. P(at least one head)?
questions.hint: Complement: P(at least 1)=1-P(none).

questions.q: Shaded area: large circle r=10cm with small circle r=6cm cut out?
questions.hint: Subtract small circle area from large.

questions.q: A train leaves at 8:45 AM and arrives at 11:20 AM. Journey time?
questions.hint: Count hours then minutes.

questions.q: Complete the square: x²+6x+___=(x+3)²
questions.exp: (x+3)²=x²+6x+9. Missing number=9
questions.hint: (x+b)²=x²+2bx+b². So b²=?

questions.q: A rhombus has all sides 10cm. One diagonal is 12cm. Other diagonal?
questions.exp: Half diags: 6 and d/2. 6²+(d/2)²=10². (d/2)²=64. d/2=8. d=16cm
questions.hint: Diagonals bisect at right angles. Use Pythagoras.

questions.q: If all X are Y, and Z is an X, then Z is definitely:
questions.exp: All X→Y. Z is X→Z is Y.
questions.hint: Chain: Z is X, all X are Y, so Z is Y.

questions.exp: Even numbers: +2 each
questions.hint: Add 2 each time.
Multiply by 3.

questions.exp: Fibonacci: 13+21=34
questions.hint: Each = sum of previous two.

questions.q: Reverse alphabet: A=Z, B=Y... Encode: CAB
questions.exp: C→X, A→Z, B→Y. CAB→XZY
questions.hint: Each letter maps to its mirror: A↔Z, B↔Y, C↔X...

questions.q: 3 boxes: one has gold, two have nothing. Box A says 'Gold is in me'. Box B says 'Gold is not in me'. Box C says 'Gold is not in A'. Only ONE statement is true. Where is the gold?
questions.exp: If gold in B: A='in me'=false✓, B='not in me'=false✗. Try: If gold in B, A is false, B is false(says not in me, but it IS), C='not in A'=true. One true statement=C. ✓
questions.hint: Test each box: if gold is there, which statements are true?

questions.q: BOOK:SHELF as CAR:___
questions.exp: A book is stored on a shelf. A car is stored in a garage.
questions.hint: Where is each item kept?

questions.q: SEED:TREE as EGG:___
questions.exp: A seed grows into a tree. An egg grows into a bird.
questions.hint: What does each develop into?

questions.q: DEMOCRACY:AUTOCRACY as FREEDOM:___
questions.exp: Democracy↔autocracy (opposite governance). Freedom↔tyranny (opposite states).
questions.hint: Democracy and autocracy are opposites. Freedom and ___ are opposites.

questions.q: 'You should eat vegetables because they are healthy.' This is:
options: A valid argument with a reason
questions.exp: Claim + reason (because) = argument with support.
questions.hint: Does the statement give a reason for the claim?

questions.q: Which does NOT belong: Apple, Banana, Carrot, Mango?
questions.exp: Apple, Banana, Mango = fruits. Carrot = vegetable.
questions.hint: Three are fruits.

questions.q: Which does NOT belong: Democracy, Monarchy, Republic, Capitalism?
questions.exp: Democracy, Monarchy, Republic = political systems. Capitalism = economic system.
questions.hint: Three are political systems.

questions.q: Order smallest to largest: millimetre, metre, centimetre, kilometre
questions.exp: mm < cm < m < km

questions.q: 5 books A-E on a shelf. B is right of A. D is between B and E. C is leftmost. E is rightmost. Order?
questions.exp: C=leftmost, E=rightmost. B right of A. D between B and E. → C,A,B,D,E
questions.hint: Place definite positions first (C leftmost, E rightmost).

questions.q: How many faces on a standard die?
questions.exp: A die (cube) has 6 faces.
questions.hint: A die is a cube.

questions.q: Fold a square paper in half, then in half again. Cut a corner. Unfold — how many holes?
questions.exp: 2 folds = 4 layers. 1 cut through 4 layers = 4 holes when unfolded.
questions.hint: Each fold doubles the layers.

questions.q: LIBRARY:BOOKS as GALLERY:___
questions.exp: Library stores/displays books. Gallery stores/displays art.
questions.hint: What does a gallery display?

questions.q: ORNITHOLOGY:BIRDS as ICHTHYOLOGY:___
questions.exp: Ornithology=study of birds. Ichthyology=study of fish.
questions.hint: Both end in '-ology' (study of).

questions.q: Bar chart shows: Mon=10, Tue=15, Wed=5, Thu=20, Fri=10. Total sales?
questions.hint: Add all bars.

questions.q: Pie chart: Sport 40%, Music 25%, Art 20%, Drama 15%. 200 students total. How many chose Music?
questions.hint: Percentage × total ÷ 100

questions.q: PEN:WRITE as SCISSORS:___
questions.exp: Pen is used to write. Scissors are used to cut.
questions.hint: What is the function of scissors?

questions.q: CHAPTER:NOVEL as VERSE:___
questions.exp: A chapter is a section of a novel. A verse is a section of a poem.
questions.hint: What is a verse part of?

questions.q: EULOGY:PRAISE as ELEGY:___
questions.exp: Eulogy=speech of praise. Elegy=poem of mourning.
questions.hint: Eulogy praises. Elegy mourns.

questions.q: LETHARGIC most closely means:
questions.exp: Lethargic=lacking energy, sluggish, drowsy.
questions.hint: Lethargy = extreme tiredness and lack of energy.

questions.q: OBSEQUIOUS most closely means:
questions.exp: Obsequious=excessively eager to please or serve, often insincerely.
questions.hint: An obsequious person fawns and flatters.

questions.q: TANGIBLE most closely means:
options: Able to be touched; real and concrete
questions.exp: Tangible=perceptible by touch; concrete, real. Opposite: intangible.
questions.hint: From Latin tangere (to touch).

questions.q: ACRIMONY most closely means:
questions.exp: Acrimony=bitterness, hostility. An acrimonious divorce = bitter, angry.
questions.hint: Acrimony = intense bitterness in relationships.

questions.q: MERCURY · VENUS · EARTH · SUN · MARS
questions.exp: All others are planets. Sun is a star.
questions.hint: Four orbit the Sun.

questions.q: SIMILE · METAPHOR · ALLITERATION · ONOMATOPOEIA
questions.exp: Simile (comparison with 'like/as'), Metaphor (comparison without), Onomatopoeia (words mimicking sounds like 'buzz') are all about MEANING/representation. Alliteration is purely about SOUND repetition of initial consonants.
questions.hint: Which is fundamentally about sound rather than meaning/comparison?

questions.q: The detective's ___ eye noticed details others missed.
questions.exp: A keen eye = sharp, observant. Fits 'noticed details others missed'.
questions.hint: Which word means 'sharp and observant'?

questions.q: Her ___ approach to the problem — testing each variable systematically — was admired by her colleagues.
questions.exp: 'Testing systematically' = methodical (orderly, systematic).
questions.hint: Systematic, orderly approach = ?

questions.q: Shakespeare, Austen, Dickens, Einstein — who doesn't belong?
questions.exp: Shakespeare, Austen, Dickens = famous English authors. Einstein = scientist.
questions.hint: Three are authors.

questions.q: AMBIGUOUS most closely means:
options: Having more than one meaning; unclear
questions.exp: Ambiguous=open to multiple interpretations; unclear.
questions.hint: An ambiguous sentence could mean two different things.

questions.q: TELESCOPE:STARS as MICROSCOPE:___
questions.exp: Telescope views distant (stars). Microscope views tiny (cells).
questions.hint: What does a microscope help you see?

questions.q: HYPOTHESIS:EXPERIMENT as BLUEPRINT:___
questions.exp: Hypothesis guides experiment. Blueprint guides building construction.
questions.hint: A hypothesis is a plan for an experiment. A blueprint is a plan for a ___.

questions.q: If y=3x-2 and x=4, find y.
questions.hint: Substitute x=4.

questions.q: What is the smallest number divisible by both 6 and 8?
questions.hint: Find LCM of 6 and 8.
Convert to improper fractions with same denominator.

questions.q: Angles of a triangle: 2x, 3x, 4x. Find x.
questions.hint: Angles sum to 180°.

questions.q: 3 boys and 4 girls. Choose 2. P(both girls)?
A shop sells apples at 3 for $2. How much for 12?
questions.hint: How many groups of 3 in 12?

questions.q: Express 7/20 as a percentage.
questions.hint: Multiply by 100, or convert: 7/20 = 35/100.

questions.q: A rectangular pool: 25m × 10m × 2m. Volume in litres?
questions.hint: V=l×w×h in m³, then ×1000 for litres.

questions.q: Supplementary angles sum to?
questions.exp: Supplementary=180°. Complementary=90°.
questions.hint: Supplementary = 180°, complementary = 90°.

questions.q: If a:b=2:3 and b:c=3:4, find a:c.
questions.hint: Scale so b is the same in both ratios.

questions.q: What is the sum of all factors of 12?
questions.hint: List all factors, then add.

questions.q: A tank is 3/5 full. Adding 40L makes it 4/5 full. Tank capacity?
questions.exp: 4/5-3/5=1/5 of tank=40L. Full tank=40×5=200L
questions.hint: The difference (1/5) equals 40L.

questions.q: Two similar triangles: sides 3,4,5 and 6,8,x. Find x.
questions.exp: Scale factor=2. x=5×2=10
questions.hint: Corresponding sides have the same ratio.

questions.q: Spinner with sections 1-8 (equal). P(odd)?
questions.hint: Count odd numbers from 1 to 8.

questions.q: A is twice as old as B. In 5 years, A will be 1.5 times B's age. How old is B now?
questions.hint: Set up equations: A=2B and A+5=1.5(B+5)

questions.q:
Read:
"The Industrial Revolution transformed Britain from a predominantly agricultural society into the world's first industrial nation. Beginning in the late eighteenth century, new technologies — the steam engine, the spinning jenny, the power loom — mechanised production and concentrated workers in factories. Cities swelled as families left farms for factory work. Living conditions were often appalling: overcrowded housing, polluted air, child labour. Yet the revolution also brought unprecedented wealth, innovation, and the beginnings of a middle class."

The passage presents the Industrial Revolution as:
options: A complex transformation with both terrible costs and significant gains
questions.exp: Both negatives (appalling conditions) and positives (wealth, innovation) are presented → complex.
questions.hint: Does the passage show only positives, only negatives, or both?

questions.q:
Read:
"The Industrial Revolution transformed Britain from a predominantly agricultural society into the world's first industrial nation. Beginning in the late eighteenth century, new technologies — the steam engine, the spinning jenny, the power loom — mechanised production and concentrated workers in factories. Cities swelled as families left farms for factory work. Living conditions were often appalling: overcrowded housing, polluted air, child labour. Yet the revolution also brought unprecedented wealth, innovation, and the beginnings of a middle class."

'Unprecedented' means:
questions.exp: Unprecedented=never before seen/experienced. Un+precedent+ed.
questions.hint: Un-preceded: no precedent exists for it.

questions.q:
Read:
"The Industrial Revolution transformed Britain from a predominantly agricultural society into the world's first industrial nation. Beginning in the late eighteenth century, new technologies — the steam engine, the spinning jenny, the power loom — mechanised production and concentrated workers in factories. Cities swelled as families left farms for factory work. Living conditions were often appalling: overcrowded housing, polluted air, child labour. Yet the revolution also brought unprecedented wealth, innovation, and the beginnings of a middle class."

The phrase 'concentrated workers in factories' implies:
options: Workers were physically gathered into centralised locations, losing independence
questions.exp: 'Concentrated'=gathered together. Factories centralised labour that was previously spread across homes and farms.
questions.hint: What does 'concentrated' mean in terms of worker location?

questions.q:
Read:
"The Industrial Revolution transformed Britain from a predominantly agricultural society into the world's first industrial nation. Beginning in the late eighteenth century, new technologies — the steam engine, the spinning jenny, the power loom — mechanised production and concentrated workers in factories. Cities swelled as families left farms for factory work. Living conditions were often appalling: overcrowded housing, polluted air, child labour. Yet the revolution also brought unprecedented wealth, innovation, and the beginnings of a middle class."

The colon after 'appalling' introduces:
options: Specific examples that illustrate the general claim
questions.exp: Colon after 'appalling' introduces specific examples: overcrowding, pollution, child labour.
questions.hint: What follows the colon? General or specific?

questions.q:
Read:
"Sleep is not merely a pause in activity but a critical biological process. During deep sleep, the brain consolidates memories, transferring information from short-term to long-term storage. The glymphatic system — a network of channels that surround blood vessels — becomes active during sleep, flushing out metabolic waste products that accumulate during waking hours. Chronic sleep deprivation has been linked to impaired cognitive function, weakened immunity, and increased risk of heart disease. Yet many teenagers report sleeping fewer than seven hours on school nights."

The passage argues that sleep is:
options: Biologically essential with serious consequences if lacking
questions.exp: Multiple functions (memory, waste removal) + consequences of deprivation = biologically essential.
questions.hint: How many important functions of sleep are described?

questions.q:
Read:
"Sleep is not merely a pause in activity but a critical biological process. During deep sleep, the brain consolidates memories, transferring information from short-term to long-term storage. The glymphatic system — a network of channels that surround blood vessels — becomes active during sleep, flushing out metabolic waste products that accumulate during waking hours. Chronic sleep deprivation has been linked to impaired cognitive function, weakened immunity, and increased risk of heart disease. Yet many teenagers report sleeping fewer than seven hours on school nights."

'Chronic' means:
questions.exp: Chronic=long-lasting, ongoing. Chronic sleep deprivation=consistently not sleeping enough.
questions.hint: Chronic ≠ sudden. It means long-lasting.

questions.q:
Read:
"Sleep is not merely a pause in activity but a critical biological process. During deep sleep, the brain consolidates memories, transferring information from short-term to long-term storage. The glymphatic system — a network of channels that surround blood vessels — becomes active during sleep, flushing out metabolic waste products that accumulate during waking hours. Chronic sleep deprivation has been linked to impaired cognitive function, weakened immunity, and increased risk of heart disease. Yet many teenagers report sleeping fewer than seven hours on school nights."

The final sentence about teenagers is included to:
options: Show that the very group that needs sleep most may be getting the least
questions.exp: After explaining sleep's importance, noting teen under-sleeping creates an ironic gap between knowledge and practice.
questions.hint: What's the contrast between what the passage proves and what teenagers actually do?

questions.q:
Read:
"Sleep is not merely a pause in activity but a critical biological process. During deep sleep, the brain consolidates memories, transferring information from short-term to long-term storage. The glymphatic system — a network of channels that surround blood vessels — becomes active during sleep, flushing out metabolic waste products that accumulate during waking hours. Chronic sleep deprivation has been linked to impaired cognitive function, weakened immunity, and increased risk of heart disease. Yet many teenagers report sleeping fewer than seven hours on school nights."

The dash-enclosed phrase about the glymphatic system serves to:
options: Briefly define the technical term for readers who may not know it
questions.exp: Dashes enclose a parenthetical definition: what the glymphatic system IS.
questions.hint: What information does the dash-enclosed section provide?

questions.q:
Read:
"Tasmania's thylacine — commonly called the Tasmanian Tiger for its striped back — was declared extinct in 1936 when the last known individual died at Hobart Zoo. Despite being a marsupial, it had evolved to resemble a large dog, an example of convergent evolution where unrelated species develop similar features in response to similar environments. Periodic unconfirmed sightings continue to this day, though no credible evidence has emerged. In 2023, Colossal Biosciences announced a project to attempt de-extinction of the thylacine using gene-editing technology."

When did the last known thylacine die?
questions.exp: 'Declared extinct in 1936 when the last known individual died'
questions.hint: Find the date directly stated.

questions.q:
Read:
"Tasmania's thylacine — commonly called the Tasmanian Tiger for its striped back — was declared extinct in 1936 when the last known individual died at Hobart Zoo. Despite being a marsupial, it had evolved to resemble a large dog, an example of convergent evolution where unrelated species develop similar features in response to similar environments. Periodic unconfirmed sightings continue to this day, though no credible evidence has emerged. In 2023, Colossal Biosciences announced a project to attempt de-extinction of the thylacine using gene-editing technology."

'Convergent evolution' means:
options: Unrelated species developing similar features independently
questions.exp: 'Unrelated species develop similar features in response to similar environments'
questions.hint: The passage defines it directly.

questions.q:
Read:
"Tasmania's thylacine — commonly called the Tasmanian Tiger for its striped back — was declared extinct in 1936 when the last known individual died at Hobart Zoo. Despite being a marsupial, it had evolved to resemble a large dog, an example of convergent evolution where unrelated species develop similar features in response to similar environments. Periodic unconfirmed sightings continue to this day, though no credible evidence has emerged. In 2023, Colossal Biosciences announced a project to attempt de-extinction of the thylacine using gene-editing technology."

'No credible evidence has emerged' implies the author:
options: Is sceptical that any surviving thylacines exist despite reported sightings
questions.exp: 'Periodic sightings' BUT 'no credible evidence' = author acknowledges sightings but doesn't find them convincing.
questions.hint: Does 'no credible evidence' support or undermine the sightings?

questions.hint: Keep adding digits until you get a single digit.
